T_{final} = 233 \cdot \left(\frac{700}{450}\right)^{\frac{0.405}{1.405}} \approx 233 \cdot 1.205 \approx 281\ \text{K} \approx 8\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

This is comfortably below the 65 °C design limit, leaving the necessary thermal headroom.

### 2.3 End-Use Utilization — Three Engineering Domains

Hydrogen is utilized in three conceptually distinct domains:

- **Domain 1 — Energy:** Direct conversion to electricity, mechanical work, or heat (fuel cells, ICE, turbines).
- **Domain 2 — Chemical Feedstock:** As a reactant in industrial chemistry (ammonia, methanol, refining).
- **Domain 3 — Metallurgical / Reducing Agent:** Reduction of iron ore, semiconductor processing, glass manufacturing.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Where Used | Typical Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower Heating Value of H₂ | $LHV = 120\ \text{MJ/kg}$ | Energy-yield calculations | $33.33\ \text{kWh/kg}$ |
| Higher Heating Value of H₂ | $HHV = 142\ \text{MJ/kg}$ | Combustion with condensate recovery | $39.4\ \text{kWh/kg}$ |
| Specific Energy (volumetric) | $E_v = \rho \cdot LHV$ | Tank sizing | $\text{MJ/L}$ |
| Ideal-gas density | $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ | Gas-state mass balance | $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$ |
| Isentropic compression final T | $T_{2} = T_{1} \left(P_{2}/P_{1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | PCU sizing, SAE J2601 | $\gamma_{H_2} = 1.405$ |
| Compressor work (ideal) | $W_{c} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_{1} \left[\left(P_{2}/P_{1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | Compressor power rating | kW per kg/s |
| Fuel-cell efficiency (PEM) | $\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \approx 0.55$–$0.65$ | Utilization analysis | At LHV basis $\approx 50\%$ |
| Round-trip efficiency (H₂ as carrier) | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{el} \cdot \eta_{comp} \cdot \eta_{FC}$ | Full-chain P2P efficiency | Typical $30$–$40\%$ |
| Mass flow from fill time | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\Delta(PV)/(RT)}{\Delta t}$ | Dispenser rating | kg/s |
| SOC (pressure-based) | $SOC = \dfrac{P_{tank} - P_{min}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}$ | Fill-termination logic | Dimensionless, $0 \le SOC \le 1$ |
| Energy stored in tank | $E = m \cdot LHV = \rho V \cdot LHV$ | Vehicle range estimation | MJ or kWh |
| Equivalent gasoline energy | $E_{eq} = \dfrac{E_{H_2}}{LHV_{gas}}$ | Cross-fuel comparison | $LHV_{gas} \approx 44\ \text{MJ/kg}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use the vertical bar `|` symbol inside markdown tables** — it breaks the table parser. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value / conditional notation. Example: write $P \mid_{final}$ as $P \vert_{final}$.

### 2.5 Why the Compressor Power Rating Matters

A 700-bar HRS serving 50–100 vehicles/day needs to compress anywhere from **200 to 1000 kg of H₂ per day**. With isentropic efficiency of ~70 %, the electrical demand of the compressor train is in the range **2–4 kWh per kg of H₂ delivered**. This is a non-trivial parasitic load — the reason why on-site **renewable-powered electrolysis + buffer storage** is favored over delivered-and-compressed hydrogen in net-zero scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Required to Refill a 700-bar Type-IV Vehicle Tank

**Problem statement:** A passenger FCEV (e.g., Toyota Mirai class) carries a Type-IV tank with water volume $V = 122\ \text{L}$. The tank is filled from $P_1 = 100\ \text{bar}$ (residual) to $P_2 = 700\ \text{bar}$ at a controlled inlet temperature of $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate (a) the mass of H₂ loaded, (b) the energy stored on an LHV basis, and (c) the approximate refill time for a 2 kg/min dispenser.

**Step 1 — Convert water volume to geometric volume.**

$$
V = 122\ \text{L} = 0.122\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal-gas law at the initial (residual) state to find the mass already in the tank.**

The temperature inside the tank at residual state is the equilibrium ambient, take $T_1 = 298\ \text{K}$ (25 °C).

$$
m_1 = \frac{P_1 V M}{R T_1} = \frac{(100 \times 10^5)(0.122)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(8.314)(298)}
$$

Evaluating the numerator:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 100 \times 10^5 \times 0.122 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 2.4595 \times 10^{3}\ \text{Pa·m}^3 \cdot \text{kg/mol}
$$

Evaluating the denominator:

$$
\text{Denominator} = 8.314 \times 298 = 2477.6\ \text{J/mol}
$$

Therefore:

$$
m_1 = \frac{2459.5}{2477.6} \approx 0.993\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ideal-gas law at the final state (filling temperature −40 °C = 233 K).**

$$
m_2 = \frac{P_2 V M}{R T_2} = \frac{(700 \times 10^5)(0.122)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(8.314)(233)}
$$

Numerator:

$$
700 \times 10^5 \times 0.122 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 1.7217 \times 10^{4}
$$

Denominator:

$$
8.314 \times 233 = 1937.2
$$

Therefore:

$$
m_2 = \frac{17216.6}{1937.2} \approx 8.887\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of H₂ loaded in this fill (the deliverable quantity).**

$$
\Delta m = m_2 - m_1 = 8.887 - 0.993 = 7.894\ \text{kg}
$$

Wait — this exceeds the 5–6 kg typical Mirai fill. The reason is that we are treating the *post-fill* state at $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ which is the moment the valve closes. In reality, the tank *settles* to ambient after the fill, and the SOC algorithm stops at **87.5 %** of 700 bar = 612.5 bar. Let us redo with the realistic end-of-fill pressure.

**Step 1′ — Realistic final pressure $P_2' = 612.5\ \text{bar}$ (87.5 % SOC).**

$$
m_2' = \frac{(612.5 \times 10^5)(0.122)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(8.314)(233)}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 612.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.122 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 1.5065 \times 10^4
$$

$$
m_2' = \frac{15065}{1937.2} \approx 7.777\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2′ — Final mass delivered.**

$$
\Delta m = 7.777 - 0.993 = 6.784\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3′ — Energy stored at LHV.**

$$
E = \Delta m \cdot LHV = 6.784 \times 120 = 814.1\ \text{MJ}
$$

Convert to kWh:

$$
E = 814.1 / 3.6 = 226.1\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4′ — Refill time for a 2 kg/min dispenser.**

$$
t = \frac{\Delta m}{\dot{m}} = \frac{6.784}{2.0} = 3.39\ \text{min}
$$

This matches the published 3–5 minute refueling time of the Toyota Mirai / Hyundai NEXO.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compressor Power for a 350-bar HRS

**Problem statement:** A station compresses 200 kg/day of H₂ from 30 bar (delivered via tube-trailer) to 450 bar (high-pressure storage). Inlet temperature is 25 °C. Compressor isentropic efficiency is 75 %. Mechanical efficiency is 95 %. Calculate the required electrical input power during continuous operation.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{200\ \text{kg}}{24 \times 3600\ \text{s}} = 2.315 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Molar flow rate (using $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$).**

$$
\dot{n} = \frac{\dot{m}}{M} = \frac{2.315 \times 10^{-3}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.148\ \text{mol/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Specific isentropic work (per mole).**

$$
w_s = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]
$$

Compute the pressure ratio term:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{450}{30} = 15
$$

$$
(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.405/1.405 = 0.2883
$$

$$
15^{0.2883} = e^{0.2883 \cdot \ln(15)} = e^{0.2883 \times 2.708} = e^{0.7807} = 2.183
$$

$$
15^{0.2883} - 1 = 1.183
$$

$$
\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} = \frac{1.405}{0.405} = 3.469
$$

$$
w_s = 3.469 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 1.183
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
3.469 \times 8.314 = 28.84
$$

$$
28.84 \times 298 = 8594
$$

$$
8594 \times 1.183 = 10167\ \text{J/mol}
$$

So $w_s \approx 10.17\ \text{kJ/mol}$.

**Step 4 — Ideal (isentropic) power.**

$$
\dot{W}_s = \dot{n} \cdot w_s = 1.148 \times 10.17 = 11.68\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual compressor shaft power (with isentropic efficiency 75 %).**

$$
\dot{W}_{shaft} = \frac{\dot{W}_s}{\eta_{isen}} = \frac{11.68}{0.75} = 15.57\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Electrical input (with mechanical efficiency 95 %).**

$$
\dot{W}_{elec} = \frac{\dot{W}_{shaft}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{15.57}{0.95} = 16.39\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Daily energy consumption.**

$$
E_{day} = 16.39 \times 24 = 393.4\ \text{kWh/day}
$$

Specific energy consumption:

$$
e_{spec} = \frac{E_{day}}{m_{day}} = \frac{393.4}{200} = 1.97\ \text{kWh/kg}
$$

This matches the **1.5–2.5 kWh/kg** rule-of-thumb for HRS compression energy — a high-yield KTU number.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Dispensing Protocols

| Protocol | Pressure Class | Pre-cooling | Fill Time Target | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CHIC (constant pressure, no pre-cool) | 350 bar (H35) | None | ~3 min | SAE J2601 (light duty) |
| CHIC with PCU | 700 bar (H70) | $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ mandatory | ~3–5 min | SAE J2601-2 |
| LH₂ dispensing | $\approx 1\ \text{bar (g)}$ + cryopump | Subcooled | ~5 min | SAE J2799 |
| Heavy-duty (truck/bus) | 350–700 bar | $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | 10–15 min | SAE J2601-5 |
| Stationary backup-power swap | up to 500 bar | None | < 5 min | NFPA 2 |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Utilization Sectors

| Sector | Form of H₂ Used | Key Reaction / Device | Typical Purity | Market Status (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Road transport (cars) | Compressed 700 bar | PEM fuel cell | ISO 14687 Grade D | Commercial (Mirai, NEXO) |
| Heavy transport (trucks, buses) | Compressed 350 bar | PEM fuel cell | ISO 14687 Grade D | Early commercial |
| Stationary power | Compressed / pipeline | PEM / SOFC | Industrial grade | Commercial |
| Aviation | LH₂ | Fuel cell / H₂-ICE | Aviation grade | Demonstrator |
| Maritime | LH₂ / NH₃ | Fuel cell / ICE | ISO 14687 | Pilot projects |
| Ammonia synthesis | Pipeline / SMR-derived | Haber-Bosch | Technical grade | Mature (largest consumer) |
| Refinery hydrotreating | Pipeline | Catalytic | Refinery grade | Mature |
| Steel (DRI) | Reformed gas / H₂ | Direct reduction | High purity | Emerging (HYBRIT, H2 Green Steel) |
| Rocket propulsion | LH₂ | Combustion with LOX | Ultra-high | Mature (NASA, ISRO) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a 700-bar Hydrogen Refueling Station (HRS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[On-site Electrolyser PEM or Alkaline] --> bufA[Low-pressure Buffer 30 bar]
    srcB[Tube Trailer Delivery 200 to 500 bar] --> bufB[Medium-pressure Receiver 450 bar]
    srcC[Pipeline Delivery 30 to 80 bar] --> bufA

    bufA --> comp1[Compressor Stage 1 Diaphragm 30 to 150 bar]
    comp1 --> comp2[Compressor Stage 2 Diaphragm 150 to 450 bar]
    comp2 --> bankA[HP Bank A 450 bar]
    bankA --> bankB[HP Bank B 450 bar]
    bankB --> bankC[HP Bank C 450 bar]

    bufB --> bankA
    bankA --> pcu[Pre-cooling Unit Heat Exchanger to minus 40 C]
    pcu --> disp[Dispenser Cabinet Mass Flow Meter Hose Nozzle]

    disp --> vhc1[Passenger FCEV 700 bar]
    disp --> vhc2[Bus or Truck FCEV 350 bar]
    disp --> vhc3[Stationary Fuel Cell Backup Power]

    ctrl[Station Controller PLC with SAE J2601 protocol] -.-> comp1
    ctrl -.-> comp2
    ctrl -.-> pcu
    ctrl -.-> disp
    ctrl -.-> vhc1
    ctrl -.-> vhc2
    ctrl -.-> vhc3

    sfty[Fire and Gas Detection H2 Sensors Flame Detectors Emergency Shutoff] -.-> ctrl
```

### 4.2 Dispensing Sequence Flowchart (Communication Handshake + Fill)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Arrives at HRS] --> B[Coupler Engaged IR and Power Pins Mate]
    B --> C{Station Initiates Handshake via SAE J2799}
    C --> D[Vehicle Transmits Tank Parameters Pressure Temp Volume SOC]
    D --> E[Station Controller Computes End Pressure and Ramp Profile]
    E --> F[Station Opens High-pressure Valve to Hose]
    F --> G[Pre-cooled H2 Flows into Vehicle Tank 40 C]
    G --> H{Monitor SOC in Real Time}
    H --> I[SOC Reaches 87.5 percent]
    I --> J[Close High-pressure Valve]
    J --> K[Bleed Hose to Near-zero Pressure]
    K --> L[Display Transaction Cost kg Delivered]
    L --> M[Driver Disengages Nozzle]
    M --> N[End of Fill]

    H -->|Abnormal: Temp Exceeds 65 C| O[Abort Fill Trigger Emergency Vent]
    H -->|Abnormal: Leak Detected| O
    O --> P[Station Logs Incident and Locks Out]
```

### 4.3 Hydrogen Utilization Tree (End-Use Domains)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H2[Hydrogen at Dispenser Outlet]
    H2 --> D1[Domain 1 Energy]
    H2 --> D2[Domain 2 Chemical Feedstock]
    H2 --> D3[Domain 3 Metallurgical]

    D1 --> D1a[PEM Fuel Cell FCEV Cars Buses Trucks]
    D1 --> D1b[SOFC Stationary Power Generation]
    D1 --> D1c[Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engine H2 ICE]
    D1 --> D1d[Gas Turbine Hydrogen or Hydrogen Natural Gas Blend]
    D1 --> D1e[Aviation Fuel Cell or LH2 Turbofan]
    D1 --> D1f[Rocket Propulsion LH2 plus LOX]

    D2 --> D2a[Ammonia Synthesis Haber Bosch]
    D2 --> D2b[Methanol Synthesis]
    D2 --> D2c[Refinery Hydrotreating and Hydrocracking]
    D2 --> D2d[Synthetic Liquid Fuels via Fischer Tropsch]

    D3 --> D3a[Direct Reduced Iron DRI Steel]
    D3 --> D3b[Glass Manufacturing]
    D3 --> D3c[Semiconductor Fabrication]
    D3 --> D3d[Heat Treatment Annealing and Sintering]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use purely **alphanumeric node identifiers** (e.g., `srcA`, `comp1`, `vhc1`) and **plain uppercase text labels** inside the double-quoted strings. No reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names, ensuring compilation safety.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. List and briefly explain any three codes/standards that govern hydrogen dispensing.**
> *(Module 1 — CO 1, K3 — Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **SAE J2601** — Defines fueling protocols for light-duty gaseous hydrogen vehicles. Specifies pressure classes (H35 at 350 bar and H70 at 700 bar), pre-cooling temperatures (typically $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for H70), and the CHIC (constant hydrogen inlet condition) algorithm for safe, repeatable fills.

2. **ISO 17268** — Specifies the geometry and performance of the *receptacle and nozzle coupling* used to transfer gaseous hydrogen to vehicles. Defines leak rates, durability cycles, and the IR communication pin-out for the data handshake.

3. **NFPA 2** — The *Hydrogen Technologies Code* published by the National Fire Protection Association. Covers all aspects of hydrogen infrastructure, including station layout, separation distances, ventilation, and emergency shutdown.

4. *(Bonus, optional)* **ISO 14687:2019** — Specifies the hydrogen fuel quality grades (Grade D for PEMFC road vehicles, requiring ≥ 99.97 % purity with strict limits on CO, S, NH₃, etc.).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. Define the SOC (State-of-Charge) of a hydrogen tank and explain why the fill is terminated at 87.5 % rather than 100 % of the nominal pressure.**
> *(Module 1 — CO 1, K2 — Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

The **State-Of-Charge (SOC)** of a hydrogen tank is defined as the *dimensionless ratio* of the *available hydrogen mass* to the *maximum storable mass*:

$$
SOC = \frac{P_{tank} - P_{min}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}
$$

where $P_{min}$ is the residual pressure (tank empty, ~10–100 bar) and $P_{max}$ is the nominal maximum working pressure (e.g., 700 bar).

**Why stop at 87.5 %?**

1. **Thermal headroom:** Rapid filling (in 3–5 min) heats the gas inside the tank due to adiabatic compression and the Joule–Thomson-like heating during throttling. The actual pressure can climb *above* the value at the moment of valve closure as the gas cools back to ambient (this is called **pressure creep**). Stopping at 87.5 % ensures that even with thermal creep, the pressure never exceeds the design limit of 700 bar × 1.25 (the 1.25 safety factor per **EC 79**).
2. **Standardization:** SAE J2601 publishes a *lookup table* of "end-of-fill pressure" values for various ambient conditions. The 87.5 % number corresponds to the standard **CHIC** end-of-fill temperature profile at $T_{ambient} = 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_{inlet} = -40\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
3. **Cycle life:** Avoiding constant excursions to 100 % rated pressure extends the composite overwrap fatigue life.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Question]**
> **Q3. (a)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the major sub-systems of a 700-bar hydrogen refueling station (HRS). Explain the role of the pre-cooling unit and the IR-communication protocol. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A passenger FCEV has a Type-IV tank with water volume **120 L**. The tank is filled from a residual pressure of **100 bar** to an end-of-fill pressure of **612.5 bar** at an inlet temperature of **−40 °C**. Calculate the mass of hydrogen loaded and the energy stored on an LHV basis. Assume ideal-gas behaviour with $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$ and $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol·K}$. **(7 marks)**
> *(Module 1 — CO 1, K3 / K4 — Apply / Analyze)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

The major sub-systems of a 700-bar HRS (as illustrated in Section 4.1) are:

1. **Hydrogen supply** — either on-site (electrolyser) or delivered (tube-trailer, LH₂ tanker, or pipeline). (1 mark)
2. **Compression train** — multi-stage diaphragm or piston compressor, taking inlet gas from delivery pressure to **~450 bar** station storage. (1 mark)
3. **High-pressure bulk storage** — cascade of three banks (low / medium / high) at ~450 bar, type-IV composite cylinders, to allow differential pressure filling. (1 mark)
4. **Pre-cooling unit (PCU)** — a refrigeration-based heat exchanger that chills H₂ to **−40 °C** before it enters the dispenser hose. It is **mandatory** at 700 bar to keep the final tank temperature below the design limit of 65 °C (per SAE J2601). (2 marks)
5. **Dispenser** — houses the hose, the IR nozzle, the mass flow meter, and the HMI screen. Performs the SOC algorithm and terminates the fill. (1 mark)
6. **Safety & control** — H₂ sensors, flame detectors, emergency shut-off (ESO), vent stack, and the PLC running the SAE J2601 state machine. (1 mark)

**Role of IR-communication protocol:**
The nozzle and the vehicle receptacle exchange digital data over an **infrared (IR) link** (per ISO 17268). The vehicle transmits: (i) tank pressure, (ii) tank temperature, (iii) tank water volume, and (iv) current SOC. The station controller uses these to compute the *end-of-fill pressure* and to modulate the fill rate. This handshake is mandatory for the **CHIC** algorithm and prevents over-pressurization. *(No marks reserved separately, included within sub-system description.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $V = 120\ \text{L} = 0.120\ \text{m}^3$, $P_1 = 100\ \text{bar} = 100 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$, $P_2 = 612.5\ \text{bar} = 612.5 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$, $T_1 = 298\ \text{K}$ (residual state at ambient), $T_2 = 233\ \text{K}$ (fill temperature, $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$).

**Step 1 — Mass at residual state $m_1$.** (2 marks)

$$
m_1 = \frac{P_1 V M}{R T_1} = \frac{(100 \times 10^5)(0.120)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(8.314)(298)}
$$

$$
m_1 = \frac{2419.2}{2477.6} = 0.9765\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass at end-of-fill state $m_2$.** (2 marks)

$$
m_2 = \frac{P_2 V M}{R T_2} = \frac{(612.5 \times 10^5)(0.120)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(8.314)(233)}
$$

$$
m_2 = \frac{14815.8}{1937.2} = 7.648\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass delivered $\Delta m$.** (1 mark)

$$
\Delta m = m_2 - m_1 = 7.648 - 0.9765 = 6.672\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy stored on LHV basis.** (2 marks)

$$
E = \Delta m \times LHV = 6.672 \times 120 = 800.6\ \text{MJ}
$$

$$
E = 800.6 / 3.6 = 222.4\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Final answer:** Approximately **6.67 kg of H₂** is loaded, storing **800.6 MJ (≈ 222.4 kWh)** of chemical energy.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question — Internal-Choice Alternative]**
> **Q4. (a)** Classify the major end-use sectors of hydrogen and give one example reaction/device for each sector. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 350-bar HRS must compress 150 kg/day of H₂ from 25 bar to 420 bar at 25 °C. The isentropic efficiency is 72 % and the mechanical efficiency is 96 %. Calculate the **electrical input power (kW)** during continuous operation, and the **specific compression energy (kWh per kg)**. **(7 marks)**
> *(Module 1 — CO 1, K3 / K4 — Apply / Analyze)*

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks**

The three end-use domains of hydrogen are:

1. **Energy domain** — direct conversion to useful work. Example: **PEM fuel cell** in a passenger FCEV. The electrochemical reaction is $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{electricity} + \text{heat}$. The cell efficiency is ~50–60 % on an LHV basis. *(2 marks)*
2. **Chemical feedstock domain** — H₂ as a reactant. Example: **Haber-Bosch ammonia synthesis**: $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightleftharpoons 2NH_3$ over an iron catalyst at 400–500 °C and 150–300 bar. This is the single largest consumer of H₂ globally (~70 % of dedicated production). *(2 marks)*
3. **Metallurgical / reducing agent domain** — H₂ as a reductant. Example: **Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) steel-making**: $Fe_2O_3 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2Fe + 3H_2O$, replacing coke from coal and eliminating process-CO₂. Pilots such as HYBRIT (Sweden) and H2 Green Steel are scaling this. *(2 marks)*

**Additional sub-mark for any one numerical example or pilot project name (e.g., HYBRIT, Mirai, NEXO, IOCL R&D Gujarat).** *(1 mark)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $\dot{m}_{day} = 150\ \text{kg/day}$, $P_1 = 25\ \text{bar}$, $P_2 = 420\ \text{bar}$, $T_1 = 298\ \text{K}$, $\eta_{isen} = 0.72$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.96$, $\gamma = 1.405$, $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$.** (1 mark)

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{150}{24 \times 3600} = 1.736 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Molar flow rate $\dot{n}$.** (1 mark)

$$
\dot{n} = \frac{1.736 \times 10^{-3}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.861\ \text{mol/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Specific isentropic work $w_s$.** (2 marks)

$$
w_s = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]
$$

Compute the pressure ratio:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{420}{25} = 16.8
$$

Exponent:

$$
(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.2883
$$

$$
16.8^{0.2883} = e^{0.2883 \cdot \ln(16.8)} = e^{0.2883 \times 2.821} = e^{0.8133} = 2.255
$$

$$
16.8^{0.2883} - 1 = 1.255
$$

$$
\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} = 3.469
$$

$$
w_s = 3.469 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 1.255
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
3.469 \times 8.314 = 28.84
$$

$$
28.84 \times 298 = 8594
$$

$$
8594 \times 1.255 = 10785\ \text{J/mol} \approx 10.79\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Ideal (isentropic) power $\dot{W}_s$.** (1 mark)

$$
\dot{W}_s = \dot{n} \cdot w_s = 0.861 \times 10.79 = 9.29\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Electrical input power $\dot{W}_{elec}$.** (1 mark)

$$
\dot{W}_{elec} = \frac{\dot{W}_s}{\eta_{isen} \cdot \eta_{mech}} = \frac{9.29}{0.72 \times 0.96} = \frac{9.29}{0.6912} = 13.44\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Specific compression energy.** (1 mark)

$$
e_{spec} = \frac{\dot{W}_{elec}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{13.44}{1.736 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.74\ \text{kJ/s per kg/s} = 7.74\ \text{kWh/kg per 1000}
$$

$$
e_{spec} = 7.74 / 3.6 = 2.15\ \text{kWh/kg}
$$

**Final answer:** $\dot{W}_{elec} \approx 13.44\ \text{kW}$ and $e_{spec} \approx 2.15\ \text{kWh/kg}$, which lies within the textbook range of **1.5–2.5 kWh/kg** for HRS compression.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units trap:** Always convert bar → Pa (multiply by $10^5$) and litres → m³ (divide by 1000) before substituting into the ideal-gas law. Students who leave units as bar·L lose 1–2 marks silently.
> 2. **Temperature trap:** At *residual* state the gas is at *ambient* temperature (≈ 298 K), but at *end-of-fill* state the gas is at the *fill temperature* ($-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ = 233 K). Using the same temperature for both is a common error costing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Efficiency stacking:** In compression problems the *isentropic efficiency* and *mechanical efficiency* **multiply** in the denominator of the electrical-power formula. Confusing them as "efficiencies to be added" is a recurring mistake.
> 4. **SOC misinterpretation:** Some students assume "100 % SOC = 700 bar exactly." The correct end-of-fill pressure is **87.5 % × 700 = 612.5 bar**, per SAE J2601, due to thermal creep. Failing to apply this gives a wrong $\Delta m$ and wrong energy.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the "Dispensing and Utilization" Module (Module 1):**

- **Dispensing** is the controlled transfer of H₂ from a station to an onboard storage, governed by **SAE J2601**, **ISO 17268**, **NFPA 2**, and **ISO 14687**.
- **Two standard pressure classes:** H35 (350 bar) for buses/trucks/back-up; H70 (700 bar) for passenger FCEVs.
- **Pre-cooling to $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is mandatory** at 700 bar to stay below the $65\ ^\circ\text{C}$ composite-tank design limit.
- **End-of-fill SOC = 87.5 %** to leave thermal-creep headroom; **thermal creep** is the pressure rise after valve closure as gas cools.
- **IR-communication handshake** between nozzle and vehicle transmits $P$, $T$, $V$, and current SOC.
- **Specific compression energy for HRS:** **1.5–2.5 kWh per kg of H₂** — a key KTU number.
- **Hydrogen LHV = 120 MJ/kg** (≈ 33.33 kWh/kg); HHV = 142 MJ/kg (≈ 39.4 kWh/kg).
- **Hydrogen's MIE = 0.017 mJ** — about 10× lower than gasoline; this is the *why* behind the strict leak-tight couplings.
- **Utilization domains:** (1) Energy (PEMFC, SOFC, H₂-ICE, turbines, aviation, rockets), (2) Chemical feedstock (NH₃, methanol, refining, e-fuels), (3) Metallurgical (DRI steel, glass, semiconductors, heat treatment).
- **Largest current consumer of dedicated H₂:** Ammonia (Haber-Bosch) — ~70 % of dedicated production.
- **Quality standard for PEM-FCEVs:** **ISO 14687 Grade D** — ≥ 99.97 % purity, with ppb-level limits on CO, H₂S, NH₃, and halides.
- **Round-trip efficiency (P2P)** of a hydrogen pathway: ~30–40 %, much lower than direct electrification (~80–90 %); this drives the "**hydrogen last**" application prioritization.
- **Type-IV tanks** (polymer liner + carbon-fiber overwrap) are the standard for 700-bar vehicle storage, replacing Type-I/II/III metal tanks.
- **Cryogenic LH₂** dispensing is the alternative to compressed-gas, used for trucks, ships, and aviation, with sub-cooled liquid at ~**20 K**.
- **Safety triad at HRS:** (i) leak-tight IR nozzle, (ii) flame + H₂ gas detection, (iii) emergency shut-off + vent stack — required by NFPA 2.
- **KTU formula quickies:** $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$, $m = PVM/(RT)$, $E = m \cdot LHV$, $SOC = (P-P_{min})/(P_{max}-P_{min})$.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1: Dispensing and Utilization**
*All formulas, constants, and standards referenced are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OECHT616 and current (2024) industry practice as published by SAE, ISO, and NFPA.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Production Plants
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Production Plants

# Hydrogen Production Plants

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Production Plant (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *hydrogen production plant* is an integrated industrial system comprising feed-handling units, primary conversion reactors (chemical, electrochemical, thermochemical, or biological), separation and purification modules, compression/storage sub-systems, balance-of-plant (BOP) utilities, and process control instrumentation, designed to convert a hydrogen-bearing feedstock into high-purity molecular hydrogen (H₂) at a defined production rate (Nm³/hr or kg/day) and a target purity level conforming to ISO 14687 / SAE J2719 standards.

The plant is engineered to operate at a specific **production capacity (Cₚ)** measured in **kg/day** or **tonnes/year**, with an associated **energy efficiency (η_plant)** defined as the ratio of the higher heating value (HHV) of the hydrogen produced to the total primary energy input.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Hydrogen Factory"**
> Think of a hydrogen production plant like a **water purification plant**, but reversed. Just as a water plant takes dirty river water and outputs clean drinking water through filters and chemical treatments, a hydrogen plant takes hydrogen-rich "raw material" (natural gas, water, biomass) and outputs clean H₂ gas through reactors and purifiers. The "dirt" being filtered out are the unwanted atoms — carbon (becoming CO₂), sulfur, oxygen, and other trace gases.

Imagine a **tea-making setup**:
- **Feedstock** = the tea leaves + water (raw material)
- **Reformer/Electrolyzer** = the kettle (extraction device)
- **Purifier (PSA / membrane)** = the strainer (removing impurities)
- **Compressor** = the pump (pressurizing the output)
- **Storage tank** = the thermos (holding the product)

The "plant" is simply all of these integrated into one continuous industrial operation with proper safety, instrumentation, and utilities.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Production Capacity (Cₚ):** 100 – 1,00,000 kg/day (utility) ; > 1,00,000 kg/day (merchant)
> - **Purity Grade:** 99.99% (ISO 14687 Grade D) for PEM fuel cells ; 99.999% (Grade E) for specialty electronics
> - **Energy Efficiency (η):** 60 – 75% (SMR) ; 70 – 85% (PEM electrolysis, LHV basis)
> - **Hydrogen Cost (LCOH):** **USD 1 – 8/kg** depending on feedstock and scale
> - **Carbon Intensity:** 0 – 10 kg CO₂/kg H₂ (green) ; > 9 kg CO₂/kg H₂ (grey)

### 1.4 Classification of Hydrogen Production Plants

| Plant Type | Feedstock | Color Code | CO₂ Emissions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** | Natural gas (CH₄) | Grey/Brown | High (9–12 kg/kg) |
| **SMR + Carbon Capture (CCS)** | Natural gas + CCS | Blue | Low (1–3 kg/kg) |
| **Electrolysis (PEM/Alkaline/SOEC)** | Water (H₂O) + Renewable electricity | Green | Near zero |
| **Coal Gasification** | Coal | Brown/Black | Very high |
| **Biomass Gasification** | Biomass | Green (negative possible) | Low / Negative |
| **Photoelectrochemical / Photobiological** | Water + Sunlight | Green/Yellow | Zero |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Module 1 emphasizes the three major industrial routes — **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)**, **Electrolysis**, and **Coal/Biomass Gasification** — along with their plant-level engineering architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Hydrogen Production Plant

A typical hydrogen production plant consists of **seven functional blocks**, regardless of the production route:

1. **Feedstock Pre-treatment Unit** — Desulfurization, water demineralization, natural gas compression
2. **Primary Conversion Reactor** — Reformer, electrolyzer stack, or gasifier
3. **Heat Recovery / Steam Generation** — Waste heat boiler, superheater
4. **Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reactor** — Converts CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (SMR/gasification route only)
5. **Hydrogen Purification System** — Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA), membranes, or cryogenic separation
6. **Compression & Storage Section** — Multi-stage compressor, buffer tanks
7. **Balance of Plant (BOP)** — Cooling water, instrument air, flares, DCS/PLC control

### 2.2 Operating Logic — Step-by-Step

**For a Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) Plant:**

- **Step 1:** Natural gas (methane) is desulfurized to < 0.1 ppm H₂S to protect the nickel catalyst.
- **Step 2:** Methane is mixed with **high-pressure steam** (steam-to-carbon ratio S:C ≈ 2.5–3.5) at ~ 30 bar.
- **Step 3:** The mixture enters the **primary reformer** at **800–850 °C** over a nickel-on-alumina catalyst:

  CH₄ + H₂O ⇌ CO + 3H₂   ΔH = +206 kJ/mol

- **Step 4:** Reformed gas passes to a **High-Temperature Shift (HTS)** reactor at 350–400 °C, then a **Low-Temperature Shift (LTS)** at 190–250 °C, both using iron-chrome and copper-zinc catalysts respectively:

  CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂    ΔH = -41 kJ/mol

- **Step 5:** The CO₂ is removed in an **absorber** (e.g., MEA/DEA amine wash or Benfield process).
- **Step 6:** The crude H₂ (95–98% purity) goes to a **PSA unit** to reach 99.99%+ purity.
- **Step 7:** Off-gas from PSA is recycled to the reformer as fuel, raising overall efficiency.

**For a PEM Water Electrolyzer Plant:**

- **Step 1:** Deionized water (conductivity < 1 μS/cm) is fed to the anode side.
- **Step 2:** DC electricity (typically 1.8–2.5 V per cell) splits water:

  H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂

- **Step 3:** H₂ passes through a gas–liquid separator and a **drying column** (silica gel / molecular sieve).
- **Step 4:** O₂ is vented or captured.
- **Step 5:** Dry H₂ is compressed (20–80 bar) and stored in buffer tanks.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Memorize the formula, its boundary conditions, and the units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{HHV} = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \cdot HHV_{H_2}}{E_{in}^{thermal} + E_{in}^{elec}}$ | Plant thermal efficiency (HHV basis) | dimensionless | 0 < η < 1 |
| 2 | $\eta_{LHV} = \dfrac{m_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}{E_{in}^{thermal} + E_{in}^{elec}}$ | Plant thermal efficiency (LHV basis) | dimensionless | Used for SMR plants |
| 3 | $C_{p} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{t_{op}}$ | Plant production capacity | kg/day or tonnes/year | Typical 100 – 1,00,000 kg/day |
| 4 | $\text{S:C} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2O}}{\dot{n}_{C,\text{in}}}$ | Steam-to-carbon ratio (SMR) | mol/mol | Optimal: **2.5 – 3.5** |
| 5 | $P_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Power per electrolyzer cell | W | 1.8 V < V_cell < 2.5 V |
| 6 | $\text{Faradaic Eff. } \eta_F = \dfrac{nF \cdot m_{H_2}}{M_{H_2} \cdot I \cdot t}$ | Electrolyzer Faraday efficiency | % | 95 – 99.9% for PEM |
| 7 | $\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot R \cdot T}{P}$ | Volumetric H₂ flow | Nm³/hr | At STP (273.15 K, 1 atm) |
| 8 | $\text{LCOH} = \dfrac{CRF \cdot C_{capex} + C_{opex}}{m_{H_2,\text{lifetime}}}$ | Levelized Cost of Hydrogen | USD/kg | CRF = capital recovery factor |
| 9 | $CRF = \dfrac{r(1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor | dimensionless | r = discount rate, n = years |
| 10 | $X_{CO} = \dfrac{F_{CO,in} - F_{CO,out}}{F_{CO,in}}$ | CO conversion in WGS | dimensionless | 0.9 – 0.99 (HTS+LTS) |
| 11 | $K_{eq}(T) = \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}\right)$ | Equilibrium constant (WGS) | — | Temperature dependent |
| 12 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{H_2,\text{annual}}}{8760 \cdot P_{rated}}$ | Capacity factor of electrolyzer | % | 40 – 90% (renewable) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 Tip:** When solving numerical problems, always state the **basis** of efficiency (LHV vs HHV) and the **standard conditions** (STP vs NTP). The HHV of H₂ = **142 MJ/kg**, LHV = **120 MJ/kg**.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does a KTU engineer need to know this?**
> - **Refinery design:** Hydrogen plants supply 30–40% of refinery H₂ demand for hydrodesulfurization (HDS) and hydrocracking.
> - **Ammonia & Methanol:** Green ammonia plants integrate large-scale electrolysis (3–5 GW) to produce H₂ for Haber-Bosch synthesis.
> - **Steel industry:** Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) using green H₂ is the most disruptive decarbonization pathway for steelmaking.
> - **Mobility:** Fuel-cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) require ISO 14687 Grade D H₂ (99.99% purity) — achievable only via PSA or membrane purification.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Hydrogen Yield from SMR

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 typical):**
A steam methane reforming plant processes 1000 Nm³/hr of pure methane (CH₄) at STP. Assuming 100% conversion in the reformer and complete CO shift, calculate:
(a) The volumetric flow rate of H₂ produced.
(b) The mass of H₂ produced per day.
(c) The required steam flow rate at S:C = 3.0.

**Given:**
- Feed: $\dot{V}_{CH_4} = 1000 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$
- $T_{STP} = 273.15 \; \text{K}$, $P_{STP} = 1 \; \text{atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa}$
- $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \; \text{g/mol}$, $M_{CH_4} = 16.04 \; \text{g/mol}$, $M_{H_2O} = 18.02 \; \text{g/mol}$

**Reaction Set:**
- Primary Reformer: $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$
- Water-Gas Shift: $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$

**Net reaction (SMR + WGS, 100% CO shift):**
$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$$

---

**Step 1 — Molar flow rate of CH₄**

Use ideal gas law: $PV = nRT \Rightarrow n = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$.

$$
\dot{n}_{CH_4} = \dfrac{P \cdot \dot{V}_{CH_4}}{R \cdot T_{STP}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\dot{n}_{CH_4} = \dfrac{(1.01325 \times 10^5 \; \text{Pa}) \cdot (1000/3600 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s})}{8.314 \cdot 273.15}
$$

**Text row:** Convert Nm³/hr → m³/s by dividing by 3600, then apply ideal gas law.

$$
\dot{n}_{CH_4} = \dfrac{1.01325 \times 10^5 \times 0.27778}{2270.97} = \dfrac{28148.6}{2270.97} = 12.395 \; \text{mol/s}
$$

Convert to kmol/hr (multiply by 3.6):

$$
\dot{n}_{CH_4} = 12.395 \times 3.6 = 44.62 \; \text{kmol/hr}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric H₂ produced**

From the net reaction: **1 mol CH₄ → 4 mol H₂**

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2} = 4 \times \dot{n}_{CH_4} = 4 \times 44.62 = 178.5 \; \text{kmol/hr}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate of H₂ at STP**

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot R \cdot T_{STP}}{P}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{178.5 \times 10^3 \times 8.314 \times 273.15}{1.01325 \times 10^5}
$$

**Text row:** Note 178.5 kmol/hr = 178.5 × 10³ mol/hr. Convert hr → s by dividing by 3600 if needed; here we report Nm³/hr directly.

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{4.054 \times 10^{10}}{1.01325 \times 10^5} = 4001.5 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Result (a):** $\dot{V}_{H_2} \approx 4000 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$

---

**Step 4 — Mass of H₂ per day**

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot M_{H_2} = 178.5 \times 2.016 = 359.86 \; \text{kg/hr}
$$

$$
m_{H_2,\text{day}} = 359.86 \times 24 = 8636.6 \; \text{kg/day} \approx 8.64 \; \text{tonnes/day}
$$

**Result (b):** $\approx 8.64 \; \text{tonnes/day}$

---

**Step 5 — Required steam flow rate at S:C = 3.0**

Definition: $\text{S:C} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2O}}{\dot{n}_{CH_4,\text{carbon}}}$

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2O} = 3.0 \times \dot{n}_{CH_4} = 3.0 \times 44.62 = 133.86 \; \text{kmol/hr}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2O} = 133.86 \times 18.02 = 2412 \; \text{kg/hr} \approx 2.41 \; \text{tonnes/hr}
$$

**Result (c):** Steam flow $\approx 2.41 \; \text{tonnes/hr}$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Efficiency of an Electrolyzer Plant

**Problem Statement:**
A 5 MW PEM electrolyzer operates at a cell voltage of 2.0 V and a current density of 1.5 A/cm², with an active area of 1000 cm² per cell and 200 cells in the stack. The Faradaic efficiency is 98%. Calculate:
(a) Total H₂ production rate (kg/day)
(b) Specific energy consumption (kWh/kg H₂)
(c) Stack energy efficiency (LHV basis)

**Given:**
- $V_{cell} = 2.0 \; \text{V}$, $j = 1.5 \; \text{A/cm}^2$, $A_{cell} = 1000 \; \text{cm}^2$
- $N_{cells} = 200$, $\eta_F = 0.98$
- $F = 96485 \; \text{C/mol}$ (Faraday's constant)
- $LHV_{H_2} = 33.33 \; \text{kWh/kg}$ (lower heating value)

---

**Step 1 — Current per cell and total stack current**

$$
I_{cell} = j \cdot A_{cell} = 1.5 \times 1000 = 1500 \; \text{A}
$$

$$
I_{stack} = N_{cells} \cdot I_{cell} \quad \text{(cells in series carry same current)}
$$

**Text row:** In a series-connected electrolyzer stack, current is identical through every cell. Voltage adds up.

$$
I_{stack} = 1500 \; \text{A} \quad (\text{no multiplication for series current})
$$

---

**Step 2 — Total stack voltage**

$$
V_{stack} = V_{cell} \cdot N_{cells} = 2.0 \times 200 = 400 \; \text{V}
$$

---

**Step 3 — H₂ production rate (Faraday's law)**

Faraday's law: $n_{H_2} = \dfrac{\eta_F \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$, where $n = 2$ electrons per H₂.

**Text row:** Reaction is $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$; so 2 electrons produce 1 mol H₂.

Molar flow rate of H₂:

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{\eta_F \cdot I}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{0.98 \times 1500}{2 \times 96485}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{1470}{192970} = 7.617 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mol/s}
$$

Mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot M_{H_2} = 7.617 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 1.535 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Result (a):** Per day:

$$
m_{H_2,\text{day}} = 1.535 \times 10^{-5} \times 86400 = 1.327 \; \text{kg/day}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**

$$
SEC = \dfrac{V_{stack} \cdot I_{stack}}{\dot{m}_{H_2}} = \dfrac{400 \times 1500}{1.535 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
SEC = \dfrac{600000}{1.535 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.909 \times 10^{10} \; \text{J/kg} = \dfrac{3.909 \times 10^{10}}{3.6 \times 10^6} \; \text{kWh/kg}
$$

**Result (b):** $SEC \approx 54.3 \; \text{kWh/kg}$ (typical for PEM)

---

**Step 5 — Stack energy efficiency (LHV)**

$$
\eta_{stack} = \dfrac{LHV_{H_2}}{SEC} = \dfrac{33.33}{54.3} = 0.614 = 61.4\%
$$

**Result (c):** $\eta_{stack} \approx 61.4\%$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | SMR Plant | PEM Electrolysis | Coal Gasification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedstock | CH₄ + H₂O | H₂O + Renewable e⁻ | Coal + H₂O + O₂ |
| Operating T (°C) | 800 – 850 | 50 – 80 | 900 – 1500 |
| Operating P (bar) | 20 – 35 | 20 – 80 | 30 – 70 |
| H₂ Purity (after PSA) | 99.99% | 99.999% | 99.9% |
| Efficiency (LHV) | 65 – 75% | 60 – 70% | 50 – 60% |
| CO₂ emissions (kg/kg H₂) | 9 – 12 | < 1 (with renewable) | 18 – 25 |
| LCOH (USD/kg) | 1.0 – 2.5 | 3.0 – 8.0 | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| Carbon footprint | Grey | Green | Black/Brown |
| Plant scale (kg/day) | 10⁴ – 10⁶ | 10² – 10⁵ | 10⁴ – 10⁶ |

### 4.4 Schematic Block — Mass & Energy Flow in a Generic Hydrogen Plant

| Block | Input Streams | Output Streams | Energy Carrier | Key Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feed Handling** | Raw feedstock | Purified feed | — | Filters, compressors, desulfurizer |
| **Conversion** | Purified feed | Syngas / H₂-rich gas | Heat + Electricity | Reformer / Electrolyzer / Gasifier |
| **Shift / Conditioning** | Syngas | H₂-enriched gas | Heat | WGS reactors, coolers |
| **Purification** | H₂-enriched gas | Pure H₂ (99.99%) | Electricity | PSA / Membrane / Cryogenic |
| **Compression** | Pure H₂ (low P) | Pure H₂ (high P) | Electricity | Multi-stage compressor |
| **Storage** | High-P H₂ | Pressurized H₂ | — | Buffer tanks, underground caverns |
| **BOP** | Utilities (water, air) | Steam, cooling | Heat | Boilers, chillers, cooling towers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Visual Tip:** When drawing a plant in exam, always show **at least 5 labeled blocks**, indicate **input/output streams with arrows**, and mark **key operating conditions (T, P)** on the conversion reactor.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define a "hydrogen production plant" and list any four key performance indicators used to evaluate its performance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hydrogen production plant** is an integrated industrial system that converts a hydrogen-bearing feedstock (e.g., natural gas, water, biomass, coal) into high-purity molecular hydrogen (H₂) at a defined production rate, purity, and energy efficiency, complying with ISO 14687 standards. (2 marks)

**Four Key Performance Indicators (any four, ½ mark each):**
1. **Production capacity (Cₚ)** in kg/day or tonnes/year
2. **Energy efficiency (η)** — LHV or HHV basis
3. **Hydrogen purity (%)** — 99.99% to 99.999%
4. **Specific energy consumption (SEC)** — kWh/kg H₂
5. **Levelized cost of hydrogen (LCOH)** — USD/kg
6. **CO₂ footprint** — kg CO₂/kg H₂

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Distinguish between "grey hydrogen" and "green hydrogen" with suitable examples of production plants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Grey Hydrogen | Green Hydrogen |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | H₂ produced from fossil fuels without carbon capture | H₂ produced by splitting water using renewable electricity |
| **Production plant** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) plant | PEM / Alkaline / SOEC electrolyzer plant |
| **Feedstock** | Natural gas (CH₄) | Water + Solar/Wind electricity |
| **CO₂ emissions** | 9 – 12 kg CO₂/kg H₂ | < 1 kg CO₂/kg H₂ (near zero) |
| **Example** | SMR plant in Kerala Refinery (Kochi) | Cochin International Airport solar-electrolyzer pilot |
| **Color of plant** | Grey/Brown | Green |

(3 marks — full comparison table)
