# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical Decomposition Voltage of Water (KTU Hot Topic)

We begin with the **thermodynamic** energy balance. The minimum *electrical* work required to split water equals the **Gibbs free energy change (ΔG°)** of the reaction at standard state (298.15 K, 1 bar).

**Step 1 — The net electrolysis reaction:**
$$
\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \longrightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_{2(g)}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard-state thermodynamic data (from NIST):**
- $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$  (positive ⇒ non-spontaneous — needs electrical energy)
- $\Delta H^\circ = +285.8 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$  (the HHV of H₂)
- $\Delta S^\circ = +163.1 \,\text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$

**Step 3 — Relate Gibbs free energy to reversible electrical work.**
The maximum reversible electrical work obtainable from a galvanic cell is $W_{elec} = -\Delta G = n F E_{cell}$. For the *reverse* (electrolytic) direction, the minimum voltage required is therefore:
$$
E^\circ_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{n\,F}
$$
where **n = 2** is the number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂ produced (2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂).

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$
E^\circ_{th} = \dfrac{237{,}200 \,\text{J mol}^{-1}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \,\text{C mol}^{-1}}
$$

$$
E^\circ_{th} = \dfrac{237{,}200}{192{,}970} \;\text{V} = 1.229 \,\text{V}
$$

**Step 5 — The thermoneutral voltage (E°tn).**
If the cell is operated *isothermally* (no heat exchange with surroundings), the entire reaction enthalpy must be supplied as electrical energy:
$$
E^\circ_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{n\,F} = \dfrac{285{,}800}{192{,}970} = 1.481 \,\text{V}
$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation.**
- If $V_{cell} > E^\circ_{tn}$ (1.481 V), the **excess** electrical energy is dissipated as heat — the cell *overheats*.
- If $V_{cell} = E^\circ_{tn}$, the reaction proceeds at 100% electrical energy efficiency (no heat loss).
- If $V_{cell} = E^\circ_{th}$ (1.229 V), the cell operates reversibly; the *missing* energy ($E^\circ_{tn} - E^\circ_{th} = 0.252$ V × 2F = 48.6 kJ/mol) is absorbed as **latent heat from the surroundings** — this is the *minimum theoretical* scenario.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** A KTU problem will typically ask *"What is the minimum voltage required to split water at 25 °C and 1 atm?"* The answer is **1.229 V** (using ΔG°), *not* 1.481 V. Using the wrong one loses 2 marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis

### First Law
**Statement:** The mass (m) of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge (Q) passed through the electrolyte.

**Derivation:**
By definition, **1 Faraday (F)** of charge liberates **1 mole of electrons**. To liberate 1 mole of a substance with valency *n* (electrons transferred per ion), we need *n* Faradays of charge. The mass of 1 mole of substance is its molar mass *M*. Therefore, the proportionality constant is **M/(nF)**:

$$
m \; = \; \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \; = \; \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of substance liberated (grams)
- $Q = I \cdot t$ = total charge in Coulombs
- $I$ = current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $M$ = molar mass of substance (g mol⁻¹)
- $n$ = valency / number of electrons transferred per ion
- $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹

### Second Law
**Statement:** When the *same* quantity of electricity is passed through *different* electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their **chemical equivalent weights (E = M/n)**.

$$
\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} \; = \; \dfrac{E_1}{E_2} \; = \; \dfrac{M_1 / n_1}{M_2 / n_2}
$$

### Numerical — Typical KTU Question
> **Problem:** An alkaline water electrolyzer operates at 500 A. Calculate (a) the mass of H₂ produced in 1 hour, and (b) the volume of H₂ at STP. Given M(H₂) = 2 g mol⁻¹, n = 2, F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹, Vm = 22.4 L mol⁻¹.

**Solution:**

(a) Mass of H₂:
$$
m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{500 \times 3600 \times 2}{2 \times 96{,}500}
$$

$$
m = \dfrac{3{,}600{,}000}{193{,}000} = 18.65 \,\text{g}
$$

(b) Moles of H₂ produced:
$$
n_{H_2} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{18.65}{2} = 9.327 \,\text{mol}
$$

Volume at STP:
$$
V_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times V_m = 9.327 \times 22.4 = 208.9 \,\text{L}
$$

**Verification (charge balance):** Charge passed = 500 A × 3600 s = 1,800,000 C. Moles of electrons = 1,800,000 / 96,500 = 18.65 mol. Moles of H₂ (n = 2) = 18.65 / 2 = 9.327 mol. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Insight:** Always re-verify by computing moles of electrons first. It catches arithmetic errors instantly. KTU examiners award 1 mark for this cross-check step.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Types of Electrolyzers (KTU Comparison)

| **Parameter** | **Alkaline (AEL)** | **PEM** | **Solid Oxide (SOEC)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Electrolyte | 25–30 wt% KOH (liquid) | Perfluorinated sulfonic acid (Nafion™) membrane | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) ceramic |
| Operating Temperature | 60 – 90 °C | 50 – 80 °C | 700 – 850 °C |
| Charge Carrier | OH⁻ | H⁺ | O²⁻ |
| Current Density | 0.2 – 0.6 A cm⁻² | 1.0 – 3.0 A cm⁻² | 0.3 – 1.0 A cm⁻² |
| Cell Voltage | 1.8 – 2.4 V | 1.8 – 2.2 V | 1.0 – 1.5 V |
| H₂ Purity | ≥ 99.5% | ≥ 99.99% | ≥ 99.99% |
| Stack Efficiency (HHV) | 63 – 70% | 70 – 82% | 80 – 90% |
| Capital Cost (₹/kW) | Lowest (mature) | High (Pt catalysts) | Very High (research stage) |
| Dynamic Operation (renewables) | Slow ramp-rate | Fast ramp (best) | Very slow (thermal cycling risk) |
| Maturity / TRL | TRL 9 (commercial) | TRL 8–9 (commercial) | TRL 5–7 (pilot) |
| **KTU 1-line takeaway** | Workhorse, cheapest | Best for fluctuating renewables | Highest efficiency, research-grade |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** The reason SOEC achieves *higher* efficiency is that at 800 °C, both $\Delta G$ and the activation overpotentials are *reduced*, lowering $V_{cell}$ closer to $E^\circ_{th}$. However, the high temperature introduces thermal-stress and sealing challenges.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 POX Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FEED: Natural Gas or Heavy Oil] --> B[DESULFURIZATION ZnO or HCDS]
    B --> C[PRE-HEATER 400 C]
    C --> D[POX REACTOR 1300 to 1500 C]
    O[PURE OXYGEN 99.5 percent] --> D
    D --> E[WASTE HEAT BOILER HP Steam Out]
    E --> F[SOOT SCRUBBER Water Wash]
    F --> G[HIGH TEMP WGS Reactor Fe3O4 or Cr Catalyst]
    G --> H[LOW TEMP WGS Reactor Cu Zn Al Catalyst]
    H --> I[CO2 ABSORBER AGR MEA or Selexol]
    I --> J[PSA Pressure Swing Adsorber]
    J --> K[H2 PRODUCT 99.99 percent Purity]
    J --> L[Tail Gas Recycle to POX]
    I --> M[CO2 Byproduct Sequestration or EOR]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **POX REACTOR** is the heart of the system — converting CH₄ + ½O₂ → CO + 2H₂.
- **HP Steam** is co-generated (and exported) from the WGS effluent cooling.
- **Tail gas** (unreacted CH₄, CO, H₂) is recycled to the POX burner for thermal integration.
- **CO₂** from the AGR is captured and either sequestered (CCUS pathway) or used for Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR).

---

## 4.2 Water Electrolyzer Cell — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DC [DC POWER SUPPLY RENEWABLE SOURCE]
        PS[PV ARRAY OR WIND DC BUS]
    end
    PS --> A
    A[ANODE Positive Electrode] -->|O2 evolution 2H2O yields O2 plus 4H plus 4e| C[CATHODE Negative Electrode]
    C -->|H2 evolution 2H plus 2e yields H2| PROD[H2 GAS COLLECTION]
    A --- M[MEMBRANE OR DIAPHRAM Ion Conductor]
    C --- M
    M -->|Hydroxyl ion OH or Proton H plus transport direction| M
    style DC fill:#FFF8DC
    style PROD fill:#E0FFE0
    style A fill:#FFE4E1
    style C fill:#E0FFFF
    style M fill:#FAFAD2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **DC power supply** is the *energy source*; for *green* H₂ this is a renewable (PV or wind) feed.
- At the **ANODE**, water is oxidized to O₂ (gas bubbles rise and are collected).
- At the **CATHODE**, H⁺ (or H₂O, in alkaline) is reduced to H₂ (gas bubbles rise and are collected).
- The **MEMBRANE** allows only the relevant ion (H⁺ in PEM, OH⁻ in AEL, O²⁻ in SOEC) to pass — it is the ionic "highway" and the gas separator in one.

---

## 4.3 Energy & Mass Flow — Block Diagram (Cell-Stack-System Levels)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        E1[ELECTRICAL ENERGY kWh]
        W1[DEIONIZED WATER H2O]
    end
    E1 --> CELL
    W1 --> CELL
    CELL[STACK OF CELLS N Cells in Series] --> OUTPUTS
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        H2P[H2 Gas High Purity]
        O2P[O2 Byproduct or Vent]
        HEAT[Waste Heat 60 to 90 C]
    end
    CELL --> HEAT
    CELL --> H2P
    CELL --> O2P
    H2P --> COMP[Compressor to 350 or 700 bar]
    COMP --> STORAGE[Buffer Storage]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Inputs:** 1 kg of H₂ requires ~9 kg of deionized water and ~50–55 kWh of electricity.
- **Outputs:** For every 1 Nm³ of H₂, you also produce 0.5 Nm³ of O₂ (a marketable byproduct in medical/industrial markets).
- **Heat:** The stack's waste heat (~30% of input energy) can be recovered for space heating or absorption chilling, boosting the *system* efficiency to > 85%.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Partial Oxidation of hydrocarbons. Write the balanced chemical equation for the partial oxidation of methane.**

**Model Answer:**
Partial Oxidation (POX) is the reaction of a hydrocarbon with a *limited* (sub-stoichiometric) supply of pure oxygen, producing a mixture of CO and H₂ known as **synthesis gas (syngas)**.

$$
\text{CH}_4 \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \; \longrightarrow \; \text{CO} \; + \; 2\,\text{H}_2
$$

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for balanced equation, 1 for noting the H₂:CO = 2:1 ratio.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis.**

**Model Answer:**
- **First Law:** The mass *m* of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge *Q* passed: $m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.
- **Second Law:** When the *same* charge is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their *equivalent weights*: $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{E_1}{E_2}$.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for each law with correct equation.)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the theoretical decomposition voltage of water at 25 °C and 1 atm. Clearly distinguish between the **thermodynamic reversible voltage (E°th)** and the **thermoneutral voltage (E°tn)**, and explain the practical significance of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the three principal types of water electrolyzers — **Alkaline (AEL), PEM, and Solid Oxide (SOEC)** — in terms of electrolyte, operating temperature, charge carrier, current density, and suitability for renewable energy integration. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Decomposition Voltage

**Step 1:** Write the net electrolysis reaction.
$$
\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_{2(g)}
$$

**Step 2:** Recall the standard-state thermodynamic data.
- $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$
- $\Delta H^\circ = +285.8 \,\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$
- $n = 2$ electrons per mole of H₂

**Step 3:** Apply the relation $E^\circ = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{n\,F}$.

$$
E^\circ_{th} = \dfrac{237{,}200 \,\text{J mol}^{-1}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \,\text{C mol}^{-1}} = 1.229 \,\text{V}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the thermoneutral voltage.

$$
E^\circ_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{n\,F} = \dfrac{285{,}800}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.481 \,\text{V}
$$

**Step 5:** Distinguish the two voltages.

| Voltage | Energy Basis | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E^\circ_{th} = 1.229$ V | Gibbs free energy ΔG° | Minimum voltage to *drive* the reaction; the cell *absorbs* 48.6 kJ/mol of heat from surroundings |
| $E^\circ_{tn} = 1.481$ V | Enthalpy ΔH° | Voltage at which the cell is *thermally self-sufficient*; all energy supplied as electricity |

**Step 6:** Practical significance.
- $E^\circ_{th}$ sets the **absolute floor** on cell voltage — no electrolyzer can go below this in practice (and even this is unreachable due to overpotentials).
- $E^\circ_{tn}$ defines the **energy-efficient operating point** (100% electrical-to-chemical conversion). Operating *above* 1.481 V wastes energy as heat; operating *below* 1.481 V requires external heating.
- Real cells operate at **1.8 – 2.5 V** due to **activation overpotential (η_act), ohmic overpotential (η_ohm), and concentration overpotential (η_conc)**.

#### Part (b) — Comparison of Electrolyzer Types

| Feature | **Alkaline (AEL)** | **PEM** | **Solid Oxide (SOEC)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Electrolyte | 25–30 wt% KOH aqueous | Nafion™ proton membrane | YSZ ceramic (ZrO₂ + Y₂O₃) |
| Temperature | 60 – 90 °C | 50 – 80 °C | 700 – 850 °C |
| Charge carrier | OH⁻ | H⁺ | O²⁻ |
| Current density | 0.2 – 0.6 A cm⁻² | 1.0 – 3.0 A cm⁻² | 0.3 – 1.0 A cm⁻² |
| Stack efficiency (HHV) | 63 – 70% | 70 – 82% | 80 – 90% |
| Renewable suitability | **Moderate** (slow ramp) | **Excellent** (fast dynamic response) | **Poor** (thermal stress) |
| Catalyst | Ni / Ni-Mo | Pt + IrO₂ | Perovskite (LSM, LSCF) |
| Cost | **Lowest** | High | Highest |
| TRL | 9 (commercial) | 8–9 | 5–7 (pilot) |

**Conclusion:** PEM electrolyzers are best suited for *intermittent* renewable sources (solar, wind) due to their fast dynamic response. AEL remains the workhorse for steady-state industrial H₂. SOEC has the highest *intrinsic* efficiency but is still in the pilot phase.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]` *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the help of a neat process-flow block diagram, describe the **Partial Oxidation (POX)** method of hydrogen production from methane. Include the role of the **Water-Gas Shift (WGS)** reactor and **acid-gas removal (AGR)** unit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An alkaline water electrolyzer operates at a current of **1500 A** for **8 hours**. Calculate: (i) the total mass of H₂ produced (in kg), (ii) the volume of H₂ at STP (in Nm³), and (iii) the **energy efficiency** of the electrolyzer if the cell voltage is **2.0 V**. Given: M(H₂) = 2 g mol⁻¹, n = 2, F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹, Vm = 22.4 L mol⁻¹, HHV(H₂) = 285.8 kJ mol⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — POX Process Description

**Block sequence** (must be drawn in the answer script):
1. **Feed pre-treatment** — desulfurization of natural gas to < 1 ppm H₂S.
2. **Mixing** with pure O₂ at O₂/C ratio of 0.45 – 0.6.
3. **POX Reactor** at 1200 – 1500 °C, 30 – 80 bar, refractory-lined, non-catalytic.
4. **Waste Heat Boiler (WHB)** for HP steam generation.
5. **Soot scrubber** (water wash) to remove carbon particulates.
6. **High-Temperature WGS** (Fe₃O₄/Cr catalyst, 350 – 450 °C) — bulk CO conversion.
7. **Low-Temperature WGS** (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst, 200 – 250 °C) — residual CO polishing to < 1%.
8. **AGR (CO₂ removal)** using MEA, Selexol, or Rectisol solvent.
9. **PSA** — final purification to **99.99% H₂**.

**Role of WGS:** Converts each mole of CO to one additional mole of H₂:
$$
\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2
$$
The WGS unit *doubles* the hydrogen yield per mole of methane.

**Role of AGR:** Removes CO₂ (and residual H₂S) from the shifted gas to deliver food-grade or fuel-cell-grade H₂.

**Net reaction** (POX + WGS):
$$
\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2 \quad \text{(overall steam-and-O}_2\text{-based H}_2\text{ production)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Electrolyzer Numerical

**Given:** I = 1500 A, t = 8 h = 28,800 s, V = 2.0 V, F = 96,500 C mol⁻¹, n = 2, M(H₂) = 2 g mol⁻¹, Vm = 22.4 L mol⁻¹, HHV = 285.8 kJ mol⁻¹.

**(i) Mass of H₂ produced:**
$$
m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{1500 \times 28{,}800 \times 2}{2 \times 96{,}500}
$$

$$
m = \dfrac{86{,}400{,}000}{193{,}000} = 447.67 \,\text{g} \approx 0.448 \,\text{kg}
$$

**(ii) Volume of H₂ at STP:**
$$
n_{H_2} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{447.67}{2} = 223.83 \,\text{mol}
$$

$$
V_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \times V_m = 223.83 \times 22.4 = 5014 \,\text{L} = 5.014 \,\text{Nm}^3
$$

**(iii) Energy efficiency (HHV basis):**

Moles of H₂ per second:
$$
\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{223.83}{28{,}800} = 7.77 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mol s}^{-1}
$$

Chemical energy produced (HHV basis):
$$
\dot{E}_{chem} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \times \text{HHV} = 7.77 \times 10^{-3} \times 285{,}800 = 2222.7 \,\text{W} = 2.22 \,\text{kW}
$$

Electrical energy input:
$$
\dot{E}_{elec} = V \times I = 2.0 \times 1500 = 3000 \,\text{W} = 3.0 \,\text{kW}
$$

Efficiency:
$$
\eta = \dfrac{\dot{E}_{chem}}{\dot{E}_{elec}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.22}{3.00} \times 100 = 74.1\%
$$

**Sanity check:** $\dfrac{E^\circ_{th}}{V_{cell}} = \dfrac{1.229}{2.0} = 61.5\%$ (the thermodynamic ceiling). Our 74.1% is *higher* than this ceiling — **this is impossible if we used E°th**. The correct comparison is $\dfrac{E^\circ_{tn}}{V_{cell}} = \dfrac{1.481}{2.0} = 74.05\%$ ✓ — confirming the HHV-basis efficiency of 74.1% is the correct interpretation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not confuse E°th with E°tn.** E°th (1.229 V) is used for *reversible work* (Gibbs), E°tn (1.481 V) is used for *total enthalpy*. Mixing them gives nonsensical efficiencies > 100%.
> 2. **Always state the basis** (HHV or LHV) when computing electrolyzer efficiency. KTU examiners explicitly mark the basis (1 mark).
> 3. **For POX, the H₂/CO ratio from methane is 2:1**, *not* 1:1. Confusing this with the SMR ratio (3:1) is the most common deduction point.
> 4. **Faraday's efficiency assumes 100% current utilization.** If the problem states "Faradaic efficiency = 95%", *multiply* the H₂ mass by 0.95. Many students forget this and lose 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **POX reaction for CH₄:** CH₄ + ½O₂ → CO + 2H₂. The **H₂/CO molar ratio = 2**.
- ✅ **POX operating conditions:** 1200 – 1500 °C, 30 – 80 bar, **pure O₂** (not air).
- ✅ **POX is mildly exothermic** (−36 kJ/mol); SMR is strongly endothermic (+206 kJ/mol). This is a frequently tested contrast.
- ✅ **WGS reaction:** CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ (ΔH = −41 kJ/mol). Doubles the H₂ yield from POX.
- ✅ **Net electrolysis:** 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂.
- ✅ **Theoretical voltage (ΔG°):** $E^\circ_{th} = 1.229$ V. **Thermoneutral (ΔH°):** $E^\circ_{tn} = 1.481$ V.
- ✅ **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹ ≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹ in numericals.
- ✅ **Faraday's First Law equation:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — commit this to memory.
- ✅ **Faraday's Second Law:** $m_1/m_2 = E_1/E_2$ (ratio of equivalent weights).
- ✅ **Electrolyzer types:** AEL (cheap, mature), PEM (fast dynamic, costly Pt), SOEC (highest efficiency, high T).
- ✅ **1 kg of H₂ requires** ~ 9 kg of deionized water and ~ 50 – 55 kWh of electricity (at typical 70% efficiency).
- ✅ **Cell voltage breakdown:** $V_{cell} = E^\circ_{th} + \eta_{act} + \eta_{ohm} + \eta_{conc}$.
- ✅ **Energy efficiency (HHV basis)** = (HHV × moles of H₂) / (V × I × t) × 100.
- ✅ **POX is preferred for heavy feedstocks** (residual oil, coal); **SMR is preferred for natural gas** at small-to-medium scale.
- ✅ **PSA delivers 99.99% H₂** from the shifted syngas; tail gas is recycled to the POX burner.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photo Electro Chemical Method, Thermo-chemical Water Splitting, Biological Process: Hydrogen Production from Microbial Biomass Conversion - Gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Photo Electro Chemical Method, Thermo-chemical Water Splitting, Biological Process: Hydrogen Production from Microbial Biomass Conversion - Gasification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Photo Electrochemical (PEC) Method of Hydrogen Production

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Photo Electrochemical (PEC) Method** is a direct solar-to-hydrogen conversion process in which water molecules are split into hydrogen and oxygen using semiconductor photoelectrodes immersed in an aqueous electrolyte and illuminated by sunlight. The phenomenon is fundamentally based on the **Honda–Fujishima Effect (1972)**, where photon absorption generates electron-hole pairs that drive the water-splitting redox reactions at the semiconductor-electrolyte interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The minimum thermodynamic voltage required to split water is **1.23 V** at standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm). However, due to overpotential losses, practical photoelectrodes require a band-gap energy of **1.6 – 2.4 eV**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **leaf floating on a pond**. The leaf (semiconductor) uses sunlight to push electrons from water molecules, releasing bubbles of oxygen on top and hydrogen below. In a PEC cell, the semiconductor plays the role of the leaf, and the water is the pond. Light acts as the energy "waiter" that carries energy to the catalyst (semiconductor), which then "tears" the water molecule apart into H₂ and O₂. No external electrical grid is needed — the photons do all the work directly.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Gibbs free energy (ΔG°) | **+237.2 kJ/mol** | Minimum energy required |
| Standard enthalpy (ΔH°) | **+285.8 kJ/mol** | Total heat input needed |
| Theoretical minimum voltage | **1.23 V** | E° = ΔG°/(nF) |
| Solar constant | **1361 W/m²** | AM 1.5 reference |
| Avogadro's number (Nₐ) | **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** | Stoichiometric scaling |
| Faraday constant (F) | **96485 C/mol** | Charge per mole e⁻ |

---

## 1.2 Thermochemical Water Splitting

### Formal Academic Definition
**Thermochemical Water Splitting** is a closed-loop, multi-step cyclic process in which high-temperature thermal energy (from solar concentrators, nuclear reactors, or industrial waste heat) drives a sequence of chemical reactions that ultimately decompose water into hydrogen and oxygen, with all intermediate chemicals being recycled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout:** A thermochemical cycle must satisfy: (1) Net reaction = H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂, (2) All intermediates fully recycled, (3) Total ΔH = +285.8 kJ/mol H₂.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of it like a **chemical "Rube Goldberg machine"**. Instead of using a single violent reaction, we use a chain of milder chemical reactions at progressively different temperatures. Each step is a "trick" that gradually dismantles the water molecule, and at the end, all the helper chemicals are returned to the start — like actors in a play returning to the stage after each scene. The benefit is that the peak temperature required is much lower than the direct thermal dissociation of water (>2500 °C).

### Key Reference Cycles
- **Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle** — General Atomics cycle, max temp ≈ **900 °C**
- **Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) Cycle** — Maximum temp ≈ **550 °C**
- **Zinc-Zinc Oxide (Zn-ZnO) Cycle** — Two-step, max temp ≈ **2000 °C**
- **Hybrid Sulfur (HyS) Cycle** — Uses electrolysis, max temp ≈ **900 °C**

---

## 1.3 Biological Hydrogen Production — Microbial Biomass Conversion / Gasification

### Formal Academic Definition
**Biological Hydrogen Production** encompasses processes where microorganisms (bacteria, algae, cyanobacteria) or their enzymatic systems produce H₂ from organic substrates under ambient or mild conditions. **Microbial biomass gasification** specifically refers to the thermochemical conversion of solid biomass feedstocks (agricultural residues, algae, energy crops) into a hydrogen-rich producer gas using partial oxidation, steam, or biological catalysts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass Gasification Reaction (Simplified):**
> $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 + \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{tar} + \text{char}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a **kitchen compost bin that runs in reverse**. Microbes, given a sugar-rich diet, "burp" out hydrogen and CO₂. Alternatively, in a gasifier, biomass (wood chips, algae) is heated with a limited oxygen supply so it does not burn completely — instead, it transforms into a combustible gas rich in hydrogen, much like turning wet wood into a smoky fuel in a campfire, but controlled and clean.

### Classification of Biological Routes
- **Direct Biophotolysis** — Green algae/cyanobacteria + sunlight → H₂
- **Indirect Biophotolysis** — Algae produce carbohydrates, then ferment
- **Photofermentation** — Purple non-sulfur bacteria, organic acids → H₂
- **Dark Fermentation** — Anaerobic bacteria, glucose → H₂ + acids
- **Microbial Biomass Gasification** — Combined biochemical + thermochemical
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Photo Electrochemical (PEC) Method — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The Honda–Fujishima Principle (1972)

When a semiconductor photoelectrode is immersed in an electrolyte and illuminated with photons of energy **E_photon ≥ E_g** (band gap), electrons are excited from the **valence band (VB)** to the **conduction band (CB)**, leaving behind holes in the VB.

**Mechanism in 4 logical steps:**
1. **Photon Absorption:** Sunlight (hν) strikes the photoelectrode surface.
2. **Charge Generation:** Electrons (e⁻) promoted to CB; holes (h⁺) remain in VB.
3. **Charge Separation:** Built-in electric field (or applied bias) separates the e⁻–h⁺ pairs to avoid recombination.
4. **Surface Redox Reactions:**
   - **At photoanode:** $2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4\text{h}^+ \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+$ (oxygen evolution)
   - **At photocathode:** $4\text{H}^+ + 4\text{e}^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2$ (hydrogen evolution)

### 2.1.2 Why a Band Gap of 1.6–2.4 eV?

- Below 1.23 eV → insufficient thermodynamic driving force.
- Above 3.0 eV → only ~5% of solar spectrum is usable (poor efficiency).
- 1.6–2.4 eV balances the visible-light absorption (40–60% of AM 1.5) with sufficient overpotential to overcome kinetic losses.

### 2.1.3 Engineering Materials in PEC

| Material Class | Example | Band Gap (eV) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Metal Oxides | TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, WO₃ | 2.0 – 3.2 | Stable, but TiO₂ absorbs only UV |
| III-V Semiconductors | GaAs, InP | 1.3 – 1.9 | High efficiency, but expensive & corrode |
| Chalcogenides | CdS, CdSe, MoS₂ | 1.7 – 2.4 | Good visible absorption, photocorrosion issues |
| Perovskites | CH₃NH₃PbI₃ | 1.5 – 2.3 | Emerging, stability concerns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Efficiency Definition:** The **Solar-to-Hydrogen (STH) Efficiency** is the most important metric for PEC.
> $$\eta_{STH} = \frac{\text{Chemical energy of H}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{Solar energy incident on cell}} \times 100\%$$

---

## 2.2 Thermochemical Water Splitting — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Why Thermochemical Cycles?

Direct thermal dissociation of water:
$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \quad (\Delta H = +285.8 \text{ kJ/mol})$$
requires temperatures **above 2500 °C** to achieve reasonable conversion (>10%). This demands exotic materials and extreme energy losses. Thermochemical cycles break this single demanding step into several smaller steps at lower temperatures.

### 2.2.2 Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle — Three Reactions

**Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (Exothermic, 20–120 °C):**
$$\text{SO}_2 + \text{I}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HI}$$

**Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, 800–900 °C):**
$$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

**Step 3 — Hydriodic Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, 300–450 °C):**
$$2\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{I}_2 + \text{H}_2$$

**Net Reaction:**
$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

### 2.2.3 Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) Cycle — Simplified 4-Step

| Step | Reaction | T (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $2\text{Cu} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow 2\text{CuCl} + \text{H}_2$ | 425–475 |
| 2 | $2\text{CuCl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CuCl} + \text{Cl}_2$ | 450 |
| 3 | $\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HCl} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$ | 150–200 |
| 4 | Electrolysis / thermolysis to balance | — |

### 2.2.4 The Second-Law Efficiency

The **second-law (exergy) efficiency** of a thermochemical cycle is given by:

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{Exergy of H}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{Total exergy input}}$$

For the S-I cycle, theoretical efficiencies approach **50–60%** with high-temperature nuclear or concentrated solar heat.

---

## 2.3 Biological Hydrogen Production — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.3.1 Enzymatic Machinery

Three key enzymes drive biohydrogen production:

1. **Hydrogenase** — Catalyzes: $2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{e}^- \leftrightarrow \text{H}_2$
2. **Nitrogenase** — Catalyzes N₂ reduction; obligatorily produces H₂ as a side product.
3. **Formate Hydrogen Lyase (FHL)** — Splits formate into H₂ + CO₂.

### 2.3.2 Microbial Biomass Gasification — Sub-processes

1. **Drying** — Moisture removed (100–200 °C).
2. **Pyrolysis** — Biomass decomposes into char + volatiles (300–600 °C).
3. **Oxidation (Partial)** — Some char + O₂ → CO + CO₂ (provides heat).
4. **Reduction** — Steam + char → CO + H₂ (endothermic).
5. **Water-Gas Shift (WGS):** $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ (boosts H₂ yield).
6. **Tar Reforming** — Cracking heavy hydrocarbons into lighter gases.

### 2.3.3 Key Stoichiometric Reactions in Gasification

| Reaction Name | Equation | ΔH° (kJ/mol) | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partial Oxidation | $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$ | −111 | Exothermic heat source |
| Steam Gasification (Water-Gas) | $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ | +131 | Primary H₂ source |
| Boudouard | $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$ | +172 | CO₂ consumption |
| Methanation | $\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$ | −75 | CH₄ formation |
| Water-Gas Shift | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | −41 | H₂ enrichment |
| Steam Reforming of Methane | $\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2$ | +206 | CH₄ conversion |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question on hydrogen production methods.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G° = nFE°_{cell}$ | Thermodynamic cell potential | J/mol, V |
| 2 | $E°_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta G°}{nF} = 1.23 \text{ V}$ | Minimum water-splitting voltage | V |
| 3 | $\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \Delta G°_{H_2}}{P_{solar} \cdot A}$ | Solar-to-hydrogen efficiency | % |
| 4 | $\eta_{II} = 1 - \dfrac{T_0}{T_{H}}$ | Carnot (thermochemical) limit | — |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{E_g \text{ (eV)}}$ | Absorption edge wavelength | nm |
| 6 | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{Q \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law for H₂ mass | g |
| 7 | $Y_{H_2} = \dfrac{n_{H_2,out}}{n_{biomass,in}}$ | Hydrogen yield (gasification) | mol/mol |
| 8 | $LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | Lower heating value of H₂ | MJ/kg |
| 9 | $ER = \dfrac{O_2 \text{ supplied}}{O_2 \text{ for stoichiometric combustion}}$ | Equivalence ratio (gasifier) | — |
| 10 | $\eta_{gasifier} = \dfrac{\text{Cold gas energy}}{\text{Feedstock energy}}$ | Cold gas efficiency | % |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **PEC:** Long-term NASA space missions, off-grid island power (e.g., Japan's "Artificial Leaf" project).
- **Thermochemical:** Japan Atomic Energy Agency (JAEA) HTTR reactor coupled to S-I pilot plant; concentrated solar tower (Solar-H₂, EU).
- **Biological/Gasification:** Sweden's GoBiGas project; India's National Hydrogen Energy Mission targets agri-residue gasification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Minimum Cell Voltage for Water Splitting

The overall water-splitting reaction is:
$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_{2(g)}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the standard free energy change.**
The standard Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water at 25 °C is:
$$\Delta G°_f (\text{H}_2\text{O},l) = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Since H₂ and O₂ in their standard states have $\Delta G°_f = 0$, the net $\Delta G°$ for the splitting reaction is:
$$\Delta G°_{rxn} = \Delta G°_f(\text{H}_2) + \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta G°_f(\text{O}_2) - \Delta G°_f(\text{H}_2\text{O},l)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**
$$\Delta G°_{rxn} = 0 + 0 - (-237.2) = +237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The positive sign confirms that splitting is **non-spontaneous**; external energy must be supplied.

**Step 3 — Relate to electrical work using the Gibbs-Helmholtz relation.**
The maximum useful (non-PV, non-PdV) work obtainable is the electrical work:
$$W_{elec} = n F E°_{cell}$$

where $n$ = electrons transferred per mole of H₂ = **2**, $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**, $E°_{cell}$ = cell voltage.

**Step 4 — Equate the electrical work to the free energy change.**
At equilibrium (minimum input):
$$n F E°_{min} = \Delta G°_{rxn}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E°_{min}$.**
$$E°_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta G°_{rxn}}{n F} = \dfrac{237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E°_{min} = 1.229 \text{ V} \approx 1.23 \text{ V}$$

This is the **thermodynamic floor** — no PEC or electrolytic cell can split water below 1.23 V.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Threshold Wavelength from Band Gap

The band gap of a semiconductor must be at least 1.23 eV. Convert this energy to the maximum usable wavelength.

**Step 1 — Use Planck–Einstein relation.**
$$E = h \nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the universal constants.**
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- Speed of light: $c = 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- 1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum wavelength (absorption edge).**
$$\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{h c}{E_g}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in $E_g = 1.23 \text{ eV}$.**
$$\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{1.23 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9705 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.0087 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 1008 \text{ nm}}$$

**Interpretation:** Photons with wavelength shorter than ~1008 nm (i.e., in the visible and UV range) carry enough energy to split water. This defines the **solar window** for PEC materials.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Solar-to-Hydrogen (STH) Efficiency

A PEC cell of active area **A** is illuminated by solar irradiance **G**. The hydrogen production rate is $\dot{n}_{H_2}$ mol/s.

**Step 1 — Define the input power.**
$$P_{in} = G \cdot A \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 2 — Define the chemical power output.**
The chemical energy stored in hydrogen is its lower heating value (LHV) per unit time:
$$P_{chem} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot 240 \text{ kJ/mol (HHV basis)}$$

A more rigorous definition uses $\Delta G°_{H_2} = 237 \text{ kJ/mol}$:
$$P_{chem} = \dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \Delta G°_{H_2}$$

**Step 3 — Form the efficiency ratio.**
$$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{P_{chem}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2} \cdot \Delta G°_{H_2}}{G \cdot A}$$

**Step 4 — Sample numerical evaluation.**
Suppose a 100 cm² PEC panel produces 0.5 mmol/h of H₂ under AM 1.5G (1000 W/m²):
- $A = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$
- $\dot{n}_{H_2} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} / 3600 = 1.389 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/s}$
- $P_{in} = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10 \text{ W}$
- $P_{chem} = 1.389 \times 10^{-7} \times 237{,}200 = 0.03295 \text{ W}$

$$\eta_{STH} = \dfrac{0.03295}{10} = 0.003295 = 0.33\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> Most state-of-the-art PEC systems achieve **3–10% STH efficiency**. Values above 10% are considered commercially promising.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Faraday's Law for Hydrogen Mass Produced in Electrolysis / PEC

**Step 1 — Faraday's first law of electrolysis.**
The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is proportional to the charge passed:
$$m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass of H₂ = 2.016 g/mol, $n$ = 2.

**Step 2 — For hydrogen specifically.**
$$m_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \times 2.016}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$m_{H_2} = 1.0445 \times 10^{-5} \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{(g)}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of hydrogen at STP (22.4 L/mol).**
$$V_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \times 22{,}400}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 0.1161 \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{(mL)}$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
A PEC cell draws 2 A for 1 hour. Find $m_{H_2}$ and $V_{H_2}$.
- $I \cdot t = 2 \times 3600 = 7200 \text{ C}$
- $m_{H_2} = 1.0445 \times 10^{-5} \times 7200 = 0.0752 \text{ g}$
- $V_{H_2} = 0.1161 \times 7200 = 835.9 \text{ mL} \approx 0.836 \text{ L}$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Thermochemical Cycle — Energy Balance on S-I Cycle

**Step 1 — Write enthalpy of each step.**

| Step | Reaction | ΔH (kJ/mol H₂) |
|---|---|---|
| Bunsen | SO₂ + I₂ + 2H₂O → H₂SO₄ + 2HI | −200 (exothermic) |
| H₂SO₄ decomposition | H₂SO₄ → SO₂ + H₂O + ½O₂ | +186 |
| HI decomposition | 2HI → I₂ + H₂ | +12 |

**Step 2 — Net enthalpy (per mol H₂ produced).**
$$\Delta H_{net} = -200 + 186 + 12 = -2 \text{ kJ/mol (≈ 0)}$$

**Step 3 — Net reaction must equal water splitting.**
By Hess's law, the sum of step enthalpies equals the enthalpy of:
$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \quad \Delta H = +285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Energy input from high-temperature heat.**
The exothermic Bunsen step releases waste heat (≈ 200 kJ/mol), which can partially offset the endothermic decomposition steps. The **net high-temperature heat input** is approximately **+190 kJ/mol H₂**.

**Step 5 — Thermal efficiency of the S-I cycle.**
$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{LHV_{H_2}}{\text{Total heat input}} = \dfrac{240}{285.8} = 84\% \text{ (theoretical max)}$$

With realistic heat recovery, practical efficiencies are **40–50%**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Hydrogen Yield from Biomass Gasification

**Step 1 — Assume a biomass formula.**
Consider dry biomass with elemental composition C₆H₁₀O₅ (typical of cellulose, MW = 162 g/mol).

**Step 2 — Steam gasification (idealized, all carbon → CO, all H → H₂).**
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 6\text{CO} + 6\text{H}_2$$

After water-gas shift (WGS):
$$6\text{CO} + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 6\text{CO}_2 + 6\text{H}_2$$

**Step 3 — Total H₂ per mole of biomass.**
- From gasification: 6 mol H₂
- From WGS: 6 mol H₂
- **Total: 12 mol H₂ per mole of C₆H₁₀O₅**

**Step 4 — Hydrogen yield.**
$$Y_{H_2} = \dfrac{12}{1} = 12 \text{ mol H}_2/\text{mol biomass} = 74 \text{ mol H}_2/\text{kg biomass}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of H₂ per kg of dry biomass.**
$$m_{H_2} = 74 \times 2.016 = 149 \text{ g H}_2/\text{kg biomass}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real biomass gasifiers achieve **50–80 g H₂/kg dry biomass** due to tar formation, incomplete conversion, and heat losses.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Hydrogen Production Routes

| Criterion | PEC Method | Thermochemical | Biological / Gasification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Sunlight (direct) | High-T heat (± electricity) | Biomass + low-T heat |
| Operating temperature | 25–80 °C | 500–2000 °C | 25–900 °C (gasifier) |
| Theoretical efficiency | ~30% (STH) | 50–60% (2nd law) | 35–50% (cold gas eff.) |
| Current TRL | 3–5 (lab) | 4–6 (pilot) | 7–9 (commercial gasifiers) |
| CO₂ footprint | Zero | Zero (if heat is clean) | Near-zero (biogenic) |
| Capital cost | Medium–High | High | Low–Medium |
| Hydrogen purity | High (with membrane) | High (with separation) | Medium (needs cleanup) |
| Scalability | Limited by land | Modular | Proven industrial scale |
| Key challenge | Material stability | Materials corrosion | Tar, char, gas cleanup |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Photo Electrochemical (PEC) Cell — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sun["SUNLIGHT PHOTON SOURCE"] --> Electrode1["PHOTOANODE TiO2 OR Fe2O3 SEMICONDUCTOR FILM"]
    Electrode1 --> Electrolyte["AQUEOUS ELECTROLYTE H2O WITH NaOH OR H2SO4"]
    Electrolyte --> Electrode2["PHOTOCATHODE Pt OR NiMo COATED"]
    Electrode2 --> Wire["EXTERNAL WIRE CONDUCTION PATH"]
    Wire --> Electrode1
    Electrode1 --> GasO2["O2 GAS EVOLUTION VALENCE BAND HOLES"]
    Electrode2 --> GasH2["H2 GAS EVOLUTION CONDUCTION BAND ELECTRONS"]
    Electrode1 -.->|"h plus VB"| GasO2
    Electrode2 -.->|"e minus CB"| GasH2
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- Sunlight strikes the **photoanode**, generating electron-hole pairs.
- Holes oxidize water at the anode → **O₂** is released.
- Electrons travel through the external wire to the **photocathode**.
- At the cathode, electrons reduce H⁺ ions → **H₂** is released.

---

## 4.2 Thermochemical Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Feed["WATER FEED H2O IN"] --> Bunsen["STEP 1 BUNSEN REACTOR 20 TO 120 C"]
    Bunsen --> MixAcid["MIXTURE H2SO4 AND 2HI AQUEOUS"]
    MixAcid --> Sep1["SEPARATOR I"]
    Sep1 --> Acid["H2SO4 STREAM"]
    Sep1 --> HydAcid["HI STREAM"]
    Acid --> DecomAcid["STEP 2 SULFURIC ACID DECOMPOSER 800 TO 900 C"]
    DecomAcid --> RecycleSO2["SO2 RECYCLED TO BUNSEN"]
    DecomAcid --> O2Out["O2 GAS VENTED"]
    DecomAcid --> H2Oback["H2O RECYCLED"]
    HydAcid --> DecomHI["STEP 3 HI DECOMPOSER 300 TO 450 C"]
    DecomHI --> RecycleI2["I2 RECYCLED TO BUNSEN"]
    DecomHI --> H2Out["H2 GAS PRODUCT"]
    H2Oback --> Feed
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- Step 1 (Bunsen) consumes water, SO₂, and I₂ → produces H₂SO₄ and HI.
- The acid mixture is separated; each acid is decomposed in its own reactor.
- H₂SO₄ → H₂O + SO₂ + ½O₂; HI → ½I₂ + ½H₂.
- All intermediates (I₂, SO₂) are recycled. Net reaction: H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂.

---

## 4.3 Biomass Gasification Process — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Biomass["BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK AGRI RESIDUE OR ALGAE"] --> Dryer["DRYING ZONE 100 TO 200 C"]
    Dryer --> Pyro["PYROLYSIS ZONE 300 TO 600 C"]
    Pyro --> Char["CHAR BED SOLID RESIDUE"]
    Pyro --> Vola["VOLATILES CO H2 CH4 TAR"]
    Char --> OxZone["PARTIAL OXIDATION ZONE 800 TO 1200 C"]
    OxZone --> Heat["HEAT FOR ENDOTHERMIC REACTIONS"]
    Vola --> RedZone["REDUCTION ZONE 700 TO 900 C"]
    OxZone --> RedZone
    RedZone --> Syngas["RAW SYNGAS CO H2 CO2 CH4 N2 TAR"]
    Heat --> RedZone
    Syngas --> Cleanup["GAS CLEANUP CYCLONE FILTER SCRUBBER"]
    Cleanup --> WGS["WATER GAS SHIFT REACTOR CO H2O TO CO2 H2"]
    WGS --> Sep["H2 SEPARATION PSA OR MEMBRANE"]
    Sep --> H2Prod["PURE H2 PRODUCT 99.99 PERCENT"]
    Sep --> CO2Vent["CO2 VENT OR CAPTURED"]
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- Biomass enters a series of thermal zones where it dries, pyrolyzes, and partially oxidizes.
- Char provides heat; volatiles are reformed in the reduction zone into raw syngas.
- After cleanup and water-gas shift, hydrogen is enriched and separated via PSA (Pressure Swing Adsorption) or membranes.
- Final product: high-purity H₂ with CO₂ as a by-product.

---

## 4.4 Biological Hydrogen Production Pathways — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SunBio["SUNLIGHT"] --> DB["DIRECT BIOPHOTOLYSIS GREEN ALGAE CYANOBACTERIA"]
    SunBio --> IB["INDIRECT BIOPHOTOLYSIS ALGAE BIOMASS FIRST"]
    OrgAcid["ORGANIC ACIDS"] --> PF["PHOTOFERMENTATION PURPLE NON SULFUR BACTERIA"]
    Glucose["GLUCOSE OR SUGARS"] --> DF["DARK FERMENTATION ANAEROBIC BACTERIA"]
    IB --> Glucose
    DB --> H2Bio1["H2 PRODUCT"]
    PF --> H2Bio2["H2 PRODUCT"]
    DF --> H2Bio3["H2 PRODUCT"]
    BiomassBio["WET BIOMASS"] --> GasBio["MICROBIAL GASIFICATION ANAEROBIC DIGESTER PLUS GASIFIER"]
    GasBio --> H2Bio4["H2 RICH SYNGAS"]
```

**Explanation of flow:**
- Four biological pathways converge on hydrogen production.
- Direct and indirect biophotolysis use sunlight + water.
- Photofermentation and dark fermentation use organic substrates (no light needed for dark).
- Microbial gasification handles wet biomass via combined biochemical + thermochemical steps.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Photo Electrochemical (PEC) method of hydrogen production. State the Honda–Fujishima effect.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Photo Electrochemical (PEC) method of hydrogen production is a process in which water is split into hydrogen and oxygen using sunlight and semiconductor photoelectrodes immersed in an electrolyte. The phenomenon is based on the **Honda–Fujishima effect (1972)**, in which UV light incident on a TiO₂ photoanode generates electron-hole pairs that drive the water-splitting redox reactions — oxidation at the anode releasing O₂ and reduction at the cathode releasing H₂. Theoretically, a minimum cell voltage of **1.23 V** (corresponding to ΔG° = +237.2 kJ/mol) is required, while practical semiconductors have band-gap energies between **1.6 and 2.4 eV**.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is thermochemical water splitting? Name two well-known thermochemical cycles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thermochemical water splitting is a closed-loop, multi-step process in which high-temperature heat (from solar concentrators or nuclear reactors) drives a sequence of chemical reactions that decompose water into hydrogen and oxygen, with all intermediate chemicals fully recycled. The peak temperature required is significantly lower than direct thermal dissociation (>2500 °C).

Two well-known cycles are:
1. **Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) cycle** — three-step cycle, maximum temperature ≈ 900 °C.
2. **Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) cycle** — four-step cycle, maximum temperature ≈ 550 °C.

(Other acceptable: Zinc-Zinc Oxide cycle, Hybrid Sulfur cycle.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of the Photo Electrochemical (PEC) cell for hydrogen production. Discuss the role of band-gap energy in selecting the photoelectrode material. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle of PEC Cell (4 marks):**
A PEC cell consists of a **photoanode** (typically n-type semiconductor such as TiO₂, Fe₂O₃, or WO₃) and a **photocathode** (p-type semiconductor or metal catalyst such as Pt), both immersed in an aqueous electrolyte and connected via an external wire.

When sunlight (photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$) strikes the photoanode:
1. **Photon absorption** excites electrons from the VB to the CB, creating electron-hole pairs.
2. The **electric field at the semiconductor-electrolyte junction** separates the e⁻–h⁺ pairs, preventing recombination.
3. **At the photoanode surface (oxidation):**
   $$2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4\text{h}^+ \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+$$
4. **At the photocathode surface (reduction):**
   $$4\text{H}^+ + 4\text{e}^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2$$
5. Electrons flow through the external circuit from anode to cathode, completing the loop.

**Role of Band-Gap Energy (3 marks):**
- The band gap $E_g$ of the semiconductor determines the **maximum wavelength of absorbed photons**: $\lambda_{max} = 1240/E_g$ (in nm).
- For water splitting, $E_g$ must exceed **1.23 eV** (thermodynamic minimum).
- Smaller $E_g$ (~1.1 eV) → absorbs more sunlight, but insufficient driving force.
- Larger $E_g$ (>3.0 eV) → absorbs only UV (4–5% of solar spectrum) — poor efficiency.
- **Optimum range: 1.6 – 2.4 eV**, balancing visible-light absorption and overpotential.
- Example: Fe₂O₃ ($E_g$ = 2.1 eV) absorbs visible light and has good stability; TiO₂ ($E_g$ = 3.2 eV) is stable but absorbs only UV.

---

**(b) Derive the theoretical minimum cell voltage required to split water. Compute the maximum wavelength of light capable of driving this reaction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation of Minimum Voltage (4 marks):**
The overall water-splitting reaction:
$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \rightarrow \text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \quad \Delta G° = +237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}$$

Equating this to the maximum electrical work for $n = 2$ electrons per H₂:
$$n F E°_{min} = \Delta G°$$

$$E°_{min} = \dfrac{237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.229 \text{ V} \approx 1.23 \text{ V}$$

**Computation of Maximum Wavelength (3 marks):**
Using the Planck–Einstein relation:
$$\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{h c}{E_g} = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.23 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\lambda_{max} = 1.009 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1009 \text{ nm}$$

**Conclusion:** Photons with $\lambda < 1009$ nm (i.e., visible and UV) can in principle drive the water-splitting reaction.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) Describe the Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) thermochemical cycle for hydrogen production. Write all three reaction steps and identify the maximum temperature step. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The S-I cycle (also called the **General Atomics cycle**) is a closed-loop thermochemical process that decomposes water using high-temperature heat. Its principal advantage is that all intermediate chemicals (SO₂, I₂, H₂SO₄, HI) are fully recycled, and the maximum temperature required is only **~900 °C** (versus >2500 °C for direct thermal dissociation).

**Three Reaction Steps (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (Exothermic, 20 – 120 °C):**
$$\text{SO}_2 + \text{I}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{HI}$$
This step produces sulfuric and hydriodic acids.

**Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, 800 – 900 °C) — MAXIMUM TEMPERATURE STEP:**
$$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$
This is the most energy-intensive step and sets the upper temperature limit of the cycle.

**Step 3 — Hydriodic Acid Decomposition (Endothermic, 300 – 450 °C):**
$$2\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{I}_2 + \text{H}_2$$

**Net Reaction (1 mark):**
$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

The hydrogen yield is 1 mole per mole of water input. SO₂ and I₂ are recycled to Step 1.

---

**(b) Explain microbial biomass gasification for hydrogen production. List the major reactions and the role of the water-gas shift reaction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Microbial Biomass Gasification (2 marks):**
Microbial biomass gasification is a hybrid biochemical–thermochemical route where wet biomass (agricultural residue, algae, food waste) is converted into a hydrogen-rich producer gas. The process combines anaerobic digestion (biological) with steam/air gasification (thermochemical), making it tolerant to high moisture content that conventional gasifiers cannot handle.

**Process Steps (2 marks):**
1. **Drying** (100–200 °C) → moisture removal.
2. **Pyrolysis** (300–600 °C) → biomass → char + volatiles (CO, H₂, CH₄, tar).
3. **Partial oxidation** of char → supplies process heat.
4. **Reduction zone** → char + H₂O → CO + H₂ (steam gasification).
5. **Water-gas shift** → H₂ enrichment.
6. **Gas cleanup** (cyclone, scrubber) and H₂ separation (PSA/membrane).

**Major Reactions (2 marks):**
- Partial Oxidation: $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$ (−111 kJ/mol)
- Steam Gasification: $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ (+131 kJ/mol)
- Methanation: $\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$ (−75 kJ/mol)

**Role of Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reaction (1 mark):**
$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The WGS reaction **converts carbon monoxide (a pollutant and a non-hydrogen product) into additional hydrogen**, while simultaneously removing CO. It is typically carried out in two stages:
- **High-T shift** (350–450 °C) with Fe-Cr catalyst — fast but incomplete.
- **Low-T shift** (190–250 °C) with Cu-Zn-Al catalyst — slow but achieves high conversion.

By performing WGS, hydrogen yield increases by ~30–50%, and CO content drops below 1%, enabling cleaner downstream separation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This Before You Write!**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "minimum voltage = 1.23 V" without showing the derivation from $\Delta G° = nFE°$. Marks are lost for skipping steps.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **STH efficiency** with **Faradaic efficiency** — they are different! STH uses solar input; Faradaic uses current input.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In S-I cycle, students often write the wrong stoichiometry for HI decomposition. It is $2\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{I}_2 + \text{H}_2$, not $\text{HI} \rightarrow \text{H} + \text{I}$.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** For biomass gasification, students forget to mention **tar cracking** and **char gasification** as separate sub-processes.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Mixing up **endothermic** and **exothermic** steps in thermochemical cycles — Bunsen is exothermic; the two decomposition steps are endothermic.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Photo Electrochemical (PEC) Method
- [x] Based on the **Honda–Fujishima effect (1972)** on TiO₂.
- [x] Theoretical minimum voltage = **1.23 V** from $\Delta G° = +237.2$ kJ/mol.
- [x] Optimum band-gap range: **1.6 – 2.4 eV**.
- [x] Maximum absorption wavelength: $\lambda_{max} = 1240/E_g$ nm.
- [x] Two half-reactions: water oxidation (anode) + proton reduction (cathode).
- [x] Key metric: **Solar-to-Hydrogen (STH) efficiency**.
- [x] Major challenge: **photocorrosion of narrow-band-gap semiconductors**.

### ✅ Thermochemical Water Splitting
- [x] Direct thermal dissociation needs **>2500 °C**; cycles lower this to **500–900 °C**.
- [x] **S-I cycle:** Bunsen (20–120 °C) → H₂SO₄ decomposition (900 °C) → HI decomposition (450 °C).
- [x] **Cu-Cl cycle:** Lower temperature (~550 °C), more suitable for nuclear heat sources.
- [x] All intermediates fully recycled; net reaction = H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂.
- [x] Practical efficiency: **40–60%** (theoretical ~84% with infinite heat recovery).

### ✅ Biological Hydrogen Production & Biomass Gasification
- [x] **Direct biophotolysis** — green algae + sunlight → H₂.
- [x] **Photofermentation** — purple bacteria + organic acids + light → H₂.
- [x] **Dark fermentation** — anaerobic bacteria + glucose → H₂ (no light needed).
- [x] Key enzymes: **hydrogenase, nitrogenase, formate hydrogen lyase**.
- [x] **Biomass gasification** = drying + pyrolysis + partial oxidation + reduction + WGS + cleanup.
- [x] **Water-gas shift** doubles H₂ yield and removes CO.
- [x] Practical H₂ yield: **50–80 g H₂/kg dry biomass**.
- [x] **Equivalence ratio (ER)** = 0.2–0.4 for hydrogen-rich producer gas.

### ✅ Universal Constants to Memorize
- [x] $\Delta G°_{H_2O} = 237.2$ kJ/mol
- [x] $\Delta H°_{H_2O} = 285.8$ kJ/mol
- [x] $E°_{min} = 1.23$ V
- [x] $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- [x] $LHV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg; $HHV_{H_2} = 142$ MJ/kg
- [x] $\lambda_{max} = 1240/E_g$ nm (with $E_g$ in eV)

### ✅ Exam Strategy — KTU 2024 Scheme
- [x] **Part A (3 marks):** 2–3 sentences + key value or reaction. Be precise.
- [x] **Part B (14 marks):** Sub-parts are typically 7+7. Always include a **diagram** (worth 1–2 marks implicitly via presentation).
- [x] Use **standard KTU terminology** (e.g., "Honda–Fujishima effect", "Bunsen reaction", "Cold gas efficiency").
- [x] Show **all derivation steps** — partial marks are awarded generously.
- [x] Conclude with a **final boxed answer** wherever a numerical result is required.

topics.title: Pyrolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pyrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 MODULE 2 — PYROLYSIS: FOUNDATIONAL CONCEPTS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Terminology)

**Pyrolysis** is a **thermochemical decomposition process** in which organic feedstocks (biomass, plastics, coal, or organic wastes) are heated to elevated temperatures (**typically 300 °C – 900 °C**) in the **complete absence of oxygen (or with a stoichiometrically insignificant quantity of oxidant)**. Under these oxygen-starved conditions, the long-chain hydrocarbon molecules of the feedstock are broken down into smaller, energy-rich fragments, yielding a tri-phasic product slate:

1. **Pyrolysis Gas (Pyrogas / Non-condensable gases):** A mixture dominated by **H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄, and light C₂–C₃ hydrocarbons**.
2. **Pyrolysis Liquid (Bio-oil / Pyrolytic oil):** A complex oxygenated aqueous-organic condensate.
3. **Pyrochar (Biochar / Solid residue):** A carbon-rich, porous solid residue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition to Memorize:**
> *Pyrolysis is the thermal disintegration of carbonaceous material into char, tar, and gas fractions in an oxygen-deficient atmosphere, where the heat required for the endothermic decomposition is supplied externally.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Black Bread" Analogy

Imagine you place a slice of bread inside a sealed metal container with no air holes and put it into a very hot oven (**above 300 °C**). The bread does **not burn into ashes** (because there is no oxygen to support combustion), but it **chars, shrinks, darkens, and releases a smoky vapour that condenses as a tar-like liquid on the cooler lid of the container**, while a permanent gas escapes if you pierce a small vent. 

- The **blackened bread** → **Pyrochar (solid)**.
- The **tar-like condensate** → **Pyrolytic oil (liquid)**.
- The **smoky vapour escaping the vent** → **Pyrogas (gaseous)**.

This sealed, hot, oxygen-free degradation is **exactly** what happens in an industrial pyrolysis reactor, except the "bread" is biomass, plastic waste, or coal, and the temperature is precisely controlled to maximize the gaseous hydrogen-rich fraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for First-Time Learners:**
> Combustion = fuel + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + heat (exothermic, oxidising).
> Pyrolysis = heat + fuel (no O₂) → Char + Oil + Gas (endothermic, inert).
> Gasification = fuel + limited O₂/steam → syngas (CO + H₂) + heat (partial oxidation).

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Pyrolysis Temperature Window | **300 °C – 900 °C** | Defines the chemical pathway |
| Oxygen Concentration | **< 2 % (vol)** | Ensures non-oxidative regime |
| Heating Rate (Slow) | **0.1 – 1 °C/s** | Favours char production |
| Heating Rate (Fast) | **10 – 200 °C/s** | Favours bio-oil production |
| Heating Rate (Flash) | **> 1000 °C/s** | Favours gas + bio-oil |
| Residence Time (Slow) | **Minutes – Days** | Allows secondary char-forming reactions |
| Residence Time (Fast) | **0.5 – 2 s** | Quenches secondary cracking |
| Universal Gas Constant (R) | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** | Used in kinetic modelling |
| Activation Energy (Eₐ) | **80 – 250 kJ/mol** | First-order global kinetic model |
| Typical Particle Size | **< 3 mm** (fast), **5 – 50 mm** (slow) | Heat/mass transfer control |
| Pressure | **Atmospheric (1 atm)** | Simplest configuration |

> [!TIP]
> The **heating rate (β)** in KTU problems is conventionally defined as:
> $$\beta = \frac{dT}{dt} \;\; \text{(°C/s or K/s)}$$
> This single variable often decides the entire product distribution.

## 1.4 Position of Pyrolysis in the Hydrogen Value Chain

In the broader **Hydrogen Economy**, pyrolysis occupies a **transitional, mid-temperature rung** between mild pre-treatment (drying, torrefaction) and high-temperature reforming/gasification. When the **pyrogas is enriched in H₂ and CO** (often called **pyrolysis syngas**), it can be routed to downstream **Water-Gas Shift (WGS)** reactors to upgrade CO into additional H₂, making pyrolysis a credible **biomass-to-hydrogen (BTH)** pathway.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Yield Ranking (Qualitative, KTU-Standard):**
> Steam Gasification > Air Gasification + WGS > Fast Pyrolysis + Reforming > Slow Pyrolysis (direct H₂ poor)
> Pyrolysis is therefore usually **NOT** the highest H₂-yielding route but excels in **feedstock flexibility** and **char co-production**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS: MECHANISMS, CLASSIFICATION & FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Thermochemical Degradation Mechanism

The pyrolysis of any carbonaceous feedstock proceeds via **three overlapping kinetic regimes** (KTU syllabus uses the **Shafizadeh–Browne global model**):

### Stage 1 — Moisture Release and Pre-heating (T < 150 °C)
- Free and loosely bound water evaporates.
- Endothermic, no chemical bond breaking.
- Energy requirement: ~2.26 MJ/kg of moisture evaporated (latent heat of vaporisation of water).

### Stage 2 — Primary Pyrolysis (T = 200 °C – 600 °C)
- **Depolymerisation** of cellulose/hemicellulose/lignin (in biomass).
- **Cracking** of long-chain hydrocarbons into volatiles + char.
- Major volatile release; this stage dictates the **bio-oil yield**.
- Light gases (CO₂, formic acid, levoglucosan) appear.

### Stage 3 — Secondary Reactions (T > 600 °C)
- **Cracking** of primary tars into light gases (H₂, CH₄, CO).
- **Repolymerisation** of tars into secondary char.
- Steam reforming and Boudouard reactions intensify:
  - C + H₂O ⇌ CO + H₂ (steam gasification)
  - C + CO₂ ⇌ 2 CO (Boudouard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why secondary reactions matter:** In a **slow pyrolysis reactor with long vapour residence time**, tars are forced to contact hot char, where they crack into gases. In a **fast/flash reactor**, vapours are quenched within milliseconds, preserving bio-oil.

## 2.2 Classification of Pyrolysis (KTU-High-Yield Table)

| Pyrolysis Type | Heating Rate (°C/s) | Peak Temp (°C) | Residence Time | Dominant Product | H₂ Yield Trend |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow (Carbonisation)** | 0.1 – 1 | 300 – 500 | Minutes – Days | **Char (≈ 35 %)** | Very Low |
| **Intermediate (Conventional)** | 1 – 10 | 500 – 650 | 5 – 30 s | Char + Oil + Gas (≈ equal) | Low |
| **Fast** | 10 – 200 | 500 – 650 | 0.5 – 2 s | **Bio-oil (≈ 75 %)** | Moderate |
| **Flash (Ultra-fast)** | > 1000 | 800 – 1000 | < 0.5 s | **Gas (≈ 80 %)** | High |
| **Vacuum** | Low | 400 – 500 | 2 – 30 s | Bio-oil (good quality) | Moderate |
| **Catalytic (in-situ)** | 10 – 100 | 400 – 600 | 1 – 5 s | Deoxygenated bio-oil + H₂ | High |
| **Hydro-pyrolysis** | 10 – 100 | 600 – 900 | 1 – 10 s | Light hydrocarbons + H₂ | Very High |
| **Methanol/Pyrolysis** | 10 | 500 – 700 | 1 s | Methylated bio-oil | Moderate |

## 2.3 KTU 2024 — Master Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Biomass} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \, \text{no O}_2} \text{Char} + \text{Oil} + \text{Gas}$ | Global reaction stoichiometry | — |
| 2 | $Y_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{\text{feed,dry}}} \times 100$ | Mass yield of product *i* | % (wt) |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{\text{mol H}_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{kg dry feedstock}}$ | Hydrogen mass yield | mol/kg |
| 4 | $\beta = \frac{dT}{dt}$ | Heating rate | °C/s |
| 5 | $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z \rightarrow \text{C (char)} + \text{CO} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{tar}$ | Elemental balance | — |
| 6 | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius rate constant | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k (1 - \alpha)^n$ | First-order kinetic (n = 1) | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\alpha = \frac{m_0 - m_t}{m_0 - m_\infty}$ | Conversion / extent of pyrolysis | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q_{\text{pyrolysis}} = m_{\text{feed}} \cdot c_{p,\text{feed}} \cdot \Delta T + m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot L_v$ | Sensible + latent heat | J or kJ |
| 10 | $\text{HHV}_{\text{bio-oil}} = 0.3491 \cdot \text{C} + 1.1783 \cdot \text{H} + 0.1005 \cdot \text{S} - 0.1034 \cdot \text{O} - 0.0151 \cdot \text{N} - 0.0211 \cdot \text{Ash}$ | Channi's correlation (MJ/kg) | MJ/kg |
| 11 | $\Delta H_{\text{pyrolysis}} \approx +200 \text{ to } +500 \text{ kJ/kg}_{\text{feed}}$ | Net enthalpy change | kJ/kg |
| 12 | $\text{ER} = \frac{\text{Actual O}_2}{\text{Stoichiometric O}_2}$ | Equivalence Ratio (for pyrolysis ER < 0.2) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\text{CE} = \frac{\text{HHV}_{\text{products}}}{\text{HHV}_{\text{feed}}}$ | Cold Gas Efficiency | % |
| 14 | $\text{Carbon Balance: } m_{\text{feed}} \cdot x_C = m_{\text{char}} \cdot x_{C,\text{char}} + m_{\text{oil}} \cdot x_{C,\text{oil}} + m_{\text{gas}} \cdot x_{C,\text{gas}}$ | Carbon mass balance | kg C |
| 15 | $\text{Energy Balance: } Q_{\text{in}} + \sum m_i h_i = Q_{\text{out}} + \sum m_j h_j + Q_{\text{loss}}$ | 1st Law for reactor | kW |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Kerala-style pitfall:** Always use **dry feedstock mass** for yield calculations; otherwise moisture evaporation in Stage 1 will be miscounted as "oil" and the mass balance will close at > 100 %.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Pyrolysis Application |
|---|---|
| **Renewable Hydrogen** | Pyrolysis syngas → WGS → H₂ separation (PSA) |
| **Biofuel Industry** | Fast pyrolysis bio-oil for boilers/diesel engines |
| **Agriculture** | Biochar as soil amendment (carbon sequestration) |
| **Plastic Recycling** | Pyrolysis of waste plastics into fuels (py-oil from HDPE, PP) |
| **Tire Waste Management** | Pyrolysis of used tyres → CB (carbon black), oil, gas |
| **Activated Carbon** | Char from slow pyrolysis → AC production |
| **Carbon Capture** | Biochar as a stable, long-term carbon sink |
| **Defence / Aerospace** | Pyrolysis of polymer composites for char yield analysis |

# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL DERIVATIONS AND MASS/ENERGY BALANCE

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Global Mass Balance for a Pyrolysis Reactor

Consider a continuous feed of **F kg/h** of dry biomass entering a perfectly mixed pyrolysis reactor, producing three exit streams:
- Char: $m_c$ kg/h
- Bio-oil (condensable): $m_o$ kg/h
- Pyrogas (non-condensable): $m_g$ kg/h

**Step 1 — Conservation of Total Mass**

$$F = m_c + m_o + m_g$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Product Yields**

$$Y_c = \frac{m_c}{F} \times 100\%$$

$$Y_o = \frac{m_o}{F} \times 100\%$$

$$Y_g = \frac{m_g}{F} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Closure Check**

$$Y_c + Y_o + Y_g = 100\%$$

> This identity is the **first mass-balance verification** examiners expect in KTU numericals.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydrogen Yield from Pyrogas Composition

Pyrogas composition (typical dry basis) is:
- H₂ : 25 vol%
- CO : 35 vol%
- CO₂ : 15 vol%
- CH₄ : 15 vol%
- C₂H₄ : 5 vol%
- C₂H₆ : 3 vol%
- N₂ : 2 vol%

Let total molar flow of dry gas = **n_total = 100 kmol/h** (basis).

**Step 1 — Molar flow of each species**

$$n_{\text{H}_2} = 0.25 \times 100 = 25 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$n_{\text{CO}} = 0.35 \times 100 = 35 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$n_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.15 \times 100 = 15 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Molecular weight of H₂**

$$M_{\text{H}_2} = 2.016 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow of H₂**

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = n_{\text{H}_2} \times M_{\text{H}_2} = 25 \times 2.016 = 50.4 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Hydrogen mass yield (per 1000 kg feed, if pyrogas flow is fixed)**

If feed rate is **1000 kg/h**, then H₂ yield is:

$$\eta_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{m_{\text{H}_2}}{F} = \frac{50.4}{1000} = 0.0504 \text{ kg H}_2 / \text{kg feed}$$

> Converting to **mol/kg**:

$$\eta_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{50.4 / 2.016}{1000} = 25 \text{ mol H}_2 / \text{kg feed}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Balance Across an Indirectly-Heated Pyrolysis Reactor

Assume:
- Feed (dry wood): $F = 100$ kg/h, $c_{p,\text{feed}} = 1.6$ kJ/(kg·K), $T_{\text{in}} = 25$ °C
- Reactor outlet: $T_{\text{out}} = 500$ °C
- Char: $m_c = 30$ kg/h, $c_{p,\text{char}} = 1.2$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Bio-oil: $m_o = 45$ kg/h, $c_{p,\text{oil}} = 2.5$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Pyrogas: $m_g = 25$ kg/h, $c_{p,\text{gas}} = 1.8$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Heat of pyrolysis reaction: $\Delta H = +300$ kJ/kg (endothermic)
- Heat loss: $Q_{\text{loss}} = 0.10 \cdot Q_{\text{in}}$

**Step 1 — Energy required to raise feed to reactor temperature**

$$Q_{\text{feed}} = F \cdot c_{p,\text{feed}} \cdot \Delta T = 100 \times 1.6 \times (500 - 25)$$

$$Q_{\text{feed}} = 100 \times 1.6 \times 475 = 76{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Energy released by products cooling (back to 25 °C)**

For char:
$$Q_{\text{char,out}} = 30 \times 1.2 \times (500 - 25) = 17{,}100 \text{ kJ/h}$$

For bio-oil:
$$Q_{\text{oil,out}} = 45 \times 2.5 \times (500 - 25) = 53{,}437.5 \text{ kJ/h}$$

For pyrogas:
$$Q_{\text{gas,out}} = 25 \times 1.8 \times (500 - 25) = 21{,}375 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Total sensible heat recovered from hot products:
$$Q_{\text{products,total}} = 17{,}100 + 53{,}437.5 + 21{,}375 = 91{,}912.5 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Energy for endothermic pyrolysis reaction**

$$Q_{\text{rxn}} = F \cdot \Delta H = 100 \times 300 = 30{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Applying First Law of Thermodynamics**

$$Q_{\text{in}} + Q_{\text{feed}} = Q_{\text{products,total}} + Q_{\text{rxn}} + Q_{\text{loss}}$$

$$Q_{\text{in}} + 76{,}000 = 91{,}912.5 + 30{,}000 + 0.10 \cdot Q_{\text{in}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Q_in**

$$Q_{\text{in}} - 0.10 \cdot Q_{\text{in}} = 91{,}912.5 + 30{,}000 - 76{,}000$$

$$0.90 \cdot Q_{\text{in}} = 45{,}912.5$$

$$Q_{\text{in}} = 51{,}014 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 14.17 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The hot products carry significant **sensible heat** (≈ 91.9 MJ/h). In a well-designed plant, this is recovered via **heat exchangers** to pre-heat the incoming feedstock and combustion air, raising the overall **Energy Efficiency** to > 80 %.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — First-Order Kinetic Constant from TGA Data

A thermogravimetric analyser (TGA) records mass loss of a biomass sample. Two typical data points:

| Time (s) | Mass (mg) | Temperature (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 10.0 | 25 |
| 200 | 6.0 | 225 |
| 600 | 4.5 | 425 |
| 1200 | 3.8 | 525 |
| ∞ (final) | 3.2 | — |

**Step 1 — Compute extent of conversion α at each point**

$$m_\infty = 3.2 \text{ mg} \quad ; \quad m_0 = 10.0 \text{ mg}$$

At t = 200 s:
$$\alpha_1 = \frac{10.0 - 6.0}{10.0 - 3.2} = \frac{4.0}{6.8} = 0.588$$

At t = 1200 s:
$$\alpha_2 = \frac{10.0 - 3.8}{10.0 - 3.2} = \frac{6.2}{6.8} = 0.912$$

**Step 2 — Apply first-order kinetic equation**

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k (1 - \alpha)$$

Integrating from 0 to t:
$$-\ln(1 - \alpha) = k \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Compute k at each time point**

At t = 200 s:
$$k_1 = \frac{-\ln(1 - 0.588)}{200} = \frac{-\ln(0.412)}{200} = \frac{0.886}{200} = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

At t = 1200 s:
$$k_2 = = \frac{-\ln(1 - 0.912)}{1200} = \frac{-\ln(0.088)}{1200} = \frac{2.430}{1200} = 2.025 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Arrhenius dependence on temperature**

For two temperature points (T₁ = 498 K at t = 200 s, T₂ = 798 K at t = 1200 s):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{4.43 \times 10^{-3}}{2.025 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{498} - \frac{1}{798}\right)$$

$$\ln(2.188) = -\frac{E_a}{8.314}\left(0.002008 - 0.001253\right)$$

$$0.7825 = -\frac{E_a}{8.314} \times 0.000755$$

$$E_a = \frac{0.7825 \times 8.314}{0.000755} \approx 8{,}616 \text{ J/mol} \approx 8.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> This low Eₐ suggests the data lies in the **active pyrolysis zone (200 °C – 500 °C)** where the activation energy is dominated by hemicellulose decomposition (typical Eₐ = 80 – 120 kJ/mol). The simplified 2-point fit is for academic demonstration; in practice, **Kissinger-Akahira-Sunose (KAS)** or **Coats-Redfern (CR)** methods use ≥ 5 points.
