# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define pyrolysis. Why is it considered an oxygen-deficient process rather than a true combustion process?**

**Model Answer:**

Pyrolysis is the thermochemical decomposition of organic feedstocks at elevated temperatures (**300 °C – 900 °C**) in the **absence of oxygen** (or with oxygen concentration < 2 % by volume), producing a solid char, a condensable liquid (bio-oil), and a non-condensable gas (pyrogas) rich in **H₂, CO, CH₄, and CO₂**.

It is *not* a combustion process because:

1. In combustion, fuel reacts **stoichiometrically** with O₂, releasing maximum heat and producing only CO₂ and H₂O as final products.
2. In pyrolysis, the **deliberate absence of O₂** prevents complete oxidation; the decomposition is **endothermic**, sustained by an external heat source, and preserves the **chemical energy** of the feedstock in the form of char, oil, and combustible gas.

> [!NOTE]
> The three 'C's of pyrolysis: **Char**, **Condensate (oil)**, and **Combustible gas**.

---

### Question 2: Operational Parameter Identification `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three operating parameters that influence the product distribution in biomass pyrolysis. How does the heating rate affect bio-oil yield?**

**Model Answer:**

Three key operating parameters are:

1. **Heating rate (β)** — controls whether char, oil, or gas dominates.
2. **Final reaction temperature (T)** — determines secondary cracking.
3. **Vapour residence time (tᵣ)** — long residence promotes gas; short residence preserves oil.

**Effect of heating rate on bio-oil yield:**

- **Slow pyrolysis (β = 0.1 – 1 °C/s):** Bio-oil yield is low (~ 30 wt%) because volatiles have long residence time to undergo secondary cracking into gases and re-polymerise into char.
- **Fast pyrolysis (β = 10 – 200 °C/s):** Bio-oil yield peaks at **70 – 80 wt%** because rapid devolatilisation and immediate quenching (< 1 s) prevent secondary reactions.
- **Flash pyrolysis (β > 1000 °C/s):** Bio-oil yield drops again as vapours are too rapidly cracked into gases, shifting the dominant product to **pyrogas (H₂ + CO)**.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🅰️ QUESTION A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat schematic, explain the working of a fluidised bed fast pyrolysis reactor for biomass-to-hydrogen production. Discuss the effect of (a) temperature, (b) heating rate, and (c) equivalence ratio on the product distribution and hydrogen yield.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Working Principle and Schematic

**Working Principle:**

A bubbling fluidised bed (BFB) fast pyrolysis reactor uses an inert bed material — typically **silica sand (SiO₂)** of particle size **0.2 – 0.5 mm** — fluidised by a carrier gas (**N₂ or recycled pyrogas**). Biomass particles, ground to < 3 mm, are injected near the base of the bed through a screw feeder. The hot sand rapidly transfers heat to the biomass at rates of **10³ – 10⁴ °C/s**, causing flash devolatilisation at **500 – 550 °C** within **0.5 – 2 seconds**.

The primary vapours are immediately swept out of the bed by the fluidising gas (short vapour residence time), pass through a **cyclone** (to remove entrained char), and are quenched in a **condenser train** (typically at < 50 °C) to recover bio-oil. The non-condensable gases (pyrogas) are partially recycled as fluidising gas, with the bleed stream sent to a **WGS + PSA** train for H₂ purification.

**Schematic (text-based):**

```
            N₂ / Recycle Gas (fluidising medium)
                       │
                       ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │       BIOMASS FEEDER  ◄── Biomass       │
   └────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
                    ▼
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │      FLUIDISED BED REACTOR              │
   │   ┌─────────────────────────────────┐   │
   │   │  Sand bed  T = 500 – 650 °C    │   │
   │   │  Heat source (external)         │   │
   │   │  τ_vapour < 2 s                │   │
   │   └─────────────────────────────────┘   │
   └──────┬──────────────────────┬───────────┘
          ▼                      ▼
   ┌──────────────┐    ┌────────────────────┐
   │   CYCLONE    │    │   QUENCH COOLER    │
   │  (char sep.) │    │   T < 50 °C         │
   └──────┬───────┘    └────────┬───────────┘
          ▼                    ▼
   ┌──────────────┐    ┌────────────────────┐
   │  CHAR OUT    │    │    BIO-OIL OUT     │
   └──────────────┘    └────────────────────┘
                          │
                          ▼
                  ┌────────────────┐
                  │  PYROGAS OUT   │  ──► WGS ──► H₂
                  └────────────────┘
```

**Key Design Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Bed temperature | 500 – 650 °C |
| Fluidising velocity | 0.5 – 1.0 m/s |
| Sand particle size | 0.2 – 0.5 mm |
| Biomass feed size | < 3 mm |
| Vapour residence time | 0.5 – 2 s |
| Bio-oil yield | 70 – 80 wt% |
| H₂ in pyrogas | 15 – 25 vol% (dry) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Effect of Operating Parameters on H₂ Yield

**(i) Effect of Temperature:**
At **lower T (400 – 500 °C):** Char and bio-oil dominate; H₂ yield is low (< 5 vol% of pyrogas).
At **moderate T (550 – 650 °C):** Bio-oil yield peaks; H₂ in pyrogas is **15 – 25 vol%**.
At **high T (700 – 900 °C):** Secondary cracking intensifies → more H₂ and CO; H₂ rises to **30 – 40 vol%**, but bio-oil yield drops. **Optimal H₂ + oil zone: 550 – 700 °C.**

**(ii) Effect of Heating Rate:**
- **Slow heating (< 1 °C/s):** Promotes char formation; H₂ yield is **< 5 vol%**.
- **Fast heating (10 – 200 °C/s):** Maximises bio-oil; H₂ in pyrogas ≈ **20 vol%**.
- **Flash heating (> 1000 °C/s):** Maximises gas; H₂ rises to **30 – 50 vol%**, but with high energy penalty.

**(iii) Effect of Equivalence Ratio (ER):**
- **ER < 0.05 (pure pyrolysis):** H₂ = 20 – 30 vol% of pyrogas.
- **ER = 0.10 – 0.20 (mild oxidative pyrolysis):** Temperature rises due to partial combustion, **H₂ increases to 35 – 45 vol%** and tar is cracked, but bio-oil yield collapses.
- **ER > 0.25 (approaches gasification):** Pyrogas dominated by **CO and CO₂**, with H₂/CO ≈ 1; bio-oil essentially absent.

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** For **maximising hydrogen from biomass via pyrolysis**, the optimum is **high temperature (700 – 850 °C) + flash heating + ER ≈ 0.15 – 0.20**, followed by WGS to convert CO to additional H₂.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Derive the mass balance equation for a continuous pyrolysis reactor and calculate the product yields for a biomass feed of 500 kg/h yielding 175 kg/h char, 225 kg/h bio-oil, and 100 kg/h pyrogas. Verify the closure of the mass balance.**
**(b) The pyrogas has the following composition (dry, vol%): H₂ = 30%, CO = 35%, CO₂ = 10%, CH₄ = 15%, C₂H₄ = 5%, N₂ = 5%. Calculate (i) the mass flow rate of H₂ if the total pyrogas flow is 100 kmol/h, and (ii) the hydrogen mass yield per kg of biomass feed.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Mass Balance Derivation and Verification

**Step 1 — Generic Mass Balance for a Steady-State, Continuous Pyrolysis Reactor:**

Under steady-state and assuming no ash accumulation:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = \dot{m}_{\text{char}} + \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} + \dot{m}_{\text{pyrogas}}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Component Yields:**

$$Y_{\text{char}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{char}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$$

$$Y_{\text{oil}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{oil}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$$

$$Y_{\text{gas}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{pyrogas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Closure Identity:**

$$Y_{\text{char}} + Y_{\text{oil}} + Y_{\text{gas}} = 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Plug in Given Values:**

$$Y_{\text{char}} = \frac{175}{500} \times 100\% = 35\%$$

$$Y_{\text{oil}} = \frac{225}{500} \times 100\% = 45\%$$

$$Y_{\text{gas}} = \frac{100}{500} \times 100\% = 20\%$$

**Step 5 — Verification of Mass Balance:**

$$Y_{\text{char}} + Y_{\text{oil}} + Y_{\text{gas}} = 35\% + 45\% + 20\% = 100\% \;\;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The mass balance is **perfectly closed**, confirming the experimental data is internally consistent.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Hydrogen Mass Flow and Yield

**Step 1 — Molar Flow of H₂ in Pyrogas:**

$$n_{\text{H}_2} = 0.30 \times 100 = 30 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Molecular Weight of H₂:**

$$M_{\text{H}_2} = 2 \times 1.008 = 2.016 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Flow of H₂:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = n_{\text{H}_2} \times M_{\text{H}_2} = 30 \times 2.016 = 60.48 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Hydrogen Mass Yield per kg of Biomass Feed:**

$$\eta_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}} = \frac{60.48}{500} = 0.121 \text{ kg H}_2 / \text{kg feed}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mol/kg for Cross-Check:**

$$\eta_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{0.121 \text{ kg H}_2 / \text{kg}}{2.016 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 0.0600 \text{ kmol H}_2 / \text{kg feed} = 60 \text{ mol H}_2 / \text{kg feed}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Benchmark:** A hydrogen yield of **60 mol H₂ / kg biomass** is in the **lower-middle range** for biomass pyrolysis. With downstream WGS of the CO (35 kmol/h × 1 = 35 kmol/h H₂ added), the **total theoretical H₂ recovery rises to 65 kmol/h (130.8 kg/h)** — almost **double** the pyrolysis-only yield.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (2024 Scheme Pattern):**
> 1. **Conflating pyrolysis with gasification:** Pyrolysis ER < 0.2 (no oxidant); gasification ER = 0.2 – 0.4 (limited O₂). Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for any confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting dry-basis correction:** Moisture in biomass (5 – 50 wt%) must be subtracted before yield calculations. Reporting yield on "as-received" basis = **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Misquoting the heating rate window:** Fast pyrolysis is **10 – 200 °C/s**, NOT "very high temperature" alone. Temperature and heating rate are **independent** parameters.
> 4. **Mass balance non-closure:** Failing to verify that Y_char + Y_oil + Y_gas = 100% loses **0.5 – 1 mark** for "lack of verification."
> 5. **Wrong units in H₂ yield:** Expressing as kg/kg is correct; expressing as "percentage" without context is incomplete. Always state units explicitly.
> 6. **Ignoring carbon balance:** In the 14-mark question, writing only mass balance without carbon balance is considered an **incomplete answer (loses 2 marks)**.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Pyrolysis = Heat + No O₂ → Char + Oil + Gas.** Remember the bread-in-a-sealed-container analogy.
- The **three product fractions** are: **Char (solid), Bio-oil (liquid), Pyrogas (gas).**
- The **three kinetic stages** are: **(1) Drying (< 150 °C), (2) Primary Pyrolysis (200 – 600 °C), (3) Secondary Cracking (> 600 °C).**
- **Heating rate** is the **master knob**: slow → char, fast → oil, flash → gas.
- **Operating window:** 300 – 900 °C, O₂ < 2 vol%, atmospheric pressure.
- **ER < 0.2** distinguishes pyrolysis from gasification (ER = 0.2 – 0.4).
- **Bio-oil yield peaks at 70 – 80 wt%** under fast pyrolysis conditions (β = 10 – 200 °C/s, T = 500 – 550 °C, τ < 2 s).
- **H₂ yield from pure pyrolysis is moderate** (15 – 30 vol% of pyrogas); **WGS** is required to upgrade to high-purity H₂.
- **Fluidised bed reactors** are the industrial workhorse for fast pyrolysis; **rotary kilns** dominate slow pyrolysis.
- **Mass balance closure** is mandatory in all KTU numericals: Σ Y = 100%.
- **Arrhenius kinetics** govern the global rate: $k = A e^{-E_a / RT}$, with typical $E_a = 80 - 250$ kJ/mol.
- **Carbon balance** and **energy balance** are the two key verification tools for reactor design problems.
- **Catalytic pyrolysis** (HZSM-5, Ni-Mo, dolomite) enhances deoxygenation and raises H₂ yield.
- **Hydro-pyrolysis** (H₂ atmosphere) gives the highest direct H₂ yield (40 – 60 mol H₂ / kg feed).
- **Pyrolysis is endothermic** (ΔH = +200 to +500 kJ/kg), requiring external heat input — except in autothermal variants using char combustion.
- **Biochar co-product** provides an economic offset (soil amendment, activated carbon, carbon credits).
- **PSA** is the standard H₂ purification technology post-WGS, achieving **99.99 % purity** for fuel-cell-grade H₂.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical and Chemical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical and Chemical Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course OECHT616), **Physical Properties** of hydrogen are defined as the intrinsic measurable characteristics that do **not** alter the chemical identity of the element — these include its state, density, boiling point, melting point, viscosity, diffusivity, and thermal conductivity. **Chemical Properties**, in contrast, are those attributes that describe how hydrogen interacts with other substances, dictating its reactivity, combustion behavior, oxidation states, and thermodynamic stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** A working knowledge of these properties is a **prerequisite** for selecting storage methods (compressed gas, liquid, metal hydrides) and designing safe handling protocols. Hydrogen has the highest energy-to-mass ratio of any fuel, but the *lowest* energy-to-volume ratio — this paradox governs all engineering design choices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cosmic Chameleon" Analogy:** Imagine hydrogen as the universe's smallest, lightest, and most "shy" atom. Physically, it behaves like an invisible, odorless gas that escapes almost any container — much like helium from a party balloon, but even more eager to escape because it is **14 times lighter than air**. Chemically, however, it is incredibly "sociable" — it readily marries with oxygen in a fierce, exothermic bond to release massive energy. This duality (a tiny invisible molecule with explosive sociability) is the central challenge of the hydrogen economy.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants of Hydrogen

The following constants must be memorized for the KTU examination:

- **Atomic mass:** **1.008 g/mol** (lightest element in the periodic table)
- **Molecular formula:** **H₂** (diatomic under standard conditions)
- **Bond dissociation energy (H–H):** **436 kJ/mol**
- **Density at STP (0 °C, 1 atm):** **0.0899 kg/m³**
- **Boiling point at 1 atm:** **20.27 K (–252.87 °C)**
- **Melting point at 1 atm:** **13.99 K (–259.16 °C)**
- **Critical temperature (T_c):** **33.19 K**
- **Critical pressure (P_c):** **1.296 MPa**

### 1.4 Fundamental Chemical Properties

- **Oxidation state:** **+1, –1, and 0** (the only element exhibiting all three)
- **Electronegativity (Pauling):** **2.20**
- **Flammability range in air:** **4 % to 75 % by volume**
- **Detonation range in air:** **18 % to 59 % by volume**
- **Auto-ignition temperature:** **858 K (585 °C)**
- **Minimum ignition energy:** **0.02 mJ** (about **10× lower than gasoline**)
- **Flame speed:** **2.7 m/s** (about **8× faster than gasoline**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Breakdown of Physical Properties

**1. State and Appearance**
Hydrogen is a colorless, odorless, tasteless diatomic gas (H₂) at standard temperature and pressure. Its molecule is non-polar with a bond length of **74 pm**.

**2. Density Behavior**
- At STP: **0.0899 kg/m³** (≈ 1/14 the density of air).
- Density decreases linearly with temperature (ideal gas behavior: $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$).
- As a cryogenic liquid (LH₂): **70.8 kg/m³** at 20.27 K.

**3. Thermal Behavior**
- Has the **highest thermal conductivity** of any gas (**0.182 W/m·K** at 300 K) — about **7× that of air**. This is critical for fuel-cell heat management.
- Heat capacity (C_p) at 300 K: **14.31 kJ/(kmol·K)**.

**4. Transport Properties**
- **Diffusion coefficient in air:** $6.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s — extremely high, leading to rapid dispersion and dilution (a safety advantage in open environments).
- **Viscosity:** $8.76 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa·s at 300 K (lower than air, hence prone to leak through seals).

**5. Joule-Thomson Inversion**
- The Joule-Thomson coefficient at 300 K is negative, meaning hydrogen **heats upon expansion** — a critical safety consideration in high-pressure tank rupture scenarios.

### 2.2 Structured Breakdown of Chemical Properties

**1. Combustion Stoichiometry**

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad \Delta H = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol (HHV)}$$

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)} \quad \Delta H = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol (LHV)}$$

**2. Reactivity Series**
- **With halogens:** Forms hydrogen halides (e.g., $H_2 + Cl_2 \rightarrow 2HCl$).
- **With nitrogen:** Haber process: $3H_2 + N_2 \rightarrow 2NH_3$ (high P, Fe catalyst).
- **With carbon monoxide:** Reverse water-gas shift: $H_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CO + H_2O$.

**3. Isotopic Variants**
- **Protium (¹H):** 99.985 % natural abundance — nucleus = 1 proton.
- **Deuterium (²H or D):** 0.015 % abundance — used as heavy-water moderator in nuclear reactors.
- **Tritium (³H or T):** radioactive (half-life 12.3 yr), used in fusion research and self-illuminating paints.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|----------|---------------------|---------------|------|
| 1 | Density at STP | $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ | 0.0899 | kg/m³ |
| 2 | Specific LHV (mass basis) | $\dfrac{\Delta H_{LHV}}{M_{H_2}}$ | 120.0 | MJ/kg |
| 3 | Specific HHV (mass basis) | $\dfrac{\Delta H_{HHV}}{M_{H_2}}$ | 141.8 | MJ/kg |
| 4 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $\dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}}$ | 34.3 | kg air / kg H₂ |
| 5 | Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) | $\phi_{LFL}$ in air | 4.0 | vol % |
| 6 | Upper Flammability Limit (UFL) | $\phi_{UFL}$ in air | 75.0 | vol % |
| 7 | Auto-ignition Temperature | $T_{ign}$ | 858 | K |
| 8 | Diffusivity in air | $D_{H_2,air}$ | $6.1 \times 10^{-5}$ | m²/s |
| 9 | Critical Temperature | $T_c$ | 33.19 | K |
| 10 | Critical Pressure | $P_c$ | 1.296 | MPa |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The high diffusivity explains why **ventilated hydrogen rooms** are mandatory in fuel-cell installations.
- The wide flammability range (4–75 %) and low ignition energy explain the **explosion risk** in confined spaces — every leak must be detected by sensors calibrated to 1 % by volume (i.e., one-fourth of LFL).
- The negative Joule-Thomson coefficient means **hydrogen pipelines can self-heat** during rapid depressurization events, demanding specialized pressure-relief design.
- The very low density forces engineers toward **either high-pressure compression (350–700 bar)** or **cryogenic liquefaction (20.3 K)** for any practical volumetric energy storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Energy (Mass Basis) of Hydrogen

The molar mass of H₂ is the sum of two atomic masses:

$$M_{H_2} = 2 \times 1.008 = 2.016 \text{ g/mol} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$$

The LHV per mole of hydrogen (combustion yielding water vapor) is the **absolute value** of the enthalpy change:

$$\vert \Delta H_{LHV} \vert = 241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The specific energy (energy per unit mass) is obtained by dividing molar energy by molar mass:

$$\text{Specific LHV} = \frac{241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}}$$

$$\text{Specific LHV} = \frac{241.8}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\text{Specific LHV} = 119{,}940 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 120.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

Similarly, for the Higher Heating Value (water condensed to liquid):

$$\text{Specific HHV} = \frac{285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}}$$

$$\text{Specific HHV} = \frac{285.8}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\text{Specific HHV} \approx 141.8 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At **120 MJ/kg**, hydrogen carries roughly **2.5× more energy per kg than gasoline** (~46 MJ/kg). However, gasoline occupies only ~0.75 L/kg whereas gaseous H₂ at 200 bar occupies ~6 L/kg — illustrating the *low volumetric energy density* problem.

### 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio by Mass

The stoichiometric combustion equation is:

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

**Step 1 — Moles of oxygen needed per mole of H₂:**

$$n_{O_2} = \frac{1}{2} \text{ mol per mol H}_2$$

**Step 2 — Moles of air needed (air = 21 % O₂ by volume):**

$$n_{air} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{0.5}{0.21} = 2.381 \text{ mol per mol H}_2$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass.** Average molar mass of air is 28.97 g/mol; molar mass of H₂ is 2.016 g/mol:

$$m_{air} = 2.381 \times 28.97 = 68.98 \text{ g per mol H}_2$$

$$m_{H_2} = 1 \times 2.016 = 2.016 \text{ g per mol H}_2$$

**Step 4 — Mass ratio (AFR_stoich):**

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{68.98}{2.016} \approx 34.2 \text{ kg air / kg H}_2$$

This is the canonical value **34.3 : 1** commonly tabulated for hydrogen.

### 3.3 Derivation: Specific Volume at NTP for Storage Calculations

Using the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$, the specific volume (per unit mass) at NTP (T = 293.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa) is:

$$v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{RT}{PM} = \frac{R_s T}{P}$$

where the specific gas constant for hydrogen is:

$$R_s = \frac{\bar{R}}{M_{H_2}} = \frac{8.314}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} = 4124 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$$

$$v = \frac{4124 \times 293.15}{101{,}325} \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$v = \frac{1{,}208{,}917}{101{,}325} \approx 11.93 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

So **1 kg of hydrogen occupies nearly 12 m³** at ambient conditions — the principal motivation for compression or liquefaction.

### 3.4 Derivation: Compressed-Hydrogen Tank Sizing

For a cylinder at pressure P delivering 1 kg of H₂ from T₁ to T₂, the volume required is:

$$V = \frac{mRT}{PM_{H_2}}$$

Let us size a 700-bar (70 MPa) tank delivering 5 kg of H₂ at 293 K:

$$V = \frac{5 \times 4124 \times 293}{70 \times 10^6}$$

$$V = \frac{6{,}041{,}660}{7 \times 10^7} \text{ m}^3$$

$$V = 0.0863 \text{ m}^3 \approx 86.3 \text{ L}$$

This justifies the **large physical volume** of Type IV 700-bar hydrogen tanks in modern FCEVs.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Hydrogen with Conventional Fuels

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Methane (CH₄) | Gasoline (C₈H₁₈) | Methanol (CH₃OH) |
|----------|--------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| Molecular weight (g/mol) | 2.016 | 16.04 | 114.23 | 32.04 |
| Density at STP (kg/m³) | 0.0899 | 0.717 | 740 (liquid) | 792 (liquid) |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 120.0 | 50.0 | 43.4 | 20.1 |
| LFL in air (vol %) | 4.0 | 5.0 | 1.4 | 6.7 |
| Auto-ignition (K) | 858 | 813 | 530 | 737 |
| Min. ignition energy (mJ) | 0.02 | 0.28 | 0.24 | 0.14 |
| Flame speed (m/s) | 2.7 | 0.4 | 0.4 | 0.5 |
| Stoichiometric AFR (mass) | 34.3 | 17.2 | 14.7 | 6.4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Hydrogen Properties

```mermaid
    B -->|High Diffusivity 6.1e-5 m2/s| F[Decision: Ventilate Enclosed Spaces]

    C --> I[Outcome: 700 bar Type IV Tank or 20 K LH2 Tank]

```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Combustion-Property Chain

```mermaid

    nodeA[Input: 1 mol H2 plus 0.5 mol O2] --> nodeB[Activation: Ignition Energy 0.02 mJ]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Reaction Zone: Flame Speed 2.7 m/s]

    nodeD --> nodeE[Energy Release: 241.8 kJ or 285.8 kJ]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Specific Energy: 120 or 141.8 MJ/kg]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Engineering Output: Storage and Utilization System]

    nodeG --> nodeH[Branch 1: PEM Fuel Cell 50 percent efficient]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Branch 2: Internal Combustion Engine 30 percent efficient]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Branch 3: Gas Turbine 40 percent efficient]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three physical properties of hydrogen that make it difficult to store as a fuel, and state their numerical values.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Very low gas density (0.0899 kg/m³)** — requires compression to 350–700 bar or liquefaction at 20.3 K to obtain usable volumetric energy.
2. **Extremely low boiling point (20.27 K)** — liquefaction consumes ~30–40 % of the fuel's LHV, reducing net round-trip efficiency.
3. **High diffusivity (6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s in air)** — hydrogen escapes easily through seals and micro-leaks, raising long-term storage loss.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) and Upper Flammability Limit (UFL) of hydrogen in air. Why is hydrogen considered more hazardous than gasoline despite its narrow use in transport?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **LFL** is the minimum volumetric concentration of fuel in an oxidizer below which combustion cannot propagate. For H₂, **LFL = 4 %**.
- **UFL** is the maximum concentration above which combustion is again not possible due to insufficient oxidizer. For H₂, **UFL = 75 %**.
- Hydrogen is more hazardous because its **flammability range (4–75 %) is far wider than gasoline (1.4–7.6 %)** and its **minimum ignition energy (0.02 mJ)** is an order of magnitude lower — meaning almost any electrostatic spark can ignite it.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio by mass for the complete combustion of hydrogen in air. State all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 5 kg compressed-hydrogen tank is stored at 700 bar and 298 K. Calculate (i) the volume of the tank, (ii) the chemical energy stored assuming 100 % conversion of LHV, and (iii) the equivalent energy in liters of gasoline (LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg, density 740 kg/m³). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Stoichiometric AFR derivation (7 marks):**

Write the balanced equation:

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

**Assumption 1:** Air composition is 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume (mole basis).

**Assumption 2:** Combustion is complete and stoichiometric (no excess O₂).

Moles of O₂ per mole of H₂:

$$n_{O_2} = 0.5 \text{ mol}$$

Moles of air needed:

$$n_{air} = \frac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{0.5}{0.21} = 2.381 \text{ mol}$$

Mass of air per mole of H₂:

$$m_{air} = 2.381 \times 28.97 = 68.98 \text{ g}$$

Mass of H₂ per mole:

$$m_{H_2} = 1 \times 2.016 = 2.016 \text{ g}$$

Stoichiometric AFR by mass:

$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{68.98}{2.016} = 34.22 \approx 34.3 \text{ kg air/kg H}_2$$

**Part (b) — Tank calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Tank volume.** Using ideal gas law with $R_s$ = 4124 J/(kg·K), P = 700 × 10⁵ Pa, T = 298 K, m = 5 kg:

$$V = \frac{m R_s T}{P} = \frac{5 \times 4124 \times 298}{700 \times 10^5}$$

$$V = \frac{6{,}144{,}760}{7 \times 10^7} = 0.0878 \text{ m}^3 \approx 87.8 \text{ L}$$

**(ii) Chemical energy stored (LHV basis):**

$$E = m \times \text{Specific LHV} = 5 \text{ kg} \times 120 \text{ MJ/kg} = 600 \text{ MJ}$$

**(iii) Equivalent gasoline volume:**

$$m_{gasoline} = \frac{E}{LHV_{gas}} = \frac{600}{44} = 13.64 \text{ kg}$$

$$V_{gasoline} = \frac{13.64}{740} = 0.01843 \text{ m}^3 = 18.43 \text{ L}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> - Students frequently use the **universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K)** but forget to divide by the molar mass of H₂ (2.016 g/mol) when computing the volume per *kilogram*. Using the specific gas constant **R_s = 4124 J/(kg·K)** is mandatory. **Loss of 2 marks.**
> - Many candidates forget to specify the **HHV vs LHV** distinction. Internal combustion engines must use **LHV** because exhaust water leaves as vapor. Fuel cells may use HHV. **Loss of 1 mark.**
> - Omitting the **temperature assumption (298 K)** in part (b) is a common deduction of 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the Joule-Thomson effect for hydrogen. Why does it behave oppositely to most gases at room temperature, and what is the practical safety implication for high-pressure hydrogen storage? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare hydrogen with methane (CH₄) on the basis of (i) energy density (mass and volume basis at STP), (ii) flammability range, and (iii) ignition energy. State which fuel is more hazardous in a confined garage and justify your answer with numerical reasoning. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Joule-Thomson effect (7 marks):**

The **Joule-Thomson coefficient** $\mu_{JT}$ describes the temperature change of a real gas during an isenthalpic (constant-H) expansion:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_H$$

For most gases at room temperature, $\mu_{JT} > 0$ — the gas **cools** on expansion. However, hydrogen has a **negative Joule-Thomson coefficient** at 300 K because its inversion temperature is only ~200 K.

**Physical explanation:** Because H₂ molecules repel each other at short range (small, low-electron-cloud atoms with negligible polarizability), expansion work decreases potential energy, converting into kinetic energy → temperature **rises**.

**Practical safety implication:** During a high-pressure tank rupture or rapid valve opening at room temperature, hydrogen gas **self-heats**. This heating can ignite leaking hydrogen or damage composite tank liners. Therefore, **slow-acting pressure-relief valves** and **thermal barriers** are mandated in 700-bar Type IV tanks. Conversely, pre-cooling the gas below its inversion temperature before expansion is required to achieve cryogenic storage.

**Part (b) — Hydrogen vs Methane comparison (7 marks):**

**(i) Energy density:**

| Basis | Hydrogen | Methane |
|-------|----------|---------|
| Mass (LHV) | 120 MJ/kg | 50 MJ/kg |
| Volume (STP) | 10.8 MJ/m³ | 35.8 MJ/m³ |

Methane has **~3.3× higher volumetric energy density** at STP, while hydrogen has **~2.4× higher gravimetric** energy density.

**(ii) Flammability range:**

- H₂: **4 % – 75 %** (range = 71 %)
- CH₄: **5 % – 15 %** (range = 10 %)

Hydrogen has a **7× wider flammable envelope**.

**(iii) Minimum ignition energy:**

- H₂: **0.02 mJ**
- CH₄: **0.28 mJ**

Hydrogen ignites with **~14× less energy**.

**Conclusion on hazard in confined garage:** Hydrogen is significantly more hazardous because (1) almost any small spark ignites it, (2) leaks anywhere in the wide 4–75 % range create an explosive atmosphere, and (3) its high diffusivity spreads the cloud rapidly. Although methane has narrower limits, the cumulative ignition sensitivity makes **hydrogen the more dangerous fuel in confined spaces** — justifying NFPA 2 and ISO 19880 sensor set-points at **1 % by volume (¼ LFL)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - In part (a), students often write that hydrogen "cools on expansion like all gases." This is wrong and costs **3 marks** outright. Always state the inversion temperature (~200 K) and the **negative sign** of $\mu_{JT}$ at room temperature.
> - In part (b), comparing only gravimetric density without volumetric density is a **2-mark deduction**.
> - Forgetting to convert MJ/kg into MJ/m³ using the STP density is a frequent mistake. **Loss of 1 mark.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Physical & Chemical Properties**

- **Atomic mass of H₂:** 2.016 g/mol. **Bond energy (H–H):** 436 kJ/mol.
- **LHV of H₂:** 241.8 kJ/mol → **120 MJ/kg**. **HHV of H₂:** 285.8 kJ/mol → **141.8 MJ/kg**.
- **Specific volume at STP:** ~11.93 m³/kg — drives compression/liquefaction necessity.
- **Stoichiometric AFR:** 34.3 kg air/kg H₂.
- **Flammability limits:** 4–75 % in air; **detonation range:** 18–59 %.
- **Auto-ignition temperature:** 858 K; **minimum ignition energy:** 0.02 mJ (lowest of common fuels).
- **Diffusivity in air:** 6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s — high → fast dispersion; safety advantage outdoors, hazard indoors.
- **Thermal conductivity:** 0.182 W/m·K at 300 K — high → fuel-cell heat-management challenge.
- **Joule-Thomson:** **negative at 300 K** (inversion T ≈ 200 K) → gas heats on expansion.
- **Critical point:** T_c = 33.19 K, P_c = 1.296 MPa — defines the cryogenic liquid envelope.
- **Isotopes:** ¹H (protium, 99.985 %), ²H (deuterium, 0.015 %), ³H (tritium, radioactive).
- **Engineering rule-of-thumb sensors:** set alarms at **¼ LFL = 1 % by volume** in air.
- **Standard energy comparison vs gasoline:** H₂ has **2.6× higher gravimetric energy** but **~3000× lower volumetric energy** at STP — motivation for 700-bar compression or 20 K liquefaction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Storage Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** General Storage Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Storage (KTU 2024 Definition):** Hydrogen storage refers to the containment of molecular hydrogen (H₂) in a usable form, either as a gas, liquid, or bonded to a solid/liquid matrix, such that it can be reliably retrieved at a later time with acceptable energy efficiency, safety margins, and economic cost.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, **General Storage Methods** encompasses the principal physical and material-based techniques used to overcome the intrinsic challenge of hydrogen's extremely low ambient density (**0.0899 kg/m³ at STP**), which is roughly **14× lighter than air** and **8× lighter than natural gas (methane)**.

### 1.2 Classification of Storage Methods

The KTU syllabus categorizes hydrogen storage into **four primary classes**:

1. **Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)** – high-pressure gas in tanks (350–700 bar).
2. **Cryo-Compressed / Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** – liquefaction at **−252.87 °C (20.28 K)**.
3. **Solid-State Storage** – metal hydrides, complex hydrides, and chemical hydrogen carriers.
4. **Underground & Large-Scale Storage** – salt caverns, depleted reservoirs, and aquifers.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower Principle"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of hydrogen storage the way a city stores its drinking water. The water utility cannot pipe enough water at the moment of demand, so it pumps it uphill into a **water tower (compressed gas)**, freezes it into **ice blocks (liquid hydrogen)**, chemically binds it to a **sponge (metal hydrides)**, or buries it in a **giant underground cistern (salt cavern)**. Each method trades off **energy cost, time, safety, and density** differently — there is no single "best" storage mode, only the *optimum for a given application*.

### 1.4 Why Hydrogen Storage is Uniquely Challenging

| Property | Hydrogen | Comparison |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular diameter | **0.289 nm** | Smallest molecule — leaks through seals |
| Boiling point | **20.28 K** | Requires extreme cryogenics |
| Flammability range | **4–75 vol% in air** | Very wide → high explosion risk |
| Diffusion coefficient | Highest of all gases | Rapid permeation through metals |
| Energy by mass (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** | 3× gasoline |
| Energy by volume (STP) | **10.8 MJ/m³** | 1/3000 of gasoline |

> [!WARNING]
> **Hydrogen Embrittlement:** Because the H₂ molecule is so small, it diffuses into the steel lattice and **decoheses the metal grains**, causing catastrophic vessel failure. This single phenomenon dictates nearly every material choice in storage engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Storage Method Selection

Every storage method is evaluated on these four engineering metrics:

- **Gravimetric Density (wt%):** mass of H₂ stored ÷ total system mass.
- **Volumetric Density (kg H₂/m³):** mass of H₂ stored ÷ system volume.
- **Thermodynamic Round-Trip Efficiency (%):** energy recovered ÷ energy input.
- **Capital Cost ($/kg H₂ stored).**

### 2.2 Method 1 — Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)

This is the **dominant near-term technology** for fuel-cell vehicles and stationary buffers.

**Operating Principle:** Hydrogen is mechanically compressed by a multi-stage diaphragm or piston compressor and stored in Type III (metal liner + carbon-fiber wrap) or Type IV (polymer liner + carbon-fiber wrap) composite pressure vessels.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Type IV tanks operate at **700 bar (≈10,000 psi)** and reach a volumetric density of approximately **40 kg H₂/m³** — about **3,700× the STP density**.

### 2.3 Method 2 — Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)

Hydrogen is cooled below its critical temperature (**33.19 K**) and condensed to a cryogenic liquid with a density of **70.8 kg/m³**.

**The Boil-Off Problem:** Even in a perfectly insulated tank, heat ingress causes continuous vaporization. Typical boil-off rates (BOR) are **0.1–0.3 % per day** for stationary dewars, but can exceed **1–2 % per day** for vehicle tanks due to vibrational convection.

### 2.4 Method 3 — Solid-State Storage

Hydrogen is **chemisorbed or physisorbed** into a host material:

- **Physisorption (weak van der Waals):** Activated carbon, MOFs (Metal-Organic Frameworks), CNTs.
- **Chemisorption (strong covalent/ionic):** Metal hydrides (LaNi₅, FeTi), complex hydrides (NaAlH₄, LiBH₄), and chemical hydrides (NH₃, LOHC — Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the distinction between **reversible** (metal hydrides, MOFs) and **non-reversible off-board regeneration** (chemical hydrides like NaBH₄).

### 2.5 Method 4 — Underground Storage

For grid-scale (GWh–TWh) buffering, hydrogen is injected into:

- **Salt caverns** (most common, leak-tight, used in Texas/Germany).
- **Depleted oil/gas reservoirs.**
- **Aquifers** (sandstone, capped by impermeable shale).

This method repurposes existing natural gas infrastructure with minor retrofits.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Module 3)**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Pressure (compression) | $PV = nRT$ | Pa, m³, mol, J/mol·K | Used to find final P after adiabatic compression |
| 2 | Compression Work (isothermal, ideal) | $W_{iso} = nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | J | Minimum theoretical work |
| 3 | Compression Work (polytropic) | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{nRT}{\gamma - 1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | J | $\gamma$ = 1.405 for H₂ |
| 4 | Liquefaction Energy (theoretical min.) | $W_{min} = T_0 \Delta S - \Delta H$ | J | Carnot-refrigeration bound |
| 5 | Boil-Off Rate | $BOR = \dfrac{m_{vap}}{m_{stored}} \times 100\%$ | %/day | Critical for LH₂ logistics |
| 6 | Gravimetric Density | $\rho_g = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{system}} \times 100\%$ | wt% | DOE 2025 target: **5.5 wt%** |
| 7 | Volumetric Density | $\rho_v = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{V_{system}}$ | kg H₂/m³ | DOE 2025 target: **40 kg/m³** |
| 8 | Hydride Decomposition (Van't Hoff) | $\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ | Pa, J/mol | Plots as straight line in $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ |
| 9 | Energy Density by Mass (LHV) | $E_m = 120.0$ | MJ/kg | Lower Heating Value of H₂ |
| 10 | Energy Density by Volume (STP) | $E_v = 10.8$ | MJ/m³ | At 0 °C, 101.325 kPa |
| 11 | Compressor Outlet Temp. (adiabatic) | $T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | K | Verifies multi-stage cooling needs |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo):** 700 bar Type IV tanks.
- **Aircraft (Airbus ZEROe concepts):** LH₂ at cryogenic conditions.
- **Grid Buffering (HyDeploy UK, GET H₂ Germany):** Salt caverns at 100–200 bar.
- **Portable Power (Drones, Submarines):** Metal hydride cartridges for low-pressure safety.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Minimum Compression Work

The **minimum reversible work** to compress hydrogen isothermally from pressure $P_1$ to $P_2$ at temperature $T$ is found by integrating the work done against the gas.

**Step 1:** Start with the first law for a closed, isothermal system. Since $T$ is constant for an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$, and all heat added equals the work done.

$$Q = W_{iso}$$

**Step 2:** For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, use the identity $PV = nRT$ (per mole, $Pv = RT$).

Differential work for a small volume change:
$$dW = P \, dv$$

**Step 3:** Express $P$ in terms of $v$ using the ideal gas law:
$$P = \dfrac{RT}{v}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the work integral from initial specific volume $v_1$ to final specific volume $v_2$:
$$W_{iso} = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \dfrac{RT}{v} \, dv$$

**Step 5:** Since $T$ is constant in an isothermal process, factor it out and integrate:
$$W_{iso} = RT \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \dfrac{dv}{v} = RT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Use the ideal gas relation at constant $T$ to swap volume for pressure: $P_1 v_1 = P_2 v_2$, so $\dfrac{v_2}{v_1} = \dfrac{P_1}{P_2}$.

$$W_{iso} = nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Equation Step 6 is the result most useful to engineers because the storage tank's pressure rating is the **design variable**, not the volume. Notice the logarithm — doubling the pressure costs an *equal increment* of work as the *next* doubling. This is why going from 350 → 700 bar (just 2×) is enormously more expensive than 1 → 2 bar.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polytropic Compression Work (Real Compressors)

Real compressors follow a **polytropic process** $PV^k = \text{constant}$ with $1 < k < \gamma$ (typically $k \approx 1.25$–$1.35$ for hydrogen, because of the high specific-heat ratio and internal heat transfer).

**Step 1:** Write the polytropic equation in differential form.
$$P v^k = C \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{1/k}$$

**Step 2:** Use the general formula for boundary work in a closed system.
$$W = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} P \, dv$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $P = C v^{-k}$ into the integral.
$$W = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} C v^{-k} \, dv = C \left[ \dfrac{v^{1-k}}{1-k} \right]_{v_1}^{v_2}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate at the limits. Note that $C = P_1 v_1^k = P_2 v_2^k$.
$$W = \dfrac{P_2 v_2 - P_1 v_1}{1 - k}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to a form using initial pressure and the pressure ratio. Using $P v = ZRT$ and $v_2 = v_1 (P_1/P_2)^{1/k}$:
$$W_{poly} = \dfrac{ZRT}{k-1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1\right]$$

For ideal gas ($Z = 1$):
$$W_{poly} = \dfrac{nRT}{k - 1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1\right] \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** In Step 5, the exponent $\frac{k-1}{k}$ is *smaller* than $(\gamma - 1)/\gamma$, so the polytropic work is **less** than the adiabatic work at the same pressure ratio. This is because real compressors dump some heat to the surroundings, lowering the final temperature below the adiabatic peak.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boil-Off Rate of a Cryogenic LH₂ Tank

Heat ingress $Q$ through the dewar walls causes mass loss $m_{vap}$. For a spherical dewar of radius $r$ and insulation thermal conductance $UA$ (W/K):

**Step 1:** Heat leakage rate into the dewar.
$$\dot Q = UA \,(T_{amb} - T_{LH_2})$$

**Step 2:** The latent heat of vaporization of hydrogen at 1 atm is $L_v = \mathbf{445.6 \text{ kJ/kg}}$.

**Step 3:** Mass boil-off rate is heat inflow divided by latent heat.
$$\dot m_{vap} = \dfrac{\dot Q}{L_v} = \dfrac{UA \,(T_{amb} - T_{LH_2})}{L_v}$$

**Step 4:** Boil-off ratio per unit time.
$$BOR = \dfrac{\dot m_{vap}}{m_{stored}} = \dfrac{UA \,(T_{amb} - T_{LH_2})}{L_v \, m_{stored}}$$

**Step 5:** Numerical illustration — a 5,000 kg LH₂ truck dewar with $UA = 15$ W/K, ambient $T_{amb} = 300$ K, $T_{LH_2} = 20.28$ K:

$$\dot m_{vap} = \dfrac{15 \times (300 - 20.28)}{445{,}600} = \dfrac{15 \times 279.72}{445{,}600} = 9.42 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$BOR_{daily} = \dfrac{9.42 \times 10^{-3} \times 86{,}400}{5{,}000} = 0.163 \text{ \%/day}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** ~0.16 %/day is consistent with industrial literature. A shipment of 4,000 kg loses **~6.5 kg/day** even in a perfectly designed dewar. This is why LH₂ is uneconomical for long-distance haulage.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Van't Hoff Equation for Metal Hydrides

A metal hydride $\text{MH}_n$ releases H₂ when heated above its equilibrium temperature. The Van't Hoff equation links equilibrium pressure $P_{eq}$ to temperature $T$.

**Step 1:** For the decomposition reaction $\text{MH}_n \rightleftharpoons \text{M} + \tfrac{n}{2}\text{H}_2$, the equilibrium constant is:
$$K_p = P_{H_2}^{n/2}$$

**Step 2:** Standard thermodynamic relation $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_p$.

$$\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln P_{H_2}^{n/2} = -\tfrac{n}{2} RT \ln P_{H_2}$$

**Step 3:** Use Gibbs–Helmholtz: $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$.

$$\Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ = -\tfrac{n}{2} RT \ln P_{H_2}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\ln P_{H_2}$:
$$\ln P_{H_2} = -\dfrac{2 \Delta H^\circ}{n R T} + \dfrac{2 \Delta S^\circ}{n R}$$

**Step 5:** Group constants. The plot of $\ln P_{H_2}$ vs $1/T$ is a straight line:
$$\boxed{\;\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** A negative slope (as plotted) confirms that $\Delta H > 0$ — i.e., desorption is **endothermic** (heat must be added to release H₂). For LaNi₅H₆, $\Delta H \approx 30.8$ kJ/mol H₂, and the plateau pressure reaches 1 bar near **40 °C** — a common near-ambient-temperature hydride.

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | CGH₂ (700 bar) | LH₂ (20 K) | Metal Hydride (LaNi₅) | Salt Cavern |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Volumetric density (kg H₂/m³) | **40** | **70.8** | **80–100** | **5–15** (depends on P) |
| Gravimetric density (wt%) | **5–7 (system)** | **15+ (pure LH₂)** | **1.4 (LaNi₅)** | **N/A (huge mass)** |
| Operating T (°C) | −40 to +60 | **−253** | 20–80 | 30–60 |
| Operating P (bar) | **700** | 1–10 | 1–30 | 100–200 |
| Round-trip efficiency | **90–95 %** | 60–70 % (BOR) | 50–60 % | 95 % |
| Energy to store (kWh/kg) | **2.0–2.5** | **10–13** | 7–10 | 1.5–2.0 |
| Best application | Mobility | Aerospace, bulk transport | Portable, stationary buffer | Grid-scale, seasonal |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Distinguish between physisorption and chemisorption in the context of solid-state hydrogen storage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Physisorption (1 mark):** Hydrogen molecules are bound to the host surface by weak **van der Waals forces** (typical binding energy 4–10 kJ/mol). The process is fully reversible and requires **low temperatures (77 K liquid nitrogen)** to achieve meaningful capacity. Examples: activated carbon, MOFs, CNTs.

- **Chemisorption (1 mark):** Hydrogen atoms are bound to the host via **chemical bonds** (binding energy 50–200 kJ/mol). The H–H bond is dissociated, and H atoms occupy interstitial sites in the metal lattice. Examples: LaNi₅H₆, NaAlH₄, FeTiH₂.

- **Key distinguishing feature (1 mark):** Chemisorption offers **higher volumetric capacity** and operates near ambient temperature, but requires **heat input for desorption** and may suffer from poor kinetics/reversibility.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **What is the "boil-off" problem in liquid hydrogen storage? State two methods to mitigate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Boil-off defined (1 mark):** Continuous vaporization of LH₂ due to heat ingress from the surroundings, quantified as the **Boil-Off Rate (BOR in %/day)**. Even with multilayer insulation (MLI) of thermal conductivity ≤ 0.001 W/m·K, ambient heat leaks in and flashes a fraction of the liquid to gas.

- **Mitigation 1 (1 mark):** **Improved insulation** — vacuum-jacketed dewars with 30–60 layers of aluminized Mylar MLI, plus a radiation shield cooled by the vented boil-off gas (re-liquefaction loop).

- **Mitigation 2 (1 mark):** **Re-liquefaction systems** — small on-board cryocoolers (e.g., Gifford–McMahon or pulse-tube refrigerators) recondense the boil-off gas back to liquid, reducing BOR to <0.01 %/day at the cost of ~2–4 % extra energy.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Module Choice (14 Marks)

> **Question A (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of a 700 bar Type IV compressed hydrogen storage system** for automotive applications. Discuss the materials used and the reason for selecting Type IV over Type I/II/III. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel-cell vehicle has a storage tank of volume $V = 0.12$ m³ filled with hydrogen at $P_1 = 700$ bar and $T = 298$ K. Calculate the **(i)** mass of hydrogen stored, and **(ii)** minimum isothermal work to compress this hydrogen from atmospheric pressure ($P_2 = 1.013$ bar) at the same temperature. Take $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working of 700 bar Type IV Tank (7 marks):**

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** A Type IV tank consists of (i) a **polymer (HDPE) liner** that is impermeable to H₂, (ii) a **carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) overwrap** that bears the structural pressure load, (iii) metal **boss fittings** at the ends with integrated **valves and pressure regulators**, and (iv) a **thermal pressure-relief device (TPRD)** that vents the tank in case of fire.

   ```
   ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
   │      CFRP Overwrap (Load)        │
   │  ┌────────────────────────────┐  │
   │  │   HDPE Liner (Sealant)     │  │
   │  │  ┌──────────────────────┐  │  │
   │  │  │   H₂ @ 700 bar       │  │  │
   │  │  └──────────────────────┘  │  │
   │  └────────────────────────────┘  │
   └──────────────────────────────────┘
       ↑ Bosses, TPRD, Valve Assembly
   ```

2. **Materials Used (2 marks):**
   - **Liner:** High-density polyethylene (HDPE) — chosen for ultra-low H₂ permeability (≈ 10⁻¹⁵ mol·m/m²·s·Pa).
   - **Overwrap:** T700-grade carbon fiber with epoxy matrix — provides 1,500 MPa hoop strength at <2 kg/L density.
   - **Bosses:** 7075-T6 aluminum or 316L stainless steel.

3. **Why Type IV over Types I/II/III (2 marks):**
   - **Type I (all steel):** Too heavy — unsuitable for mobility (gravimetric density <1 wt%).
   - **Type II (steel liner + CFRP hoop wrap):** Still ~50 % heavier than Type IV.
   - **Type III (metal liner + full CFRP wrap):** Susceptible to **hydrogen embrittlement** in the metal liner under cyclic loading.
   - **Type IV (polymer liner + full CFRP):** Eliminates embrittlement risk, achieves the highest gravimetric density (≈ 6–7 wt%) and meets the **DOE 2025 target of 5.5 wt%**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass of hydrogen stored (3 marks):**

$$n = \dfrac{P_1 V}{RT} = \dfrac{700 \times 10^5 \times 0.12}{8.314 \times 298}$$

$$n = \dfrac{8.4 \times 10^6}{2{,}477.6} = 3{,}390.6 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{H_2} = n \times M = 3{,}390.6 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 6.835 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Minimum isothermal compression work (4 marks):**

$$W_{iso} = nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = P_1 V \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

$$W_{iso} = 8.4 \times 10^6 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{700}{1.013}\right) = 8.4 \times 10^6 \times \ln(690.9)$$

$$\ln(690.9) = 6.538$$

$$W_{iso} = 8.4 \times 10^6 \times 6.538 = 5.49 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 54.9 \text{ MJ}$$

$$\boxed{W_{iso} = 54.9 \text{ MJ to store } 6.835 \text{ kg H}_2 \Rightarrow 8.03 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

This is the **thermodynamic minimum** — real compressors consume 12–15 % more (10–12 MJ/kg) due to irreversibilities.

---

> **Question B (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Van't Hoff equation** for a metal hydride. With reference to the LaNi₅–H₂ system, show how the equilibrium pressure varies with temperature. Mention two engineering advantages and one limitation of metal hydride storage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A magnesium hydride (MgH₂) storage bed operates between 300 °C and 400 °C. Given $\Delta H = 74.5$ kJ/mol H₂ and $\Delta S = 132$ J/mol·K, calculate the equilibrium pressures at 300 °C and 400 °C. Hence comment on the **practical viability** of MgH₂ for vehicular storage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Van't Hoff Equation for LaNi₅ (7 marks):**

1. **Derivation outline (2 marks):**
   $$\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$$

2. **Application to LaNi₅H₆ (2 marks):** For LaNi₅, $\Delta H \approx 30.8$ kJ/mol H₂ and $\Delta S \approx 108$ J/mol·K. The desorption plateau pressure is **1 bar near 40 °C**, **5 bar near 80 °C**, and **10 bar near 100 °C**. This makes LaNi₅ ideal for **near-ambient-temperature operation**.

3. **Two engineering advantages (2 marks):**
   - **Very safe** — releases H₂ only on demand via heating; no high-pressure tank.
   - **High volumetric density** (~80–100 kg H₂/m³) — higher than 700 bar CGH₂.
   - *Also acceptable:* long cycle life (>3,000 cycles), simple solid-state handling.

4. **One limitation (1 mark):**
   - **Low gravimetric density** — LaNi₅H₆ stores only **1.4 wt%**, well below the DOE 5.5 wt% target.
   - *Also acceptable:* poisoning by O₂, CO, H₂O, and sulfur compounds.

**(b) MgH₂ Equilibrium Pressures (7 marks):**

Using $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K:

**At T = 300 °C = 573.15 K:**

$$\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{74{,}500}{8.314 \times 573.15} + \dfrac{132}{8.314}$$

$$\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{74{,}500}{4{,}765.0} + 15.876 = -15.635 + 15.876 = 0.241$$

$$P_{eq}(300°C) = e^{0.241} = 1.27 \text{ bar}$$

**At T = 400 °C = 673.15 K:**

$$\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{74{,}500}{8.314 \times 673.15} + \dfrac{132}{8.314}$$

$$\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{74{,}500}{5{,}596.3} + 15.876 = -13.313 + 15.876 = 2.563$$

$$P_{eq}(400°C) = e^{2.563} = 12.97 \text{ bar}$$

**Comment on practical viability (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\text{MgH}_2: P_{eq} = 1.27 \text{ bar at 300 °C} \to 12.97 \text{ bar at 400 °C}}$$

MgH₂ has excellent **gravimetric density (7.6 wt%)** but requires **temperatures above 300 °C** to desorb appreciable H₂. For vehicular PEM fuel cells (which prefer 1–10 bar at 60–80 °C), MgH₂ is **not viable without a high-temperature heat-management system**. The slow desorption kinetics below 350 °C further hinder its use, making it more suitable for **stationary, high-temperature applications** (e.g., thermal energy + H₂ co-generation).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks (Module 3):**
>
> 1. **Confusing wt% with kg/m³.** The question may ask for *gravimetric density* (mass fraction) and the student writes a *volumetric* value or vice versa. **Always re-read** the question.
> 2. **Forgetting units in $W_{iso}$.** The final answer **must be in MJ or kWh** with the per-kg H₂ value. A bare number loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using $\gamma = 1.4$ for H₂.** Hydrogen is a **diatomic molecule**, so $\gamma = C_p/C_v = 1.405$ (not exactly 1.4). Use 1.405 for accuracy.
> 4. **Missing the negative sign in Van't Hoff.** A common slip is to write $\ln P = +\Delta H/R \cdot 1/T$ — which makes pressure *decrease* with temperature. **It is the opposite** because desorption is endothermic.
> 5. **Boil-off rate must be in %/day**, not %/hour or %/month. Convert with care.
> 6. **Type IV vs Type III.** Examiners often test whether you know the *polymer liner* in Type IV. Drawing a metal liner loses 1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — General Storage Methods**

- ☐ Hydrogen's ambient density is **0.0899 kg/m³** — 14× lighter than air; this is *why* storage is hard.
- ☐ **CGH₂ (700 bar):** Gravimetric 5–7 wt%, Type IV tank, ≈ 40 kg H₂/m³, ideal for **automotive**.
- ☐ **LH₂ (20 K):** Volumetric 70.8 kg H₂/m³, boil-off 0.1–0.3 %/day, ideal for **aerospace & bulk**.
- ☐ **Metal Hydrides:** Van't Hoff equation governs $P_{eq}(T)$; LaNi₅H₆ releases at 40 °C but only 1.4 wt%.
- ☐ **Complex Hydrides (NaAlH₄, LiBH₄):** High wt% but high $T$ and slow kinetics.
- ☐ **Chemical Hydrides (NaBH₄, NH₃, LOHC):** Off-board regeneration, excellent for **long-distance transport**.
- ☐ **Underground (Salt Cavern):** Lowest cost per kWh, ideal for **GWh–TWh seasonal grid storage**.
- ☐ **Isothermal compression work:** $W_{iso} = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$ — minimum theoretical energy.
- ☐ **Polytropic work:** $W_{poly} = \dfrac{nRT}{k-1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1\right]$, $k \approx 1.25$–$1.35$ for real H₂ compressors.
- ☐ **Van't Hoff:** $\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ — straight-line plot in $\ln P$ vs $1/T$.
- ☐ **Embrittlement, leakage, and wide flammability range (4–75 %)** drive the *safety* engineering of all storage systems.
- ☐ **DOE 2025 Targets (memorize):** **5.5 wt%** gravimetric and **40 kg/m³** volumetric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressed Storage - Composite Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compressed Storage - Composite Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Compressed Hydrogen Gas (CHG) Storage** is a physical method of storing molecular hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$) in gaseous form at elevated pressures, typically **35 MPa (350 bar)** for low-pressure or **70 MPa (700 bar)** for high-pressure applications. A **Composite Cylinder** (or Composite Pressure Vessel) is a multi-layered, hybrid pressure vessel designed to safely contain high-pressure hydrogen by combining a **non-load-bearing impermeable liner** (the inner liner) with a **load-bearing filament-wound fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) overwrap** that bears the structural load.

Under the **ISO 11439** and **ECE R110** international standards, composite hydrogen cylinders are classified into two primary categories:

| Type | Liner Material | Structural Layer | Application Class |
|------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Type III** | Metallic (Aluminum alloy 6061-T6) | Fully wrapped composite | Heavy-duty vehicles, buses |
| **Type IV** | Polymeric (HDPE / PA6) | Fully wrapped composite | Fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEV) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes **Type IV composite cylinders** because they offer the highest gravimetric density ($\geq 6.0\%\ \text{wt}\ \text{H}_2$) and are the industry standard for FCEVs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **balloon inside a steel pipe**. If you try to inflate the balloon, the rubber stretches and the steel pipe takes the pressure. Now, replace the steel pipe with a **weave of carbon fiber** (stronger than steel by weight, but lighter than aluminum). The balloon (the **liner**) only prevents hydrogen from leaking out, while the carbon-fiber armor (**composite overwrap**) does all the heavy lifting of holding the pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use thick steel?** Pure steel tanks for 70 MPa hydrogen would weigh roughly **40-50 kg per kg of H₂ stored**, making vehicles impractical. Composite cylinders reduce this mass by **4x to 5x**, achieving system-level gravimetric densities of **6–10% wt H₂**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Pressure for FCEV:** $P = 70\ \text{MPa}$ (operating), with proof test at $1.5 \times P = 105\ \text{MPa}$
- **Burst Pressure (Minimum):** $2.25 \times P = 157.5\ \text{MPa}$ (per ISO 11439)
- **Hydrogen Density at 70 MPa, 25°C:** $\rho \approx 40.8\ \text{g/L}$
- **Carbon Fiber Tensile Strength:** $\sigma_{cf} \approx 3.5\text{–}7.0\ \text{GPa}$ (T700-T1100 grades)
- **Design Lifetime:** **15 years / 5,000–11,000 fill cycles**

> [!NOTE]
> **Callout — Hydrogen Embrittlement:** Pure metals suffer hydrogen embrittlement. The **polymer liner in Type IV cylinders** acts as a hydrogen permeation barrier ($\text{Permeation} \leq 1.5\ \text{Ncm}^3/\text{hr/L}$ at 70 MPa), which is why Type IV dominates modern FCEV deployment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Architecture of a Composite Cylinder

A composite hydrogen cylinder is functionally partitioned into **four concentric layers**, each serving a discrete engineering role:

1. **Hydrogen Contact Surface / Liner Core** — Provides hermetic sealing against gas permeation.
2. **Leak-Prevention Barrier** — Often an additional metallic or thermoplastic foil inside the liner.
3. **Composite Overwrap (Load Bearing)** — Wound carbon, glass, or aramid fibers in an epoxy matrix.
4. **External Protective Coating** — UV-resistant polymer jacket (EPDM/Polyurethane) to prevent impact damage.

### 2.2 Mechanics of Pressure Containment — Why Fiber Composites?

When gas pressure $P$ acts on the internal wall of a cylinder, two principal stresses are generated in a thin-walled vessel:

- **Hoop (Circumferential) Stress** $\sigma_h$: acts to "split" the cylinder open along its axis.
- **Longitudinal (Axial) Stress** $\sigma_l$: acts to "stretch" the cylinder along its length.

The hoop stress is **twice** the axial stress: $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$. Therefore, the fiber architecture in composite cylinders is **deliberately non-isotropic** — most fibers (typically **>80%** of total fiber volume) are wound at **±10° to ±20° helical angles** to optimally resist the dominant hoop stress.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Equation | $PV = nRT$ | $P$ (Pa), $V$ (m³), $n$ (mol) | Mass of H₂ stored |
| 2 | Hydrogen Mass in Tank | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{PM_{H_2}V}{RT}$ | $M_{H_2} = 2.016\ \text{g/mol}$ | Capacity calculation |
| 3 | Hoop Stress (Thin-Wall) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{Pr}{t} = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ | $r$ (m), $t$ (m), $P$ (Pa) | Cylinder sizing |
| 4 | Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{Pr}{2t} = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ | — | End-cap load |
| 5 | Von Mises Stress (Ductile Liner) | $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_l + \sigma_l^2}$ | — | Liner yield check |
| 6 | Ts'ai-Wu Failure Criterion | $F_1\sigma_1 + F_2\sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + 2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 \leq 1$ | Strength tensors $F$ | Composite failure |
| 7 | Fiber Volume Fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{A_{fiber}}{A_{total}} = \dfrac{t_{fiber}}{t_{composite}}$ | — | Mass optimization |
| 8 | Burst Pressure (Netting Theory) | $P_b = \dfrac{2 t_w \sigma_{f,u} \cos^2\alpha}{r}$ | $\alpha$ = winding angle | Hoop-dominated burst |
| 9 | Gravimetric Density | $\rho_{grav} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{tank}+m_{H_2}} \times 100\%$ | % | DOE 2025 target: 6.5% |
| 10 | Volumetric Density | $\rho_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{V_{tank}}$ | g/L | DOE 2025 target: 50 g/L |
| 11 | Stress Ratio (Hoop/Axial) | $\dfrac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = 2$ | dimensionless | Design rule |
| 12 | Composite Laminate Stress | $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$ | $V_f+V_m=1$ | Rule of mixtures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numerical:** At 70 MPa and inner radius 175 mm, with a 25 mm composite overwrap, hoop stress in the composite layer reaches $\sigma_h \approx \dfrac{70 \times 10^6 \times 0.175}{0.025} = 490\ \text{MPa}$. This is why **T700-grade carbon fiber** ($\sigma_{ult} \approx 4900\ \text{MPa}$) is mandatory with a safety factor of **≥ 2.5**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs):** Toyota Mirai uses **3 Type IV tanks @ 70 MPa** holding 5.6 kg H₂.
- **Heavy-Duty Transport:** Buses and trucks (Type III tanks @ 35 MPa) for transit fleets.
- **Backup Power:** Telecom and data center fuel cell backup systems.
- **Hydrogen Refueling Stations:** Cascade storage banks using composite cylinders.
- **Aerospace:** Space Launch Systems use composite overwrapped tanks for cryogenic + high-pressure LH₂.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

**Assumption:** Wall thickness $t \leq \dfrac{r}{10}$ (thin-wall approximation valid for composite overwraps).

**Step 1 — Free Body Cut:** Imagine slicing the cylinder longitudinally into two half-cylinders. The internal gas pressure $P$ acts on the projected area $A = 2rL$ (where $L$ is cylinder length).

**Step 2 — Force Balance on the Half-Cylinder:** The pressure force is balanced by the hoop stress acting across the two cut walls, each with cross-sectional area $tL$.

$$P \cdot (2rL) = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Simplification:** Cancel $2L$ from both sides:

$$P \cdot r = \sigma_h \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Final Hoop Stress Equation:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{P r}{t} = \frac{PD}{2t}}$$

This derivation shows that to keep $\sigma_h$ within allowable limits, we must either **increase wall thickness $t$** or **reduce radius $r$**. Increasing $t$ adds mass, so designers opt for **higher-strength fibers** instead.

### 3.2 Derivation II: Longitudinal Stress

**Step 1 — Consider a Cross-Sectional Cut** perpendicular to the cylinder axis. The pressure force on the end cap is $F = P \cdot \pi r^2$.

**Step 2 — Balancing Force** in the longitudinal direction by stress in the cylindrical wall (cross-sectional area $= 2\pi r t$):

$$P \cdot \pi r^2 = \sigma_l \cdot 2\pi r t$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$P \cdot r = 2 \sigma_l \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Final:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{P r}{2t} = \frac{PD}{4t}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of Stress Ratio:**

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{PD/2t}{PD/4t} = 2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 2:1 ratio **proves** the cylinder is hoop-stress dominated, and therefore **fiber winding at low helical angles** ($\pm 15°$) is mechanically optimal.

### 3.3 Derivation III: Burst Pressure from Netting Analysis

The **Netting Theory** (classical composite mechanics) assumes the matrix carries no load, and only fibers contribute. Let the composite thickness be $t_w$, the winding angle be $\alpha$ (measured from the cylinder axis), and fiber ultimate tensile strength be $\sigma_{f,u}$.

**Step 1 — Force Balance in the Hoop Direction** at the burst point:

$$P_b \cdot 2r = 2 t_w \sigma_{f,u} \cos^2 \alpha$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Burst Pressure:**

$$\boxed{P_b = \frac{2 t_w \sigma_{f,u} \cos^2 \alpha}{r}}$$

**Step 3 — Design Safety Factor** is applied:

$$\text{Safety Factor} = \frac{P_b}{P_{\text{working}}} \geq 2.25$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example:**

Given: $r = 0.175\ \text{m}$, $t_w = 0.025\ \text{m}$, $\alpha = 15°$, $\sigma_{f,u} = 4900\ \text{MPa}$, $P_{\text{working}} = 70\ \text{MPa}$.

$$P_b = \frac{2 \times 0.025 \times 4900 \times 10^6 \times \cos^2(15°)}{0.175}$$

$$P_b = \frac{2 \times 0.025 \times 4900 \times 10^6 \times 0.9330}{0.175}$$

$$P_b = \frac{228{,}585{,}000}{0.175} = 1{,}306{,}200{,}000\ \text{Pa} \approx 1306\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Safety Factor:**

$$\text{SF} = \frac{1306}{70} \approx 18.66 \gg 2.25 \quad \text{✓ SAFE}$$

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Hydrogen Mass Stored in Composite Cylinder

**Step 1 — Apply the Ideal Gas Equation:** $PV = nRT$

**Step 2 — Express moles in terms of mass:** $n = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{M_{H_2}}$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$P V = \frac{m_{H_2}}{M_{H_2}} R T$$

**Step 4 — Solve for hydrogen mass:**

$$\boxed{m_{H_2} = \frac{P V M_{H_2}}{R T}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (Type IV tank, 70 MPa, 122 L volume, 25°C):**

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{70 \times 10^6 \times 0.122 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 298.15}$$

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{17{,}216.6}{2478.7} \approx 6.946\ \text{kg}$$

This matches the **Toyota Mirai 2nd Gen** spec (5.6 kg usable / ~7 kg total).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Type III vs. Type IV Composite Cylinders

| Performance Parameter | Type III (Al Liner) | Type IV (Polymer Liner) | KTU Exam Relevance |
|------------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Liner Weight | Higher (Al 6061) | Lower (HDPE/PA) | Compare gravimetric density |
| Gravimetric Density | 3.5–4.5% wt H₂ | 5.5–7.5% wt H₂ | DO NOT confuse units |
| Volumetric Density | 25–30 g/L | 35–45 g/L | Storage efficiency |
| Cycle Life | 11,000 | 5,000–7,500 | FCEV durability |
| Cost (USD/kWh) | 18–22 | 14–18 | Industry comparison |
| Hydrogen Permeation | $< 1.0\ \text{Ncm}^3/\text{hr/L}$ | $< 1.5\ \text{Ncm}^3/\text{hr/L}$ | SAE J2579 limit |
| Burst Pressure | 165–200 MPa | 157.5–180 MPa | ISO 11439 compliance |
| Operating Pressure | 35–70 MPa | 70 MPa | FCEV standard |
| Manufacturing | Filament winding | Filament winding + liner molding | Process engineering |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Composite Cylinder Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[External Protective Jacket - EPDM or Polyurethane] --> B[Composite Overwrap Layer - Carbon Fiber and Epoxy Matrix]
    B --> C[Composite Overwrap Layer - Hoop Dominant Helical Winding]
    C --> D[Leak Barrier - Metallic or Polymer Foil]
    D --> E[Inner Liner - Aluminum 6061 or HDPE or PA6]
    E --> F[Internal Hydrogen Gas at 70 MPa]

    G[Filament Winding Machine] -->|winds fiber at 15 degrees| B
    H[Autoclave Curing Oven] -->|cure at 80 to 150 degrees| B
    I[Pressure Testing Station] -->|proof test at 105 MPa| A
    J[Helium Mass Spectrometer] -->|leak check| E
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Stress Distribution in Composite Cylinder

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Internal Pressure P = 70 MPa] --> B[Hoop Stress sigma h = PD over 2t]
    A --> C[Longitudinal Stress sigma l = PD over 4t]
    B --> D[Dominant Stress on Fiber Winding Angle 15 degrees]
    C --> E[Secondary Stress on End Cap Region]
    D --> F[Carbon Fiber T700 Carries 80 percent of Load]
    E --> G[Aluminum Liner Carries 20 percent Seal Load]
    F --> H[Burst Pressure 157.5 MPa Minimum]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Safety Factor 2.25 - ISO 11439 Compliant]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow: Manufacturing & Qualification of Type IV Cylinders

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HDPE Polymer Liner Injection Molding] --> B[Surface Treatment and Barrier Foil Lamination]
    B --> C[Filament Winding of Carbon Fiber Tow over Epoxy Resin]
    C --> D[Helical Winding at 15 degrees Hoop Dominant]
    C --> E[Circumferential Winding at 90 degrees for Hoop Strength]
    D --> F[Autoclave Curing at 80 degrees for 4 hours]
    E --> F
    F --> G[External Jacket Molding with EPDM]
    G --> H[Pressure Proof Test at 1.5 times Working Pressure]
    H --> I[Hydraulic Burst Test to Verify 2.25 times Factor]
    I --> J[Cycle Test 5000 to 11000 Cycles]
    J --> K[Helium Leak Test using Mass Spectrometer]
    K --> L[Certification per ISO 11439 and ECE R110]
```

### 4.4 Functional Load Path Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hydrogen Gas Pressure Load] --> B[Polymer Liner Distributes Load]
    B --> C[Carbon Fiber Composite Overwrap Carries Hoop Stress]
    B --> D[End Dome Region Carries Longitudinal Stress]
    C --> E[Load Transfer via Shear at Fiber Matrix Interface]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Reaction Force at Boss and Valve Fitting]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`
**Differentiate between Type III and Type IV composite hydrogen storage cylinders with respect to liner material, gravimetric density, and typical application.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Type III** uses an **aluminum alloy (6061-T6) metallic liner** with a fully wrapped composite overwrap. Gravimetric density is lower, around **3.5–4.5% wt H₂**, and is typically used in **heavy-duty vehicles and buses** at 35–70 MPa.
- **Type IV** uses a **polymeric liner (HDPE or PA6)** with a fully wrapped composite overwrap. Gravimetric density is higher, around **5.5–7.5% wt H₂**, and is the **industry standard for FCEVs** like Toyota Mirai at 70 MPa.
- **Key Distinction:** Type IV eliminates the heavy metallic liner, thus improving system-level mass efficiency at the cost of higher hydrogen permeation resistance requirements.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sessional Exam 1]`
**Why is the fiber winding angle in composite hydrogen cylinders typically chosen at ±15° rather than ±90° (purely circumferential)?**

**Model Answer:**
The hoop stress $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ is **twice** the longitudinal stress $\sigma_l = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$. A **purely circumferential winding (90°)** would carry the hoop stress efficiently but **fail catastrophically in the longitudinal direction** because no fibers resist axial loads. A **winding angle of ±15°** provides an optimal balance — the majority of fibers ($\cos^2 15° \approx 0.93$ of the load) align with the dominant hoop stress, while the helical off-axis component still contributes to longitudinal reinforcement. This dual-axis capability is essential for cylinder integrity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Question Paper Module 3]`**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel, explaining the underlying assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Type IV composite hydrogen cylinder has an **inner radius of 180 mm**, a **composite wall thickness of 28 mm**, and is designed for a **working pressure of 70 MPa**. The fiber winding angle is 15°, and the carbon fiber (T700) ultimate tensile strength is **4900 MPa**. Calculate the burst pressure using netting analysis and verify the safety factor against ISO 11439. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
Refer to the derivations in **Section 3.1 and 3.2** of these notes. Key assumptions to state:
- Thin-wall approximation: $t \leq r/10$
- Uniform internal pressure $P$
- Material is homogeneous and isotropic (for metallic liner case)
- End caps contribute negligibly to the cylindrical wall analysis
- Stress distribution is uniform across wall thickness

Result:
- $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ (Hoop)
- $\sigma_l = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ (Longitudinal)
- Stress ratio $\sigma_h/\sigma_l = 2$

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 0.180\ \text{m}$, $t_w = 0.028\ \text{m}$, $\alpha = 15°$, $\sigma_{f,u} = 4900 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa}$, $P_{\text{working}} = 70 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Netting Theory Burst Pressure Equation:**

$$P_b = \frac{2 t_w \sigma_{f,u} \cos^2 \alpha}{r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$P_b = \frac{2 \times 0.028 \times 4900 \times 10^6 \times \cos^2(15°)}{0.180}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\cos^2(15°)$:**

$$\cos(15°) = 0.9659 \Rightarrow \cos^2(15°) = 0.9330$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerator:**

$$2 \times 0.028 \times 4900 \times 10^6 \times 0.9330 = 255{,}999{,}600\ \text{N/m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Burst Pressure:**

$$P_b = \frac{255{,}999{,}600}{0.180} = 1{,}422{,}220{,}000\ \text{Pa} \approx 1422\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Safety Factor:**

$$\text{SF} = \frac{P_b}{P_{\text{working}}} = \frac{1422}{70} \approx 20.32$$

**Step 7 — Verification:** The required minimum safety factor per ISO 11439 is **2.25**. Since $20.32 \gg 2.25$, the design is **SAFE and compliant**. The high SF indicates significant over-design, which is common in safety-critical hydrogen applications.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]`**

**(a)** Explain the constructional features and functional role of each layer in a Type IV composite hydrogen cylinder. Discuss why polymer liners are preferred over metallic liners for modern FCEV applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 122-liter Type IV composite tank stores hydrogen at 70 MPa and 25°C. Using the ideal gas law, calculate the mass of hydrogen stored. If the empty tank mass is 42.5 kg, compute the **gravimetric density** and compare it with the **DOE 2025 target of 6.5% wt H₂**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Layer-wise Explanation (7 marks):**
- **Inner Liner (HDPE/PA6):** Provides hermetic hydrogen barrier, prevents permeation and leakage, distributes internal pressure uniformly onto the composite overwrap.
- **Barrier Foil (Optional Metallic/Polymer):** Reduces hydrogen permeation rates below SAE J2579 limits ($< 1.5\ \text{Ncm}^3/\text{hr/L}$).
- **Composite Overwrap (Carbon Fiber + Epoxy):** Carries **>95% of the structural load**; fibers wound helically to resist both hoop and longitudinal stresses.
- **External Jacket (EPDM/PU):** Protects against UV degradation, abrasion, and minor impact damage.

**Why polymer over metal liners?**
- **Weight reduction:** HDPE ($\rho = 950\ \text{kg/m}^3$) is **3x lighter** than aluminum ($\rho = 2700\ \text{kg/m}^3$).
- **No hydrogen embrittlement:** Polymers are immune to H₂-induced cracking.
- **Higher gravimetric density:** Enables FCEV range targets (500+ km).
- **Manufacturing flexibility:** Allows complex dome geometries via blow molding.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V = 0.122\ \text{m}^3$, $P = 70 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa}$, $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, $m_{\text{tank}} = 42.5\ \text{kg}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Ideal Gas Law:**

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{P V M_{H_2}}{R T}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{70 \times 10^6 \times 0.122 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 298.15}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerator:**

$$70 \times 10^6 \times 0.122 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 17{,}216.6\ \text{N·m} \cdot \text{s/m}$$

In SI units, this simplifies to $17{,}216.6$ in numerator units.

**Step 4 — Compute Denominator:**

$$8.314 \times 298.15 = 2478.66$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Mass:**

$$m_{H_2} = \frac{17{,}216.6}{2478.66} \approx 6.946\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Gravimetric Density:**

$$\rho_{grav} = \frac{m_{H_2}}{m_{\text{tank}} + m_{H_2}} \times 100\%$$

$$\rho_{grav} = \frac{6.946}{42.5 + 6.946} \times 100\% = \frac{6.946}{49.446} \times 100\%$$

$$\rho_{grav} \approx 14.05\%$$

**Step 7 — Comparison with DOE 2025 Target:**

$$\text{Achieved} = 14.05\% \gg \text{Target} = 6.5\%$$
