## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*State any three advantages of transporting hydrogen through dedicated pipelines as compared with road-based compressed gas trailers.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Lower Specific Energy Consumption** — Pipelines consume only 0.2 – 0.5% of LHV per 200 km, whereas compressed gas trailers consume 8 – 12% of LHV for the same distance due to multiple compression stages.
2. **Continuous & Scalable Delivery** — A single 0.5 m pipeline can transport 50 – 100 tonnes/day of H₂ continuously, equivalent to 200 – 400 truck trips.
3. **Lower Operational Cost per kg-km** — Once amortized, pipeline transport costs **$0.5 – 1.5 per kg per 1000 km**, versus **$5 – 12 per kg per 1000 km** for truck transport.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is hydrogen embrittlement? Mention two mitigation strategies used in pipeline material selection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hydrogen embrittlement is the **degradation of mechanical properties (ductility, fracture toughness) of metals due to the diffusion of atomic hydrogen into the metal lattice**, leading to HIC, SOHIC, or SSC failures.

Mitigation strategies (any two, 1.5 marks each):
1. Use of **low-sulfur, inclusion-controlled steels** (API 5L X65/X70 grade) with vacuum-degassed production.
2. Application of **internal polymer/epoxy liners** (e.g., 3LPE — Three-Layer Polyethylene) to physically block H₂ contact with steel.
3. Installation of **dehydration units** upstream of the pipeline to maintain moisture below −40 °C dew point.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Module-Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B in the 2024 Scheme carries 14 marks per question. Students answer ONE out of TWO choices. Each choice typically carries two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both sub-parts require descriptive explanation **plus** numerical support.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled schematic, explain the **five-stage architecture** of a dedicated hydrogen pipeline system, from production to end-use delivery. State typical operating pressures and the function of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
1. **Production & Gathering (1 mark)** — H₂ produced by electrolyzers or SMR at low pressure (1 – 30 bar); gathered into manifold headers.
2. **Inlet Compression (1 mark)** — Multi-stage reciprocating compressors boost pressure to **40 – 100 bar** depending on trunk class. Reciprocating units are preferred over centrifugal for low flow rates.
3. **Trunk Transmission (1.5 marks)** — Buried API 5L steel pipelines of diameter **0.3 – 1.0 m** carry H₂ over hundreds of kilometres. Internal epoxy coating prevents embrittlement. Periodic **cathodic protection** mitigates external corrosion.
4. **Booster / Re-compression (1 mark)** — Centrifugal compressors at intervals of 80 – 120 km restore line pressure. Each station consumes **1.5 – 3 kWh/kg H₂**.
5. **City Gate & Distribution (1.5 marks)** — Pressure is reduced to **10 – 30 bar** via pressure-regulating valves; flow is metered through turbine or Coriolis meters. Final-mile distribution uses **MDPE pipes** at 1 – 10 bar.
6. **End-Use Delivery (1 mark)** — Industrial users receive H₂ directly; vehicle refuelling stations cascade-compress to **350 or 700 bar**.

---

**(b)** A hydrogen pipeline of diameter **D = 0.4 m** and length **L = 80 km** carries hydrogen at a mass flow rate of $\dot m = 0.5$ kg/s. The average temperature is **288 K**, inlet pressure is **70 bar**, and the Darcy friction factor (from Moody chart) is **0.014**. Use the isothermal compressible flow equation to determine the **outlet pressure**. Take Z = 1.03. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

$$P_1^{2} - P_2^{2} = \dfrac{16 f L \dot m^{2} Z R T}{\pi^{2} D^{5} M}$$

Substituting values (R = 8.314 J/mol·K, M = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol):

$$P_1^{2} - P_2^{2} = \dfrac{16 \times 0.014 \times 80{,}000 \times (0.5)^{2} \times 1.03 \times 8.314 \times 288}{\pi^{2} \times (0.4)^{5} \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Numerator:**
$$16 \times 0.014 = 0.224$$
$$0.224 \times 80{,}000 = 17{,}920$$
$$17{,}920 \times 0.25 = 4{,}480$$
$$4{,}480 \times 1.03 = 4{,}614.4$$
$$4{,}614.4 \times 8.314 = 38{,}373$$
$$38{,}373 \times 288 \approx 1.105 \times 10^{7}$$

**Denominator:**
$$\pi^{2} \approx 9.870$$
$$(0.4)^{5} = 0.01024$$
$$9.870 \times 0.01024 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2.038 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Quotient:**
$$P_1^{2} - P_2^{2} \approx \dfrac{1.105 \times 10^{7}}{2.038 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 5.42 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa}^{2}$$

With $P_1 = 70 \text{ bar} = 70 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$, so $P_1^{2} = 4.9 \times 10^{15} \text{ Pa}^{2}$.

$$P_2^{2} = 4.9 \times 10^{15} - 5.42 \times 10^{10} \approx 4.8999 \times 10^{15}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_2 \approx 6.999 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \approx 69.99 \text{ bar}\,}$$

The pressure drop over 80 km is therefore only **≈ 0.01 bar**, demonstrating that for *moderate* mass flow rates, large-diameter pipelines experience negligible ΔP — validating the **economic superiority of pipelines at high volumetric throughput**.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Compare **dedicated hydrogen pipelines** with **repurposed natural-gas pipelines** for hydrogen service. Discuss, with reasons, the engineering challenges and metallurgical limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Comparison Axis | Dedicated H₂ Pipeline (3.5 marks) | Repurposed NG Pipeline (3.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Capital Cost** | New build costs $1 – 2 million/km | Retrofitting costs $0.3 – 0.8 million/km (40 – 60% savings) |
| **Material** | API 5L X65/X70 with internal epoxy liner; designed for H₂ from day 1 | Original carbon steel may not meet ASTM A333 Grade 6 hydrogen compatibility |
| **Operating Pressure** | 40 – 100 bar | Limited by original NG design (typically 30 – 70 bar) |
| **Compressor Selection** | Hydrogen-rated diaphragm / piston compressors | Compressors must be replaced due to seal compatibility |
| **Leakage Risk** | Lower due to newer gaskets, modern welding (orbital TIG) | Higher due to legacy flange joints, cast-iron sections |
| **Service Life** | 40 – 50 years (designed) | 15 – 25 years residual life (depends on prior NG service) |
| **Blending Limit** | Pure H₂ transmission | Maximum 5 – 20 vol% H₂ blend in NG without appliance modification |

**Engineering Challenges (any two, 0.5 mark each within the 3.5 marks):**
- Hydrogen-induced cracking in high-strength steel welds.
- Compression energy penalty for very low molecular weight.
- Permeation losses through polymer liners over decades.
- Public acceptance and right-of-way acquisition for new pipeline corridors.

---

**(b)** A compressor station compresses hydrogen from **20 bar to 70 bar** at an inlet temperature of **298 K**. Calculate the **specific isentropic work** per kg of hydrogen. Assume $\gamma = 1.41$, $M = 2.016$ g/mol, and the compressor isentropic efficiency is **75%**. Express your answer both in **kJ/kg** and as a **percentage of LHV** (LHV = 120 MJ/kg). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Specific gas constant for H₂.

$$R_s = \dfrac{8.314}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} = 4124 \text{ J/kg·K}$$

**Step 2:** Pressure ratio.

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{70}{20} = 3.5$$

**Step 3:** Exponent.

$$\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.41}{1.41} \approx 0.2908$$

**Step 4:** Isentropic work.

$$W_{isentropic} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_s T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.2908} - 1\right]$$

$$W_{isentropic} = 3.439 \times 4124 \times 298 \times \left[(3.5)^{0.2908} - 1\right]$$

Compute $(3.5)^{0.2908}$:
$$\ln(3.5) = 1.2528$$
$$0.2908 \times 1.2528 = 0.3643$$
$$e^{0.3643} \approx 1.4396$$

$$W_{isentropic} = 3.439 \times 4124 \times 298 \times 0.4396$$

$$W_{isentropic} = 3.439 \times 1{,}228{,}952 \times 0.4396 \approx 1.859 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$$

**Step 5:** Actual work with efficiency.

$$W_{actual} = \dfrac{W_{isentropic}}{\eta} = \dfrac{1.859 \times 10^{6}}{0.75} \approx 2.479 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\,W_{comp} \approx 2479 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2.48 \text{ MJ/kg}\,}$$

**Step 6:** As percentage of LHV.

$$E_{transport} = \dfrac{2.479}{120} \times 100\% \approx 2.07\%$$

This means **2.07% of the hydrogen's energy content is consumed in compressing it from 20 bar to 70 bar** — an essential figure in supply-chain techno-economic analysis.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Hydrogen Pipeline Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by isentropic efficiency** — Many students compute the *isentropic* work but stop there. KTU examiners deduct **1.5 marks** if actual work is not calculated.
> 2. **Using the universal gas constant (R = 8.314) instead of specific gas constant (R_s = 4124 J/kg·K)** for hydrogen — this produces answers off by a factor of ~500. **Always check your units!**
> 3. **Confusing Darcy friction factor (f) with Fanning friction factor (f_F)**. They differ by a factor of 4: $f_{Darcy} = 4 f_{Fanning}$. Use Darcy unless the question specifies otherwise.
> 4. **Omitting the molar mass unit conversion** when computing density from the ideal gas law — a frequent source of numerical errors worth **0.5 mark deduction**.
> 5. **Not stating operating conditions** (P, T) explicitly in numerical answers — KTU 2024 scheme mandates that all engineering numerical solutions carry a clearly labelled "GIVEN:" block at the top.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Hydrogen pipelines are the most energy-efficient bulk transport mode** for distances > 200 km and flow rates > 1000 kg/day.
- ✅ **Dedicated pipelines** operate at 40 – 100 bar; **distribution networks** at 1 – 30 bar.
- ✅ Key formula: **$\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$** — Darcy-Weisbach governs pressure drop.
- ✅ Compressor work: **$W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_s T_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right] / \eta$** — must always be divided by efficiency.
- ✅ **Hydrogen-specific gas constant** $R_s = 4124$ J/kg·K — *not* 8.314 J/mol·K.
- ✅ Hydrogen's **low viscosity and low molar mass** cause higher leakage rates (~2.83× methane) and higher velocity at equal mass flow.
- ✅ **Hydrogen embrittlement** requires low-sulfur steels + internal epoxy/polymer liners.
- ✅ Booster stations needed every **80 – 120 km**.
- ✅ Energy loss per 200 km in well-designed pipeline ≈ **0.4 – 0.5% of LHV**.
- ✅ Three major existing hydrogen pipeline networks: **Gulf Coast (USA, 1100+ km), North Sea (Netherlands-Germany-Belgium), and Antwerp-Rotterdam corridor**.
- ✅ **Repurposing NG pipelines** saves 40 – 60% capital cost but is limited by **blending ratio (≤ 20 vol%)** due to end-use appliance compatibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question mentions "hydrogen transport" without specifying distance, **assume pipeline is preferred for > 300 km** and *liquid/tanker* for < 300 km. This single decision-tree logic will save you time in the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long Distance Hydrogen Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Long Distance Hydrogen Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Long Distance Hydrogen Transmission** refers to the bulk transportation of molecular hydrogen (H₂) over extended geographical distances — typically ranging from **100 km to several thousand kilometres** — from centralised production sites (e.g., large-scale electrolysers, steam methane reformers with carbon capture, or industrial by-product sources) to end-use demand centres such as refineries, ammonia plants, fuel-cell vehicle refuelling stations, steelworks, and power generation facilities.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECHT616 – Module 3)** syllabus, long-distance transmission encompasses four principal pathways:

| Pathway | Physical State | Typical Carrier Medium |
|---|---|---|
| **Compressed Gas Pipelines** | Gaseous H₂ | Steel / composite pipelines |
| **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂) Shipping** | Cryogenic liquid (−253 °C) | Insulated road tankers / ships |
| **Ammonia (NH₃) Carrier** | Liquid NH₃ at −33 °C or 9 bar | Dedicated NH₃ tankers |
| **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carrier (LOHC)** | Chemically bound H₂ | Diesel/toluene-like liquids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly stresses that transmission is *not* the same as **distribution** (which is short-range, low-pressure, last-mile delivery). Transmission deals with high-capacity, energy-intensive bulk movement governed by thermodynamic and fluid-mechanical principles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hydrogen Highway"

Think of long-distance hydrogen transmission exactly like the **national highway system** for vehicles:

- **Production plants** (electrolyser hubs, SMR+CCS) = the **Factories/Cities** generating goods.
- **Pipelines / LH₂ ships / NH₃ tankers** = the **National Highways & Freight Trains** that move bulk cargo.
- **Compressor stations along the route** = the **Toll Plazas / Refuelling Bunks** that keep the energy of the moving fluid topped up (because hydrogen, being the smallest molecule, leaks and loses pressure rapidly).
- **End users (refineries, FCVs, industries)** = the **Local Shops** where goods are finally consumed.

Just as goods cannot be economically transported over 1000 km using bicycles (low energy density), hydrogen — despite being the *lightest* molecule — cannot be moved in tiny cylinders; it needs **infrastructure-scale carriers**.

## 1.3 Why Long-Distance Transmission Matters

The geographical mismatch between renewable energy resources (solar in Rajasthan/Gujarat, wind in Tamil Nadu, hydropower in the Himalayas) and demand centres (industrial clusters in Europe, Japan, Korea) makes **long-distance H₂ transport inevitable** in a global hydrogen economy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Required:**
> - Universal Gas Constant, $R = 8.314 \; \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
> - Molar Mass of H₂, $M = 2.016 \; \text{g/mol}$
> - Specific Gas Constant of H₂, $R_{H_2} = 4124 \; \text{J·kg}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂, $\text{LHV} = 120 \; \text{MJ/kg}$ (**33.33 kWh/kg**)
> - Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂, $\text{HHV} = 142 \; \text{MJ/kg}$ (**39.4 kWh/kg**)
> - Density of H₂ at STP, $\rho_{STP} = 0.0899 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Density of LH₂ at boiling point, $\rho_{LH_2} = 70.8 \; \text{kg/m}^3$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transmission Pathway Comparison — Operational Logic

### Pathway A: Compressed Gaseous H₂ Pipelines

This is the **most mature and least energy-intensive** option for distances up to ~**1500–2000 km**.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Hydrogen is compressed to **50–100 bar** (transmission pressure) at the production site.
2. It flows through **dedicated steel pipelines** (existing natural gas pipelines can be *repurposed* with material modifications).
3. Every **80–160 km**, a **compressor station** repressurises the gas to overcome frictional and elevation losses.
4. At the city gate / industrial gate, the pressure is regulated down for distribution.

**Key Engineering Concern — Hydrogen Embrittlement:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> When atomic hydrogen diffuses into the crystal lattice of steels (especially high-strength martensitic steels), it drastically reduces ductility and tensile strength — a phenomenon called **Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE)**. KTU examiners frequently test this concept because it dictates pipe material selection (favouring austenitic stainless steels, PE100 plastic liners, or API 5L X65/X70 with controlled hardness < HRC 22).

**Why and How Compressors are Required:**
- Hydrogen has a **very low molecular weight** → high specific volume → high velocity at given mass flow.
- High velocity → **high frictional pressure drop** → frequent recompression.
- Energy required per kg of H₂ compressed from $P_1$ to $P_2$ (isentropic):

$$ W_{comp} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot R_{H_2} \cdot T_1 \cdot \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_{isen}} $$

where $\gamma = 1.41$ for diatomic H₂, $T_1$ is inlet temperature (K), and $\eta_{isen}$ is isentropic efficiency (typically **0.70–0.85** for reciprocating, **0.80–0.90** for centrifugal).

---

### Pathway B: Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂) Shipping

For **transcontinental / intercontinental** transmission (>2000 km or sea routes).

**Operational Steps:**
1. H₂ is liquefied by **Joule-Thomson expansion combined with mechanical refrigeration** (Claude cycle) at **−253 °C (20.27 K)**.
2. Stored in **double-walled, vacuum-insulated cryogenic tanks**.
3. Transported in **LH₂ road tankers** (typical capacity: 65 m³ ≈ 4600 kg) or **LH₂ marine vessels** (up to 40,000 m³ planned).
4. Regasified at the receiving terminal using ambient air or seawater as heat source.

**Energy Penalty — Liquefaction:**
- Liquefaction requires roughly **30–40 % of H₂'s LHV** (i.e., **10–13 kWh per kg of LH₂**).
- This is the **major drawback** of LH₂ transmission.

**Boil-off Losses (BOR):**
- Modern LH₂ tanks achieve BOR of **0.03–0.1 % per day**.
- For long voyages (>15 days), BOR becomes significant.

---

### Pathway C: Ammonia (NH₃) as Hydrogen Carrier

NH₃ contains **17.65 % H₂ by mass** (1 kg NH₃ carries 0.1765 kg H₂).

**Operational Steps:**
1. H₂ is reacted with N₂ (Haber-Bosch): $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$ at **400–500 °C, 150–300 bar**.
2. NH₃ is **liquefied easily** at **−33 °C or +9 bar** (much milder than LH₂).
3. Transported using existing **global NH₃ infrastructure** (well-established for fertiliser industry).
4. At destination, NH₃ is "cracked" back to H₂ and N₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Round-trip efficiency of NH₃ route ≈ 30–45 %** (vs. ~75–85 % for gaseous pipelines), but infrastructure already exists, making it a near-term bridge technology.

---

### Pathway D: Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)

**Operational Steps:**
1. H₂ is chemically bound to an organic liquid (e.g., toluene → methylcyclohexane, MCH; or dibenzyltoluene, DBT).
2. The hydrogenated LOHC is shipped at **ambient temperature and pressure** using conventional oil tankers/tank trucks.
3. At destination, catalytic dehydrogenation releases H₂; the carrier liquid is returned to origin for re-hydrogenation.

**Real-world utility:** Germany-Japan / Australia-Japan hydrogen supply chains under development rely heavily on this technology.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY LaTeX Rule:** Pipes are rendered as `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law | $P V = n R T = m R_{H_2} T$ | $P$ [Pa], $V$ [m³], $T$ [K] |
| 2 | Hydrogen Density at Pressure | $\rho = \frac{P M}{R T}$ | $\rho$ [kg/m³], $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol |
| 3 | Isentropic Compressor Work | $W_s = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{H_2} T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | $W_s$ [J/kg], $\gamma = 1.41$ |
| 4 | Polytropic Compression | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R_{H_2} T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$ | $n$ = polytropic index (1.25–1.35 typical) |
| 5 | **Darcy-Weisbach Pressure Drop** | $\Delta P = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ [m], $D$ [m], $v$ [m/s] |
| 6 | Volumetric Flow to Mass Flow | $\dot{m} = \rho A v = \rho \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} v$ | $\dot{m}$ [kg/s], $A$ [m²] |
| 7 | Pipeline Mass Throughput (steady state) | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D \Delta P}{f L \rho}}$ | Substituting for $v$ in (5) |
| 8 | Fanning Friction Factor (smooth pipe) | $f_{Fanning} = \dfrac{0.079}{Re^{0.25}}$ | Blasius correlation, $Re < 10^5$ |
| 9 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $\mu_{H_2} \approx 8.76 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa·s at 300 K |
| 10 | LHV Energy per Unit Volume | $E_{vol} = \rho \cdot \text{LHV}$ | [MJ/m³] |
| 11 | Hydrogen Mass Fraction in NH₃ | $w_{H_2} = \dfrac{3 \times 2.016}{17.031} = 0.1765$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Energy in Pipeline (delivered) | $E_{del} = \dot{m} \cdot \text{LHV} \cdot \eta_{delivery}$ | $\eta_{delivery}$ accounts for leaks & pressure regulation |
| 13 | Compressor Power (shaft) | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\dot{m} W_s}{\eta_{mech}}$ | [W], $\eta_{mech} \approx 0.95$ |
| 14 | Compression Energy per kg | $e_c = \dfrac{W_s}{\eta_{mech} \eta_{elec}}$ | [kWh/kg H₂] |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipelines:** Active H₂ pipelines exist globally (USA: ~2700 km, Europe: ~1700 km, gradually expanding).
- **LH₂ marine transport:** Japan's **"HySTRA"** project shipped LH₂ from Australia (HESC Suiso Frontier vessel) in 2022.
- **NH₃ shipping:** Already a **180 million tonne/year** global industry (fertilisers).
- **LOHC:** **"CO2nnect"** network (NOW GmbH) and **"SPERA H₂"** (Chiyoda Corporation, MCH system) are operational demonstrators.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are written **exhaustively** — every algebraic transition is shown explicitly, and each equation block is enclosed in `$$ ... $$` with one blank line before and after, in compliance with the **KTU 2024 LaTeX Isolation Rule**.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Isentropic Compression Work for Hydrogen

**Problem Statement:** A centrifugal compressor takes in hydrogen at $P_1 = 20$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K and delivers it at $P_2 = 80$ bar. Calculate the specific isentropic work and the required shaft power for a mass flow rate of $\dot{m} = 1.2$ kg/s. Assume isentropic efficiency $\eta_{isen} = 0.82$, mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech} = 0.96$.

### Step 1 — Recall the General Isentropic Work Equation
For an ideal gas undergoing isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression from state 1 to state 2, the steady-flow energy equation (neglecting KE and PE) gives:

$$ W_s = h_2 - h_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1) $$

where $c_p$ is the specific heat at constant pressure.

### Step 2 — Express in Terms of Temperatures and Pressure Ratio
Using the isentropic relation for an ideal gas $T_2 / T_1 = (P_2 / P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$:

$$ T_2 = T_1 \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} $$

Substitute:

$$ W_s = c_p T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$

### Step 3 — Convert $c_p$ to Specific Gas Constant Form
Using the Mayer relation $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{H_2}$:

$$ W_s = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R_{H_2} T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$

### Step 4 — Insert Numerical Values
Given: $\gamma = 1.41$, $R_{H_2} = 4124 \; \text{J·kg}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2/P_1 = 80/20 = 4$, $\eta_{isen} = 0.82$.

$$ \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{1.41 - 1}{1.41} = \dfrac{0.41}{1.41} = 0.2908 $$

$$ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{0.2908} = 4^{0.2908} $$

Take $\ln$:

$$ \ln(4^{0.2908}) = 0.2908 \times \ln(4) = 0.2908 \times 1.3863 = 0.4031 $$

$$ 4^{0.2908} = e^{0.4031} = 1.4966 $$

$$ W_s = \dfrac{1.41}{0.41} \times 4124 \times 300 \times (1.4966 - 1) $$

$$ W_s = 3.439 \times 4124 \times 300 \times 0.4966 $$

$$ W_s = 3.439 \times 4124 \times 149.0 $$

$$ W_s = 3.439 \times 614{,}476 $$

$$ W_s = 2{,}113{,}524 \; \text{J/kg} \approx 2113.5 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$

### Step 5 — Apply Isentropic Efficiency
Actual work required (higher than isentropic):

$$ W_{actual} = \dfrac{W_s}{\eta_{isen}} = \dfrac{2113.5}{0.82} = 2577.4 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$

### Step 6 — Apply Mechanical Efficiency to Get Shaft Power

$$ P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot W_{actual}}{\eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 2577.4}{0.96} = 3221.8 \; \text{kW} \approx 3.22 \; \text{MW} $$

### Step 7 — Energy Cost per kg of H₂ Transmitted

$$ e_c = \dfrac{2577.4}{3600} = 0.716 \; \text{kWh/kg} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This represents **2.15 %** of LHV (33.33 kWh/kg) — relatively modest, which is why compression is preferred over liquefaction for medium distances.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pipeline Mass Throughput Using Darcy-Weisbach

**Problem Statement:** A dedicated hydrogen pipeline has diameter $D = 0.5$ m, length $L = 150$ km, operating at average pressure $\bar{P} = 70$ bar and temperature $T = 290$ K. The pressure drop across the section is $\Delta P = 30$ bar. The friction factor is $f_{Darcy} = 0.015$. Calculate the mass flow rate.

### Step 1 — Compute Hydrogen Density at Operating Conditions
Using ideal gas equation:

$$ \rho = \dfrac{P M}{R T} = \dfrac{\bar{P} \cdot M_{H_2}}{R_u \cdot T} $$

where $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol, $R_u = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $\bar{P} = 70 \times 10^5$ Pa, $T = 290$ K.

$$ \rho = \dfrac{70 \times 10^5 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 290} $$

$$ \rho = \dfrac{70 \times 10^5 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{2411.06} $$

$$ \rho = \dfrac{14112}{2411.06} = 5.853 \; \text{kg/m}^3 $$

### Step 2 — Apply Darcy-Weisbach Equation

$$ \Delta P = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2} $$

Solve for $v^2$:

$$ v^2 = \dfrac{2 \Delta P D}{f L \rho} $$

Substitute numerical values: $\Delta P = 30 \times 10^5$ Pa, $D = 0.5$ m, $L = 150 \times 10^3$ m, $f = 0.015$, $\rho = 5.853$ kg/m³.

$$ v^2 = \dfrac{2 \times 30 \times 10^5 \times 0.5}{0.015 \times 150 \times 10^3 \times 5.853} $$

$$ v^2 = \dfrac{30 \times 10^5}{0.015 \times 150 \times 10^3 \times 5.853} $$

$$ v^2 = \dfrac{3 \times 10^6}{13{,}169.25} = 227.85 \; \text{(m/s)}^2 $$

$$ v = \sqrt{227.85} = 15.10 \; \text{m/s} $$

### Step 3 — Calculate Mass Flow Rate

$$ \dot{m} = \rho A v = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v $$

$$ A = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.5^2}{4} = 0.1963 \; \text{m}^2 $$

$$ \dot{m} = 5.853 \times 0.1963 \times 15.10 $$

$$ \dot{m} = 17.36 \; \text{kg/s} $$

### Step 4 — Convert to Daily and Annual Throughput

$$ \dot{m}_{daily} = 17.36 \times 86400 = 1{,}499{,}904 \; \text{kg/day} \approx 1500 \; \text{tonnes/day} $$

$$ \dot{m}_{annual} = 1500 \times 365 = 547{,}500 \; \text{tonnes/year} \approx 0.55 \; \text{Mt/year} $$

### Step 5 — Energy Equivalent Delivered

$$ E_{daily} = 1500 \times 10^3 \times 120 \; \text{MJ/kg} = 1.8 \times 10^8 \; \text{MJ/day} $$

$$ E_{daily} = 50{,}000 \; \text{MWh/day} \quad \text{or} \quad 50 \; \text{GWh/day} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This single 0.5 m pipeline segment can deliver energy equivalent to roughly **5 million litres of petrol per day** — illustrating why pipelines are the *only* feasible option at scale.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Energy Cost for LH₂ Liquefaction

**Problem Statement:** Compare the energy penalty of compressing 1 kg of H₂ to 70 bar versus liquefying it to LH₂.

### Step A — Compression to 70 bar from 1 bar
Using our earlier formula with $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2/P_1 = 70$:

$$ \left(70\right)^{0.2908} = e^{0.2908 \times \ln 70} = e^{0.2908 \times 4.2485} = e^{1.2354} = 3.439 $$

$$ W_s = 3.439 \times 4124 \times 300 \times (3.439 - 1) $$

$$ W_s = 3.439 \times 4124 \times 300 \times 2.439 $$

$$ W_s = 3.439 \times 4124 \times 731.7 = 3.439 \times 3{,}017{,}950 $$

$$ W_s = 10.38 \; \text{MJ/kg} $$

Accounting for $\eta_{isen} = 0.82, \eta_{mech} = 0.95$:

$$ e_{comp} = \dfrac{10.38}{0.82 \times 0.95 \times 3.6} = 3.70 \; \text{kWh/kg} $$

As % of LHV (33.33 kWh/kg): **11.1 %**

### Step B — Liquefaction Energy
Standard industrial figure: **10–13 kWh/kg LH₂**.

Using 12 kWh/kg: **36.0 % of LHV**.

### Step C — Comparative Summary

| Method | Energy Penalty [kWh/kg] | % of LHV |
|---|---|---|
| Compression 1 → 70 bar | 3.7 | 11.1 % |
| Compression 1 → 100 bar | 4.2 | 12.6 % |
| **LH₂ Liquefaction** | **10–13** | **30–39 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Compression is energetically far cheaper than liquefaction. LH₂ is justified *only* when sea/road transport over long distances (no pipeline available) makes the alternative more expensive.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selection of Transmission Mode

| Criterion | Gaseous Pipeline | LH₂ Shipping | NH₃ Carrier | LOHC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Best Distance Range | 100 – 2000 km | > 2000 km (sea) | > 1000 km | 1000 – 10000 km |
| Energy Penalty | **8–12 %** | 30–40 % | 25–35 % | 35–45 % |
| CapEx (per kg·km) | Low (after pipe laid) | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| Maturity (TRL) | **9** | 7 | 9 (NH₃ itself) | 6–7 |
| Boil-off / Loss | Negligible | 0.05–0.3 %/day | < 0.1 %/day | < 0.01 %/day |
| Existing Infrastructure | Partial (NG repurposed) | Limited | Excellent | None |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define long-distance hydrogen transmission. List the four principal pathways used for it.**

**Model Answer:**
Long-distance hydrogen transmission refers to the bulk movement of hydrogen over extended distances (typically **>100 km**) from centralised production facilities to demand centres such as refineries, fertiliser plants, mobility stations, and industrial users. The four principal pathways are:

1. **Compressed gaseous hydrogen pipelines** (50–100 bar).
2. **Liquid hydrogen (LH₂) cryogenic shipping** (−253 °C).
3. **Ammonia (NH₃) as hydrogen carrier** (−33 °C or 9 bar).
4. **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)** (ambient conditions).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is hydrogen embrittlement and why is it critical in pipeline transmission?**

**Model Answer:**
Hydrogen embrittlement (HE) is the loss of ductility, toughness, and tensile strength of a metal caused by atomic hydrogen diffusing into its crystal lattice, leading to crack initiation and brittle fracture under stress. It is critical in pipeline transmission because:

- Hydrogen molecules dissociate at pipe walls and penetrate the steel.
- High-strength steels (especially martensitic) are highly susceptible.
- Failures can occur at operating stresses well below yield.
- Mitigation requires **low-carbon austenitic stainless steels, PE liners, controlled hardness (≤ HRC 22)**, and special welding procedures (low hydrogen electrodes).

---

## 📘 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With neat sketches, describe the four main pathways of long-distance hydrogen transmission and state their typical distance ranges and energy penalties.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A 0.4 m diameter hydrogen pipeline of length 120 km operates at an average pressure of 60 bar and temperature 290 K. The pressure drop across the section is 25 bar. The Darcy friction factor is 0.018. Calculate:
(i) The hydrogen density.
(ii) The flow velocity.
(iii) The mass flow rate in kg/s.
(iv) The annual energy delivered in GWh, assuming LHV = 120 MJ/kg.

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) Solution:
*(The four pathways are described as in Section 2.1 of these notes. Each pathway is to be explained with a block diagram and key characteristics.)*

| Pathway | Distance Range | Energy Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| Gaseous Pipeline | 100 – 2000 km | 8 – 12 % |
| LH₂ Shipping | > 2000 km | 30 – 40 % |
| NH₃ Carrier | > 1000 km | 25 – 35 % |
| LOHC | 1000 – 10000 km | 35 – 45 % |

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step (i) — Density at 60 bar, 290 K:**

$$ \rho = \dfrac{P M}{R T} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^5 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{8.314 \times 290} = \dfrac{12096}{2411.06} = 5.017 \; \text{kg/m}^3 $$

**Step (ii) — Velocity from Darcy-Weisbach:**

$$ v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P D}{f L \rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 25 \times 10^5 \times 0.4}{0.018 \times 120 \times 10^3 \times 5.017}} $$

$$ v = \sqrt{\dfrac{20 \times 10^5}{10836.7}} = \sqrt{184.56} = 13.59 \; \text{m/s} $$

**Step (iii) — Mass Flow Rate:**

$$ \dot{m} = \rho \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} v = 5.017 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 0.16}{4} \times 13.59 $$

$$ \dot{m} = 5.017 \times 0.1257 \times 13.59 = 8.566 \; \text{kg/s} $$

**Step (iv) — Annual Energy Delivered:**

$$ \dot{m}_{annual} = 8.566 \times 86400 \times 365 = 2.701 \times 10^{8} \; \text{kg/year} $$

$$ E_{annual} = 2.701 \times 10^{8} \times 120 \; \text{MJ} = 3.241 \times 10^{10} \; \text{MJ} $$

Convert to GWh (1 GWh = 3600 × 10⁶ MJ = 3.6 × 10⁹ MJ):

$$ E_{annual} = \dfrac{3.241 \times 10^{10}}{3.6 \times 10^{9}} = 9.00 \; \text{GWh/year} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for the isentropic work required to compress hydrogen from pressure $P_1$ to $P_2$ in a multi-stage reciprocating compressor with intercooling, assuming ideal gas behaviour.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Hydrogen is to be compressed from 1 bar, 300 K to 200 bar in a 3-stage reciprocating compressor with complete intercooling back to 300 K. Calculate:
(i) The optimum pressure ratio per stage.
(ii) The isentropic work per kg of H₂.
(iii) The total compressor power for a flow rate of 0.5 kg/s. Assume $\gamma = 1.41$, $\eta_{isen} = 0.80$.

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation:

For an ideal gas undergoing isentropic compression in one stage:

$$ W_s = c_p T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$

For an $N$-stage compressor with complete intercooling (back to inlet temperature $T_1$), each stage has the same inlet temperature, and total work is:

$$ W_{total} = N \cdot c_p T_1 \left[ r^{\frac{\gamma-1}{N \gamma}} - 1 \right] $$

where $r = P_N / P_1$ is the overall pressure ratio and $r_p = r^{1/N}$ is the per-stage ratio. Minimising $W_{total}$ with respect to $N$ yields the **optimum condition $r_p = r^{1/N}$**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution:

**Step (i) — Optimum pressure ratio per stage:**

$$ r_p = r^{1/N} = 200^{1/3} $$

$$ \ln(r_p) = \dfrac{\ln(200)}{3} = \dfrac{5.2983}{3} = 1.7661 $$

$$ r_p = e^{1.7661} = 5.848 \approx 5.85 $$

**Step (ii) — Isentropic work per kg:**

Each stage: $W_1 = c_p T_1 (r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1)$

Compute per-stage pressure ratio term:

$$ r_p^{0.2908} = 5.848^{0.2908} $$

$$ \ln(5.848^{0.2908}) = 0.2908 \times \ln(5.848) = 0.2908 \times 1.7661 = 0.5135 $$

$$ 5.848^{0.2908} = e^{0.5135} = 1.671 $$

$$ c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R_{H_2}}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{1.41 \times 4124}{0.41} = 14181 \; \text{J/kg·K} $$

$$ W_1 = 14181 \times 300 \times (1.671 - 1) = 14181 \times 300 \times 0.671 $$

$$ W_1 = 2.854 \times 10^6 \; \text{J/kg} = 2854 \; \text{kJ/kg per stage} $$

Total for 3 stages:

$$ W_{total} = 3 \times 2854 = 8562 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$

**Step (iii) — Compressor power (actual):**

$$ W_{actual} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{\eta_{isen}} = \dfrac{8562}{0.80} = 10702.5 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$

$$ P = \dot{m} \cdot W_{actual} = 0.5 \times 10702.5 = 5351.25 \; \text{kW} \approx 5.35 \; \text{MW} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply isentropic efficiency** — students often quote the ideal work as the final answer, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $R_u = 8.314$ instead of $R_{H_2} = 4124$ J/kg·K** in compressor formulas without converting mass ↔ moles correctly.
> 3. **Wrong density formula** in pipeline problems — students forget to convert pressure in bar to Pa (multiply by $10^5$).
> 4. **Missing the **inter-stage pressure ratio** step** in multi-stage problems — KTU specifically expects $r_p = r^{1/N}$.
> 5. **Not commenting on hydrogen embrittlement** when discussing pipeline materials — at least one sentence is expected for full marks.
> 6. **Mixing up LHV (120 MJ/kg) and HHV (142 MJ/kg)** in energy calculations.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Long-distance H₂ transmission = bulk transport of H₂ over >100 km via pipelines, LH₂ ships, NH₃ carriers, or LOHC.
- **Four Pathways:** Gaseous Pipeline, LH₂, NH₃, LOHC — each with different distance ranges and energy penalties.
- **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg (33.33 kWh/kg)** — benchmark energy unit.
- **Isentropic compression work:** $W_s = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R_{H_2} T_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$, $\gamma = 1.41$.
- **Pipeline throughput:** mass flow depends on $\sqrt{\Delta P \cdot D^5 / (f L \rho)}$; larger diameters exponentially improve capacity.
- **Hydrogen Embrittlement:** critical material challenge — use austenitic stainless steels, PE liners, or low-hardness linepipe steels.
- **LH₂ liquefaction energy = 30–40 % of LHV** — used only for sea transport or where pipelines infeasible.
- **NH₃ carries 17.65 % H₂ by mass** — uses existing global infrastructure (fertiliser industry).
- **LOHC:** chemically bound H₂; ambient transport; dehydrogenation at destination.
- **Optimum inter-stage pressure ratio** in N-stage compressors: $r_p = r^{1/N}$ minimises total work.
- **Re-numbering of constants:** always convert bar → Pa ($1$ bar $= 10^5$ Pa) and MJ → kWh (÷ 3.6) in numerical answers.
- **For KTU:** Show units at every step, draw a labelled block diagram, and explicitly state assumptions (ideal gas, isentropic, steady state).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation via Road
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transportation via Road

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Transportation of hydrogen via road** refers to the delivery of hydrogen from production sites (or large storage terminals) to end-users such as refueling stations, industrial consumers, and power generation plants using road-based vehicles. This is currently the **most widely deployed and commercially mature method** of hydrogen distribution, especially for distances typically up to **200–300 km** from the point of production. Road transport primarily occurs in three physical states:

1. **Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)** — Most common, transported at **200–700 bar** in specialized tube trailers.
2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** — Transported at cryogenic temperatures (**−253 °C / 20 K**) in super-insulated cryogenic tankers.
3. **Hydrogen in bonded/adsorbed forms** (Metal Hydrides, LOHCs — Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers) — Emerging niche road-transport options.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Road transport is the current backbone of the early hydrogen economy. According to the Hydrogen Council and IEA, **over 90% of the hydrogen currently moved globally** travels by road in some form, primarily because pipeline infrastructure is still being built out.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of hydrogen road transportation like the **milk-delivery model** of the 20th century: instead of building pipelines from every dairy farm to every household, milk was collected in large insulated tankers (rail or road) and delivered to local dairies or directly to homes. Hydrogen road transport works the same way — hydrogen is produced in **centralized, large-scale plants** (analogous to dairy farms) and delivered to **distributed users** (analogous to homes) by **specialized trucks**, bypassing the need for an extensive pipeline network in the early stages.

- **CGH₂ trailers** = like transporting pressurized CO₂ cylinders for soft drinks.
- **LH₂ tankers** = like transporting Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂) for hospitals — cryogenic, heavily insulated, low-pressure.
- **LOHC trucks** = like transporting oil in standard tankers, with hydrogen chemically "dissolved" and released later at the destination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (Industry Standard):** A single modern CGH₂ tube trailer (Type IV, 700 bar) can deliver approximately **400–1,100 kg of hydrogen per trip**, depending on tube configuration and pressure. An LH₂ tanker can carry up to **4,000–5,000 kg H₂ per trip**.

### Why Road Transport Matters in the Hydrogen Value Chain

| Stage | Role of Road Transport |
|---|---|
| **Production to Terminal** | Trunk movement of bulk H₂ from electrolyzer/SMR plant to storage hubs |
| **Terminal to Refueling Station** | Last-mile distribution to HRS (Hydrogen Refueling Stations) |
| **Refueling Station to End-User** | Cylinder/bundle delivery for industrial use |
| **Industrial Captive Use** | Direct delivery to factories (steel, ammonia, refineries) |

Road transport acts as the **flexible, modular "arterial system"** of the hydrogen economy — much like how trucks dominate goods movement today.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂) Road Transport

#### Operating Principle
Hydrogen gas is **mechanically compressed** using multi-stage compressors and stored in high-pressure cylinders mounted on a road trailer. The trailers are designed to ASME/CGA/ISO standards for high-pressure gas transport.

#### Pressure Cascade
Modern CGH₂ transport operates at three standard pressure levels:

| Pressure Level | Use Case | Energy Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| **200 bar (20 MPa)** | Legacy, lower-cost trailers | Low |
| **350 bar (35 MPa)** | Standard for current Type III trailers | Moderate |
| **500–700 bar (50–70 MPa)** | High-capacity Type IV trailers | **High (≈10–15% of H₂ LHV lost in compression)** |

#### Trailer Types

**Type I:** All-metal (steel) cylinders, heavy, low pressure (≤200 bar). Cheapest, used historically.

**Type II:** Steel liner with glass-fiber overwrap (hoop-wound), ≤350 bar. Mid-weight.

**Type III:** Aluminum liner fully wrapped with carbon-fiber composite, ≤450 bar. Common for tube trailers.

**Type IV (State-of-the-Art):** Polymer (HDPE) liner fully overwrapped with carbon-fiber composite, capable of **700 bar** service. Lighter, larger volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type IV** dominates new CGH₂ transport fleets because it offers the **highest gravimetric density** (mass of H₂ per unit mass of storage system) among CGH₂ options.

#### Mass of Hydrogen Delivered (Workhorse Formula)

The payload of a CGH₂ trailer is calculated from the **Ideal Gas Law** with a real-gas compressibility correction:

$$ m_{H_2} = \frac{P \cdot V \cdot M_{H_2}}{Z \cdot R \cdot T} $$

Where:
- $P$ = storage pressure (Pa)
- $V$ = total internal volume of all tubes (m³)
- $M_{H_2}$ = molar mass of H₂ = **2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol**
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = temperature (K) — typically **298 K** (25 °C) at the start of delivery
- $Z$ = compressibility factor for H₂ (Z ≈ **1.10–1.30** at 700 bar and 298 K, due to non-ideal behavior)

> [!NOTE]
> **Compressibility Factor Trap:** Students often use Z = 1 (ideal gas) for H₂ calculations. This causes **significant errors** at high pressures. For 700 bar H₂ at 25 °C, the true Z is approximately **1.30**, meaning the real delivered mass is **~23% less** than ideal-gas prediction.

### 2.2 Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂) Road Transport

#### Operating Principle
Hydrogen is liquefied at the production plant by **cryogenic cooling below its critical temperature** (T_c = −240 °C / 33 K) and below its boiling point (T_b = −253 °C / 20.27 K). It is then loaded into a **vacuum-jacketed, super-insulated cryogenic tanker** for road transport.

#### Energy Cost of Liquefaction

$$ \eta_{liq} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{W_{liq}} $$

Where:
- $LHV_{H_2}$ = **120 MJ/kg** (Lower Heating Value of H₂)
- $W_{liq}$ = work input to liquefier, typically **10–13 kWh/kg** ≈ **36–47 MJ/kg**

Therefore:

$$ \eta_{liq} \approx \frac{120}{40} \approx 30\% $$

So liquefaction consumes roughly **30–40% of the energy content of the hydrogen**, which is a major drawback for LH₂ road transport.

#### Boil-Off Gas (BOG) — The Critical Operational Parameter

During transport, heat leaks into the cryogenic tank causing some LH₂ to vaporize. The **boil-off rate (BOR)** is governed by:

$$ \text{BOR} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{leak}}{L_{vap,H_2}} $$

Where:
- $\dot{Q}_{leak}$ = heat ingress rate through tank walls (W)
- $L_{vap,H_2}$ = latent heat of vaporization of H₂ at 20 K = **445 kJ/kg**

Modern vacuum-jacketed, multi-layer insulated (MLI) road tankers achieve:

$$ \text{BOR}_{LH_2} \approx 0.1\% \text{ to } 0.3\% \text{ per day} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For transport durations under 5–7 days, BOG losses are acceptable**. Beyond this, venting or reliquefaction is required.

#### Gravimetric and Volumetric Density Comparison

| Storage Mode | Gravimetric Density (kg H₂ / kg system) | Volumetric Density (kg H₂ / m³) |
|---|---|---|
| 700 bar CGH₂ (Type IV) | **0.05 – 0.08** | **40 – 45** |
| LH₂ (−253 °C) | **0.10 – 0.15** | **70 – 71** |
| 350 bar CGH₂ (Type III) | **0.03 – 0.04** | **20 – 25** |

LH₂ wins on **volumetric density** (by ~70%) and **gravimetric density** (by 2–3×), justifying its use for long-distance road transport despite liquefaction losses.

### 2.3 LOHC and Metal Hydride Road Transport (Emerging)

**LOHC (Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carrier):** Hydrogen is chemically bonded to an organic liquid (e.g., dibenzyltoluene — "H0-DBT" / "H18-DBT", or toluene / methylcyclohexane). It is loaded and unloaded via catalytic hydrogenation/dehydrogenation reactions. **Standard tank trucks can be used.**

**Metal Hydride:** Hydrogen is absorbed into a metal alloy (e.g., LaNi₅, TiFe, MgH₂) at moderate pressure and temperature, then released on demand by heating.

These are still at low TRL (Technology Readiness Level 4–6) for road transport but are the focus of pilot programs in Japan and Germany.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass of H₂ in CGH₂ trailer | $m = PVM / (ZRT)$ | kg |
| 2 | Compressibility factor Z for H₂ | Lookup from chart | ≈ 1.30 at 700 bar, 298 K |
| 3 | Liquefaction efficiency | $\eta_{liq} = LHV/W_{liq}$ | ≈ 30% |
| 4 | Boil-off rate | $\text{BOR} = \dot{Q}/L_{vap}$ | % per day |
| 5 | Latent heat of vaporization H₂ | $L_{vap}$ | 445 kJ/kg |
| 6 | Volumetric energy density LH₂ | $E_v = \rho_{LH_2} \cdot LHV$ | 8.5 GJ/m³ |
| 7 | Trailer payload (modern) | $m_{payload}$ | 400–1100 kg (CGH₂), 4000+ kg (LH₂) |
| 8 | LHV of H₂ | $LHV$ | 120 MJ/kg |
| 9 | HHV of H₂ | $HHV$ | 142 MJ/kg |
| 10 | Critical temperature H₂ | $T_c$ | 33 K (−240 °C) |
| 11 | Boiling point H₂ | $T_b$ | 20.27 K (−253 °C) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Board Exam:** Always quote **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** for H₂ unless the question explicitly says "Higher Heating Value." Mixing these is the most common arithmetic error in KTU answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mass of H₂ Delivered by a 700 bar Type IV CGH₂ Trailer

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A CGH₂ road trailer has **12 Type IV carbon-fiber composite tubes**, each of internal volume **200 liters**, operating at a delivery pressure of **700 bar** and temperature of **298 K**. Using a compressibility factor Z = 1.30, calculate the mass of hydrogen delivered. State any assumptions.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the governing equation.**

The mass of a real gas stored at high pressure is given by the real-gas equation of state:

$$ m = \frac{P \cdot V \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T} $$

**Step 2: Calculate the total internal volume of all tubes.**

$$ V_{total} = n_{tubes} \times V_{tube} $$

$$ V_{total} = 12 \times 200 \, \text{L} = 2400 \, \text{L} $$

Converting to SI units (1 m³ = 1000 L):

$$ V_{total} = \frac{2400}{1000} = 2.4 \, \text{m}^3 $$

**Step 3: Convert the delivery pressure to SI units (Pa).**

$$ P = 700 \, \text{bar} = 700 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} = 7.0 \times 10^7 \, \text{Pa} $$

**Step 4: Substitute the constant values.**

- Molar mass of H₂: $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kg/mol}$
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- Temperature: $T = 298 \, \text{K}$
- Compressibility factor: $Z = 1.30$

**Step 5: Plug values into the real-gas mass equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \frac{(7.0 \times 10^7) \times (2.4) \times (2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{1.30 \times 8.314 \times 298} \\
&= \frac{7.0 \times 10^7 \times 2.4 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{3218.0} \quad \text{(numerator assembled)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate the numerator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
7.0 \times 10^7 \times 2.4 &= 1.68 \times 10^8 \\
1.68 \times 10^8 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} &= 1.68 \times 2.016 \times 10^5 \\
&= 3.3869 \times 10^5 \quad \text{(numerator in J·kg/mol units)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Calculate the denominator.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.30 \times 8.314 \times 298 &= 1.30 \times 2477.572 \\
&= 3220.84 \quad \text{(approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Final division.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \frac{3.3869 \times 10^5}{3220.84} \\
&\approx 105.16 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Verify sanity-check range.**

For modern Type IV trailers operating at 700 bar, the typical per-trailer payload is in the range **400–1100 kg** (for larger fleets of 30+ tubes). For a 12-tube trailer, ~100 kg is consistent with the engineering reality of a small trailer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Allocation Insight:** The 7-mark sub-question on this derivation typically awards: 2 marks for the formula, 2 marks for unit conversion (bar→Pa, L→m³), 2 marks for substitution and final numerical answer, and 1 mark for the sanity check / conclusion.

### 3.2 Derivation: Boil-Off Loss for a 24-Hour LH₂ Road Trip

**Problem Statement:**
An LH₂ road tanker has a payload of **4,000 kg H₂** and a heat-leak rate of **150 W** through its vacuum insulation. Calculate the **percentage of payload lost** as boil-off gas (BOG) during a **24-hour delivery trip**. The latent heat of vaporization of H₂ is **445 kJ/kg**.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Total energy ingress over 24 hours.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 24 \, \text{h} = 24 \times 3600 = 86,400 \, \text{s} \\
Q_{total} &= \dot{Q}_{leak} \times t \\
Q_{total} &= 150 \, \text{W} \times 86,400 \, \text{s} \\
Q_{total} &= 1.296 \times 10^7 \, \text{J} = 12,960 \, \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Mass of LH₂ vaporized (boil-off).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{BOG} &= \frac{Q_{total}}{L_{vap,H_2}} \\
m_{BOG} &= \frac{12,960 \, \text{kJ}}{445 \, \text{kJ/kg}} \\
m_{BOG} &\approx 29.12 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Percentage loss of original payload.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Loss} &= \frac{m_{BOG}}{m_{payload}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{29.12}{4000} \times 100 \\
&\approx 0.728\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion.**

A 24-hour LH₂ road trip loses approximately **0.73%** of its payload, well within the operational envelope for short-haul cryogenic delivery.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Decision Table for Road Transport Mode

| Criterion | CGH₂ (200 bar) | CGH₂ (700 bar) | LH₂ | LOHC | Metal Hydride |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TRL (Technology Readiness)** | 9 (mature) | 8–9 | 9 | 5–6 | 4–5 |
| **Energy efficiency of loading** | 95–97% | 85–90% | 60–70% | 90–95% (release is lossier) | 80–85% |
| **Cost per kg H₂ delivered (USD)** | **1.0–2.0** | **2.0–4.0** | **3.0–5.0** | 4.0–6.0 (estimated) | 5.0–8.0 (estimated) |
| **Suitable delivery distance** | < 50 km | 50–300 km | 300–1000+ km | 200–800 km | < 100 km |
| **Specialized trailer needed** | Yes (tube trailer) | Yes (Type IV) | Yes (cryogenic) | **No (standard tanker)** | Yes (hydride bed) |
| **Refrigeration/cryogenics needed** | No | No | Yes (continuous) | No | No (heat on demand) |
| **Boil-off / loss in transit** | Negligible (pressure drop) | Negligible | **0.1–0.3%/day** | Negligible | Negligible |
| **Refueling station compatibility** | Direct (with booster) | Direct | Needs cryo-pump | Needs dehydrogenation reactor | Heat exchanger |
| **Regulatory framework** | Fully developed (ADR, DOT, ISO) | Fully developed | Fully developed | Emerging | Emerging |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The colors used are pure CSS-compatible (no markdown formatting) to ensure correct rendering in the KTU study platform. <!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`
**What are the three primary forms in which hydrogen is transported via road? Briefly state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three primary forms of hydrogen road transport are:

1. **Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂)** — stored at 200–700 bar in tube trailers. *Advantage:* Mature, simple technology with no cryogenic losses; widely used for short-to-medium hauls.

2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** — stored at −253 °C (20 K) in vacuum-jacketed cryogenic tankers. *Advantage:* Highest volumetric density (~71 kg/m³) enabling long-haul transport of large payloads (~4000–5000 kg per trip).

3. **LOHC (Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carrier)** — hydrogen chemically bonded to an organic liquid carrier such as dibenzyltoluene. *Advantage:* Can be transported in **standard, non-specialized tank trucks** at ambient conditions, with negligible evaporative losses.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`
**Why is a compressibility factor Z used in hydrogen mass calculations for CGH₂ transport, instead of the ideal gas law?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Hydrogen exhibits **significant non-ideal gas behavior** at high pressures (above ~100 bar). At 700 bar and 298 K, the real Z value for H₂ is approximately **1.30**, indicating strong deviations from ideal-gas behavior. If the ideal gas equation (Z = 1) is used, the predicted mass of hydrogen in a high-pressure trailer will be overestimated by ~23%. Hence, for accurate engineering and safety calculations in CGH₂ transport, the real-gas equation of state $PV = ZnRT$ with the appropriate Z is mandatory. The Z value depends on both **pressure and temperature** and is typically read from published H₂ compressibility charts or equations of state (e.g., the Leachman equation).

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂) Type IV road trailer** system. Clearly label the prime mover, tube bank, manifold, safety relief devices, and delivery interface. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CGH₂ road trailer consists of **18 Type IV carbon-fiber composite tubes**, each of internal volume **180 L**, operating at a delivery pressure of **500 bar** and a temperature of **300 K**. The compressibility factor Z for hydrogen at these conditions is **1.25**. Calculate:
- (i) The total volume of hydrogen gas stored. **(1 mark)**
- (ii) The mass of hydrogen delivered. **(4 marks)**
- (iii) The energy content of the delivered hydrogen in kWh. **(2 marks)**

Use: M = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg, 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture of CGH₂ Type IV Trailer (7 marks):**

```
Prime Mover (Diesel/EV Truck Tractor)
   │
   ├── Chassis Frame (High-strength steel, certified for hazardous load)
   │
   ├── Tube Bank: 12-30 Type IV cylinders
   │       • Each tube: HDPE (polymer) liner
   │       • Fully over-wrapped with carbon-fiber composite
   │       • Operating pressure: 350-700 bar
   │       • Mounted in cradle frames with anti-vibration pads
   │
   ├── Manifold Header (Stainless steel, welded)
   │       • Connects all tubes to a common high-pressure line
   │       • Equipped with isolation ball valves per tube
   │
   ├── Safety Subsystem
   │       • TPRD (Thermally Activated Pressure Relief Device)
   │       • Burst discs (set at ~110% of MAWP)
   │       • Pressure gauges (per tube + master)
   │       • Excess flow valves
   │
   ├── Delivery Interface
   │       • High-pressure hose reel
   │       • Quick-connect fittings (compatible with HRS)
   │       • Pneumatic / hydraulic control panel
   │
   ├── Auxiliary Systems
   │       • Reflective UN 1049 hazard placards
   │       • Electrostatic grounding reels
   │       • Fire suppression (dry chemical)
```

Mark allocation: 1 mark for prime-mover identification, 2 marks for tube bank details, 1 mark for manifold, 2 marks for safety devices, 1 mark for delivery interface & auxiliary systems.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total volume of stored gas (1 mark):**

$$ V_{total} = 18 \times 180 \, \text{L} = 3240 \, \text{L} = 3.24 \, \text{m}^3 $$

**(ii) Mass of hydrogen delivered (4 marks):**

Using the real-gas equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \frac{P \cdot V_{total} \cdot M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T} \\
&= \frac{(500 \times 10^5) \times 3.24 \times (2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{1.25 \times 8.314 \times 300} \\
&= \frac{5.0 \times 10^7 \times 3.24 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{3117.75} \\
&= \frac{3.2659 \times 10^5}{3117.75} \\
&\approx 104.75 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Energy content in kWh (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= m \cdot LHV = 104.75 \times 120 \, \text{MJ} = 12,570 \, \text{MJ} \\
E_{kWh} &= \frac{12,570}{3.6} \approx 3491.67 \, \text{kWh} \approx 3.49 \, \text{MWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Choice)**

`[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle, components, and operational workflow of liquid hydrogen (LH₂) road transport**. Discuss boil-off gas (BOG) management, latent heat considerations, and the typical liquefaction energy penalty. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LH₂ cryogenic road tanker carries a **payload of 3,500 kg** of liquid hydrogen. The vacuum-insulated double-walled tanker has a total **heat-leak rate of 120 W** to the inner vessel. Calculate:
- (i) The **total heat ingress in kJ** during a **36-hour intercity delivery trip**. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The **mass of boil-off gas (BOG)** produced. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The **percentage loss** of the original payload, and comment on whether the trip is economically acceptable. **(3 marks)**

Use: Latent heat of vaporization of H₂ = 445 kJ/kg.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Liquid Hydrogen Road Transport (7 marks):**

1. **Principle (1.5 marks):** Hydrogen is cooled below its critical temperature (T_c = 33 K) and below its boiling point (T_b = 20.27 K) using multi-stage cryogenic liquefaction. The liquid is then loaded into a **vacuum-jacketed road tanker** that maintains it at ~1 atm pressure and ~20 K by minimizing heat ingress.

2. **Components (2 marks):**
   - **Inner vessel:** Stainless steel, designed to ASME BPVC Section VIII.
   - **Outer jacket:** Carbon steel, evacuated to < 10⁻⁴ Pa.
   - **Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI):** 30–60 alternating layers of aluminized Mylar and fiberglass spacer.
   - **Cryogenic pump (submerged or external):** For offloading.
   - **BOG management system:** Pressure-buildup coil, economizer, vent stack with flame arrestor.
   - **Tanker chassis and prime mover.**

3. **Operational Workflow (1.5 marks):** Production → Liquefaction → Loading via cryogenic pump → Transit (BOG management) → Offloading at customer → Vaporization (if needed) → End use.

4. **Liquefaction Energy Penalty (1 mark):** Liquefaction consumes ~10–13 kWh/kg, equivalent to ~30–40% of H₂'s lower heating value. Hence LH₂ transport is energy-inefficient but justified for long distances and large payloads.

5. **BOG Management (1 mark):** BOG can be (a) vented (acceptable for short trips), (b) reliquefied using a small recondenser at the receiving terminal, or (c) used as fuel for the prime-mover engine (hydrogen ICE / FC truck).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total heat ingress in 36 hours (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 36 \, \text{h} = 36 \times 3600 = 1,29,600 \, \text{s} \\
Q_{total} &= 120 \times 1,29,600 = 1.5552 \times 10^7 \, \text{J} = 15,552 \, \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Mass of BOG (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{BOG} &= \frac{Q_{total}}{L_{vap,H_2}} = \frac{15,552}{445} \\
&\approx 34.95 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage loss and economic comment (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Loss} &= \frac{34.95}{3500} \times 100 \approx 0.999\% \approx 1.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Economic Comment:** A ~1% loss over a 36-hour trip is **well within the industry-accepted BOG envelope** (typically < 2% per 7-day transit). The trip is **economically acceptable**, and no reliquefaction is required. The lost H₂ represents an energy loss of about $34.95 \times 120 = 4,194$ MJ, which at industrial hydrogen prices (~USD 5–7 per kg) corresponds to a value of about **USD 175–245** — manageable as a transport overhead.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting Z in CGH₂ calculations** is the #1 mark-losing mistake. At 700 bar, Z ≈ 1.30, NOT 1. Always include Z in your formula and state its value.
> 2. **Using HHV (142 MJ/kg) instead of LHV (120 MJ/kg)** in energy calculations. Default to LHV unless the question explicitly states HHV. Mixing them is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Always convert bar → Pa (×10⁵) and liters → m³ (÷1000) before plugging into SI equations.
> 4. **Confusing CGH₂ transport pressure with refueling station pressure:** Tubes deliver at 500–700 bar; HRS dispenses at 700 bar; vehicle tanks store at 700 bar. These are not the same as production pressure (typically 30–80 bar).
> 5. **In derivation questions, do NOT skip the substitution step.** Even if the calculation is trivial, write it out — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the substitution step.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Road transport is the current backbone of the H₂ economy**, accounting for >90% of all H₂ moved globally.
- **Three primary modes:** CGH₂ (compressed gas, 200–700 bar), LH₂ (cryogenic liquid, 20 K), LOHC (chemical bonding in organic carrier).
- **CGH₂ Type IV trailers** are the workhorse for short-to-medium hauls (< 300 km); use the **real-gas equation** with Z ≈ 1.30 at 700 bar.
- **LH₂ tankers** dominate long-haul transport (> 300 km) with payloads up to 4,000–5,000 kg, but incur **30–40% liquefaction energy loss**.
- **BOG** for LH₂ is governed by $m_{BOG} = \dot{Q} \cdot t / L_{vap}$; modern tankers achieve **0.1–0.3%/day** boil-off.
- **Key physical constants:** LHV = 120 MJ/kg, HHV = 142 MJ/kg, T_b = 20.27 K, T_c = 33 K, L_vap = 445 kJ/kg.
- **Safety devices on trailers:** TPRD, burst discs, excess-flow valves, electrostatic grounding, fire suppression, UN 1049 placards.
- **Emerging modes:** LOHC (TRL 5–6) and metal hydrides (TRL 4–5); not yet competitive at scale.
- **Decision rule of thumb:** Short distance & small volume → 200 bar CGH₂; medium distance & medium volume → 700 bar CGH₂; long distance & large volume → LH₂; ambient transport preferred → LOHC.
- **Cost ranking (per kg H₂ delivered):** 200 bar CGH₂ < 700 bar CGH₂ < LH₂ < LOHC < Metal Hydride.
- **Standard codes & regulations to remember:** UN 1049 (H₂ classification), ISO 19881 (Gaseous H₂ land vehicle containers), ADR (European road transport), DOT 49 CFR (US), ASME BPVC Section VIII (vessel design).
- **For KTU board exams:** Always show unit conversions, always state Z explicitly, and always use LHV unless told otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ships and in Form of Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ships and in Form of Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)** are a class of organic liquids that can reversibly absorb and release hydrogen through catalytic hydrogenation and dehydrogenation reactions. In maritime (ship-based) hydrogen transport, LOHC systems enable the bulk shipping of hydrogen in a chemically bound liquid form that is stable at ambient temperature and pressure, eliminating the need for cryogenic tanks.

For the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3 – Storage and Transportation), the standard LOHC couples are:

| LOHC Couple | Abbreviation | Reversible Capacity |
|-------------|--------------|--------------------|
| Toluene ⇌ Methylcyclohexane | TOL / MCH | **6.16 wt%** |
| Dibenzyltoluene ⇌ perhydro-DBT | DBT / H18-DBT | **6.20 wt%** |
| N-ethylcarbazole ⇌ H12-NEC | NEC / H12-NEC | **5.79 wt%** |
| Bicyclohexyl ⇌ Biphenyl | BCH / BP | **7.20 wt%** (theoretical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LOHC transport via ships is treated as a *chemical-bound transport medium*, fundamentally different from liquid H₂ (LH₂) cryogenic transport and NH₃ transport. The carrier liquid itself is recycled, making LOHC a "**hydrogen delivery service**" rather than a one-shot fuel.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are sending a glass of water across the ocean without a bottle. You could freeze it (LH₂), dissolve it in a sponge (metal hydrides), or—most cleverly—**chemically bind the water molecules to a solid liquid carrier that you can pump through a normal pipeline or pour into a normal ship tank**. When it arrives, you "unzip" the carrier to release the water, and the empty carrier is sent back for reuse.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "LOHC Diaper" Analogy:** Toluene is the "diaper," and H₂ is the "absorbed content." The diaper can be shipped worldwide, handled with gloves, dropped, heated, and stored in any standard chemical tanker. At the destination, the diaper is "washed" (heated to ~300–400 °C) to recover pure H₂, and the clean diaper is shipped back. There is **no boil-off, no cryogenic insulation, and no high-pressure vessel** required for the bulk leg of the journey.

## 1.3 Why Ships? Why LOHC?

The world does not produce and consume hydrogen in the same places. The cheapest renewable electricity is in Patagonia, Western Australia, North Africa, the Middle East, and Patagonia. The largest demand is in industrial Europe, Japan, and South Korea. Therefore, **trans-oceanic hydrogen shipping** is inevitable, and LOHC is one of the most credible maritime vectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics for Ship-Based LOHC**
> - Operating storage state: Ambient T, near-atmospheric P (≈ 1 bar)
> - Bulk energy density (MCH): **~ 5.6 MJ/L** (comparable to gasoline)
> - Carrier reusability: **> 200 cycles** without significant degradation
> - Flammability class: Same as conventional diesel/oil cargo (handled under IGC/IGF code)
> - Round-trip H₂ loss: **< 0.1 wt% per cycle** (chemical stability)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Chemistry (Hydrogenation ↔ Dehydrogenation)

The reference LOHC system is the **toluene / methylcyclohexane (TOL / MCH)** pair. The reaction is:

$$\text{C}_7\text{H}_8 \;+\; 3\text{H}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{C}_7\text{H}_{14}$$

- **Forward (hydrogenation):** exothermic, $\Delta H \approx -205\ \text{kJ/mol H}_2$ (per mole of H₂, i.e., per 2 g)
- **Reverse (dehydrogenation):** endothermic, $\Delta H \approx +205\ \text{kJ/mol H}_2$

A small amount of the heat released during hydrogenation at the production site can offset the dehydrogenation heat demand at the destination, but in practice this heat must be supplied externally (process steam, electric heating, or a small amount of the released H₂ burned).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **At the production site (e.g., solar/wind hub):**
   - Produce H₂ via electrolysis
   - Pump H₂ into a catalytic hydrogenation reactor (Ni or Pt-based) at 150–200 °C, 10–50 bar
   - Toluene is loaded and unloaded continuously; the product MCH is a stable, non-toxic liquid
2. **Maritime transport (the ship leg):**
   - MCH is loaded into a chemical tanker (MCH is already an existing petrochemical — it is a common solvent, gasoline blendstock, and a jet fuel component)
   - Ship sails at ambient conditions — **no boil-off, no cryogenic tank, no pressure vessel**
   - Arrives at the destination port
3. **At the destination (dehydrogenation hub):**
   - MCH is heated to 300–400 °C over a Pt/Al₂O₃ (or Pt/C) catalyst
   - The released H₂ is purified (typically to > 99.97 %)
   - The empty carrier (toluene) is shipped back to the production site for reloading
4. **Hydrogen delivery to end-user:**
   - Refineries (desulfurization), ammonia plants, fuel-cell vehicles, steel (DRI), or grid injection

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All values below use standard SI units unless noted. Symbols use $\vert$ for absolute value (e.g., $\vert \Delta H \vert$ for the magnitude of reaction enthalpy).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Molecular weight of MCH (C₇H₁₄) | $M_{MCH} = 7(12) + 14(1)$ | **98 g/mol** |
| 2 | Molecular weight of toluene (C₇H₈) | $M_{TOL} = 7(12) + 8(1)$ | **92 g/mol** |
| 3 | Mass of H₂ absorbed per mole of toluene | $\Delta M = 3 \times 2$ | **6 g/mol** |
| 4 | Gravimetric H₂ capacity of MCH | $w_{H_2} = \dfrac{6}{98} \times 100\%$ | **6.12 wt%** |
| 5 | Density of MCH at 20 °C | $\rho_{MCH}$ | **770 kg/m³** |
| 6 | Volumetric H₂ density in MCH | $\rho_{V,H_2} = w_{H_2} \cdot \rho_{MCH}$ | **~ 47.1 kg H₂/m³** |
| 7 | Mass of H₂ in a cargo of mass $m_{c}$ | $m_{H_2} = w_{H_2} \cdot m_{c}$ | kg |
| 8 | Energy in H₂ cargo (LHV) | $E = m_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}$ | J (LHV = 120 MJ/kg) |
| 9 | Reaction enthalpy per mole H₂ | $\vert \Delta H \vert$ | **205 kJ/mol H₂** |
| 10 | Round-trip energy efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{hyd} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{dehyd}$ | **~ 65–75 %** |
| 11 | Energy in 1 m³ of MCH | $E_V = \rho_{V,H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}$ | **~ 5.65 GJ/m³** |
| 12 | Required heat for full dehydrogenation | $Q = m_{H_2} \cdot \dfrac{\vert \Delta H \vert}{M_{H_2}}$ | J |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

LOHC ship transport is being commercialized by:

- **Hydrogenious LOHC Technologies (Germany)** – DBT-based system, partner of Shell, Mabanaft
- **Chiyoda Corporation (Japan)** – MCH-based "SPERA Hydrogen" technology, with the Japan-Australia **HySTRA** demo
- **Hyzon Motors & партнеры** – pilot maritime fuel-cell applications

These projects position LOHC as the **dominant hydrogen export vector from Australia and the Middle East to Japan, Korea, and Northern Europe** during the 2025–2035 transition window.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because MCH is a registered petrochemical, ports like Rotterdam, Singapore, and Yokohama already have loading/unloading infrastructure, fire-fighting systems, and IGC-code expertise. This is a major CAPEX advantage over LH₂, which requires entirely new cryogenic terminals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gravimetric and Volumetric Hydrogen Capacity of MCH

**Given:**
- Toluene: C₇H₈, $M_{TOL} = 92\ \text{g/mol}$
- Methylcyclohexane: C₇H₁₄, $M_{MCH} = 98\ \text{g/mol}$
- Each toluene molecule absorbs **3 molecules of H₂**

**Step 1 — Identify the absorbed hydrogen mass.**
One mole of toluene takes up 3 moles of H₂:

$$n_{H_2,\,absorbed} = 3\ \text{mol per mole of toluene}$$

The mass of H₂ absorbed is:

$$m_{H_2} = n_{H_2} \cdot M_{H_2} = 3 \times 2\ \text{g/mol} = 6\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the molecular weight of the loaded carrier (MCH).**

$$M_{MCH} = M_{TOL} + m_{H_2} = 92 + 6 = 98\ \text{g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gravimetric H₂ capacity $w_{H_2}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
w_{H_2} &= \frac{m_{H_2}}{M_{MCH}} \times 100\% \\
        &= \frac{6}{98} \times 100\% \\
        &= 6.122\ \text{wt\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the volumetric H₂ density using $\rho_{MCH} = 770\ \text{kg/m}^3$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{V,\,H_2} &= w_{H_2} \cdot \rho_{MCH} \\
              &= 0.06122 \times 770\ \text{kg/m}^3 \\
              &= 47.14\ \text{kg H}_2\text{/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **about 67 % of the H₂ density in liquid hydrogen (70.8 kg/m³)**, but at ambient conditions — a remarkable trade-off for ship transport.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydrogen Payload in a 100,000-DWT MCH Carrier

A typical Suezmax-class chemical tanker carries ~ 100,000 tonnes of MCH cargo. We want the equivalent mass of H₂ and the energy it represents.

**Step 1 — Mass of H₂ in cargo.**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= w_{H_2} \cdot m_{cargo} \\
        &= 0.06122 \times 100{,}000\ \text{tonnes} \\
        &= 6{,}122\ \text{tonnes of H}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to energy (LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg = 120 GJ/tonne).**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 6{,}122\ \text{tonnes} \times 120\ \text{GJ/tonne} \\
  &= 734{,}640\ \text{GJ} \\
  &= 7.346 \times 10^{14}\ \text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

In more familiar units:

$$E = \frac{734{,}640}{3.6}\ \text{GWh} \approx 204{,}067\ \text{MWh} \approx 204\ \text{GWh}$$

This single ship can deliver enough energy to power **~ 24,000 average European households for one year**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Penalty of Dehydrogenation

**Step 1 — Heat required per kilogram of H₂ released.**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{DH} &= \frac{\vert \Delta H \vert}{M_{H_2}} \\
       &= \frac{205{,}000\ \text{J/mol}}{2{,}000\ \text{g/mol}} \\
       &= 10.25\ \text{kJ/g} \\
       &= 10.25\ \text{MJ/kg H}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the LHV of H₂.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{DH,\,th} &= \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{LHV_{H_2} + q_{DH}} \\
              &= \frac{120}{120 + 10.25} \\
              &= 0.9214 \;\;(92.14\%)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Include realistic losses (heat integration, side reactions, irreversibilities, heating from T_amb to 400 °C).**

A practical de-hydrogenation reactor achieves:

$$\eta_{DH,\,practical} \approx 0.80 - 0.85$$

**Step 4 — Combine with hydrogenation (~ 88 %) and transport losses (~ 99.5 %).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{RT} &= \eta_{hyd} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{dehyd,\,practical} \\
         &= 0.88 \times 0.995 \times 0.82 \\
         &\approx 0.718 \;\;(71.8\%)
\end{aligned}$$

This means **~ 28 % of the original H₂ energy** is consumed by the LOHC cycle. This is the central "tax" the system pays, and it is the key number any KTU problem will test.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LOHC vs LH₂ vs NH₃ (for ship transport)

| Criterion | MCH (LOHC) | LH₂ | NH₃ |
|----------|------------|-----|-----|
| Storage state | Liquid, ambient | Cryogenic liquid, −253 °C | Liquid, −33 °C, 9 bar |
| Volumetric H₂ density (kg/m³) | **47** | 70.8 | 121 |
| Boil-off | None | ~ 0.1–0.3 %/day | < 0.01 %/day |
| Carrier reuse | Yes (toluene back-haul) | N/A | Cracked to H₂ + N₂ |
| Toxicity | Low (petrochemical) | None | High |
| Round-trip efficiency | **~ 70 %** | ~ 92 % (just liquefaction) | ~ 60–70 % (cracking) |
| Existing port infra | **Yes** | No | Partial |
| Energy per m³ cargo | 5.6 GJ | 8.5 GJ | 14.5 GJ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Analytical Takeaway:** LOHC is the **logistics-easiest** hydrogen ship transport mode (no new terminals), but it has a **higher energy penalty** than LH₂. NH₃ has the highest volumetric density but requires a dedicated cracking reactor and carries significant toxicity risk.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: LOHC Ship-Based Hydrogen Transport Chain

The following flowchart maps the entire functional architecture — from renewable electricity at the production site to the end consumer — emphasizing the **reversible carrier loop** that is the defining feature of LOHC.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Renewable Power Source] --> A2[Electrolyzer H2 Production]
    A2 --> A3[Hydrogenation Reactor TOL to MCH 150-200 C]
    A3 --> A4[MCH Storage Tank Ambient]
    A4 --> A5[Port Loading Terminal]

    subgraph SHIP[Maritime Transport Loop]
        B1[Chemical Tanker MCH Bulk] --> B2[In-Transit Ambient Conditions]
        B2 --> B3[Destination Port Unloading]
    end

    A5 --> B1
    B3 --> C1[Dehydrogenation Reactor 300-400 C]
    C1 --> C2[H2 Purification 99.97 Percent]
    C2 --> C3[H2 End User Industrial Mobility Power]

    C1 --> D1[Toluene Recovered Empty Carrier]
    D1 --> D2[Toluene Storage Tank]
    D2 --> D3[Tanker Back-Haul to Origin]
    D3 --> A5

    style SHIP fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#90EE90
    style C3 fill:#FFD700
    style D1 fill:#FFA07A
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The LOHC Reactor Pair

This diagram shows the *process engineering view* of how the hydrogenation and dehydrogenation reactors are coupled to the ship's cargo and the end-user H₂ distribution grid.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROD[Production Hub]
        P1[Electrolysis H2 Stream] --> P2[Compressor 30 bar]
        P2 --> P3[Heat Exchanger Pre-Heat TOL]
        P3 --> P4[Hydrogenation Reactor Ni Catalyst 180 C]
        P4 --> P5[Condenser and Stabilizer]
        P5 --> P6[MCH Storage Sphere 20000 m3]
    end

    subgraph SHIP2[Ship Transport]
        S1[Cargo Tank 1 MCH 50000 m3] --> S2[At-Transit Sensors T P level]
        S2 --> S3[Cargo Tank 2 MCH 50000 m3]
    end

    P6 --> S1
    S3 --> DEST

    subgraph DEST[Destination Hub]
        DEST1[MCH Pre-Heater 250 C] --> DEST2[Evaporator 350 C]
        DEST2 --> DEST3[Dehydrogenation Reactor Pt on Al2O3]
        DEST3 --> DEST4[H2 PSA Purification 99.97 percent]
        DEST4 --> DEST5[Compressed H2 Storage 350 bar]
        DEST5 --> DEST6[Pipeline or Tube Trailer to Customer]
        DEST3 --> R1[Toluene Cooler 60 C]
        R1 --> R2[Toluene Storage Tank 20000 m3]
        R2 --> R3[Return Tanker Loading]
    end

    R3 --> P3

    style PROD fill:#e0f7e0
    style SHIP2 fill:#e6f3ff
    style DEST fill:#fff0e0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The carrier (toluene) is recycled in a *closed loop*. Hydrogen is the only commodity that crosses the ocean. The energy penalty is concentrated in two reactors: P4 (hydrogenation, exothermic) and DEST3 (dehydrogenation, endothermic). Both reactors are mature catalytic fixed-bed units used in petrochemical refineries for decades.

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Carrier Lifecycle Across Voyages

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> TolueneLoaded: TOL leaves origin
    TolueneLoaded --> MCHLoaded: Hydrogenation at 180 C
    MCHLoaded --> InTransit: Sails ambient
    InTransit --> MCHUnloaded: Arrives destination
    MCHUnloaded --> H2Released: Dehydrogenation at 350 C
    H2Released --> TolueneEmpty: Carrier stripped
    TolueneEmpty --> InTransitReturn: Back-haul voyage
    InTransitReturn --> TolueneLoaded: Returns to origin
    TolueneLoaded --> [*]: Reload cycle complete
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU board exam:** Be able to draw (or describe) the **closed-loop nature of LOHC** — the carrier is not consumed, only H₂ crosses the ocean. This is the single most important conceptual distinction between LOHC and LH₂ or NH₃ transport.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)
