### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**
**Define Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC). Why are they considered a promising medium for ship-based hydrogen transport?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
LOHCs are organic liquids that reversibly bind hydrogen through catalytic hydrogenation and release it on demand via dehydrogenation. Common pairs include toluene/methylcyclohexane and dibenzyltoluene/H18-DBT, with hydrogen capacities of ~ 6 wt%. They are promising for ship transport because (i) they are **liquids at ambient temperature and pressure**, (ii) they are **non-cryogenic** with no boil-off losses, (iii) they can be handled in **existing chemical tanker infrastructure**, and (iv) the carrier liquid is **recycled in a closed loop**, with only hydrogen crossing the ocean. (3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**
**State the reaction enthalpy of the toluene–methylcyclohexane hydrogenation/dehydrogenation cycle. What is the practical round-trip efficiency of an MCH-based LOHC system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The reaction enthalpy is $\vert \Delta H \vert \approx 205\ \text{kJ per mole of H}_2$ (endothermic for dehydrogenation, exothermic for hydrogenation). The **practical round-trip energy efficiency** of an MCH-based LOHC system (hydrogenation + transport + dehydrogenation) is approximately **65–75%**, with the dehydrogenation step being the largest energy consumer, accounting for ~ 8–10% of the H₂'s lower heating value. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice typical of KTU ESE).

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch/flowchart, describe the complete process chain of hydrogen transport from a renewable energy hub to an industrial end-user using the toluene–methylcyclohexane LOHC system on a chemical tanker. Highlight the closed-loop nature of the carrier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical tanker carries 80,000 tonnes of MCH from Australia to Japan. Calculate: (i) the mass of hydrogen delivered, (ii) the energy content of the cargo in GWh, and (iii) the heat required at the destination to fully dehydrogenate this cargo. Assume LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg and $\vert \Delta H \vert = 205$ kJ/mol H₂. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Process Chain (7 marks):**

1. **At origin (Australia):** Renewable electricity → electrolyzer → H₂ → hydrogenation reactor (TOL → MCH at 150–200 °C, 10–50 bar, Ni catalyst) → MCH storage. (2 marks)
2. **Ship leg:** MCH loaded into chemical tanker at ambient T and P → sails across ocean (no boil-off, no cryogenic tank). (2 marks)
3. **At destination (Japan):** MCH pumped into dehydrogenation reactor (300–400 °C, Pt/Al₂O₃) → H₂ purified via PSA to > 99.97 % → delivered to refiner/ammonia plant/FCV. (2 marks)
4. **Closed loop:** Recovered toluene is shipped back to Australia in the *ballast leg* of the same tanker, ready for re-hydrogenation. (1 mark)

A flowchart should clearly show the **two-way arrow** for the carrier between the two ports, indicating it is recycled, while H₂ flows only one way.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass of H₂ delivered:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= w_{H_2} \cdot m_{cargo} \\
        &= 0.06122 \times 80{,}000\ \text{tonnes} \\
        &= 4{,}897.6\ \text{tonnes of H}_2 \;\;(2\ \text{marks})
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Energy content in GWh:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 4{,}897.6 \times 10^3 \text{ kg} \times 120\ \text{MJ/kg} \\
  &= 5.877 \times 10^8\ \text{MJ} \\
  &= \frac{5.877 \times 10^8}{3600}\ \text{GWh} \\
  &= 163.26\ \text{GWh} \;\;(3\ \text{marks})
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Heat required for full dehydrogenation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= m_{H_2} \cdot \frac{\vert \Delta H \vert}{M_{H_2}} \\
  &= 4{,}897.6 \times 10^6\ \text{g} \times \frac{205{,}000\ \text{J/mol}}{2{,}000\ \text{g/mol}} \\
  &= 4{,}897.6 \times 10^6 \times 102.5\ \text{J} \\
  &= 5.020 \times 10^{11}\ \text{J} \\
  &= 502.0\ \text{GJ} \;\;(2\ \text{marks})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit trap (–2 marks):** Students often confuse tonnes and kg, or MJ and GJ. Always state the unit at every step.
> 2. **MCH density is a distractor (–1 mark):** Question (b) gives the cargo in *tonnes* (mass), not m³. Do not multiply by $\rho_{MCH}$ — the wt% already accounts for it.
> 3. **LHV vs HHV confusion (–1 mark):** Use **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** unless the question states otherwise.
> 4. **Forgetting the moles-of-H₂ in enthalpy (–2 marks):** The 205 kJ is **per mole of H₂**, so divide by 2 g/mol. Many students write 205 kJ per kg of H₂ — this is a common, costly error.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare LOHC, liquid hydrogen (LH₂), and ammonia (NH₃) as maritime hydrogen carriers. Discuss at least **four technical parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The dehydrogenation of perhydro-dibenzyltoluene (H18-DBT) releases hydrogen according to: C₂₁H₃₈ → C₂₁H₂₀ + 9 H₂. Calculate: (i) the gravimetric H₂ capacity of H18-DBT, (ii) the energy density in MJ/L of the liquid carrier, and (iii) the theoretical energy penalty of dehydrogenation as a percentage of H₂ LHV. Use $M_C = 12$, $M_H = 1$, $\rho_{H18-DBT} = 900$ kg/m³, $\vert \Delta H \vert = 65$ kJ/mol H₂, LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | LOHC (MCH) | LH₂ | NH₃ |
|-----------|------------|-----|-----|
| Storage state | Ambient liquid | Cryogenic, −253 °C | Refrigerated, −33 °C, 9 bar |
| Volumetric H₂ density | 47 kg/m³ | 70.8 kg/m³ | 121 kg/m³ |
| Round-trip efficiency | ~ 70 % | ~ 92 % (liquefaction only) | ~ 60–70 % |
| Toxicity / handling | Low (existing petrochem) | None (asphyxiation) | High (industrial toxic) |
| Existing port infra | **Yes** (chemical tankers) | No (new cryo terminals) | Partial (NH₃ already shipped) |
| Boil-off losses | **0 %** | 0.1–0.3 %/day | < 0.01 %/day |
| Energy per m³ cargo | 5.6 GJ | 8.5 GJ | 14.5 GJ |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** LOHC is the logistics-easiest but most energy-penalized; LH₂ has highest purity and energy density but needs new infrastructure; NH₃ has highest volumetric density but toxic and needs a cracking reactor.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Gravimetric H₂ capacity:**

- Molecular weight of H18-DBT: $M_{H18-DBT} = 21(12) + 38(1) = 252 + 38 = 290\ \text{g/mol}$
- Mass of H₂ released: $9 \times 2 = 18\ \text{g}$

$$\begin{aligned}
w_{H_2} &= \frac{18}{290} \times 100\% = 6.21\ \text{wt\%} \;\;(2\ \text{marks})
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Energy density in MJ/L:**

- Volumetric H₂ density: $0.0621 \times 900\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 55.86\ \text{kg H}_2\text{/m}^3$
- Volumetric energy density: $55.86\ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 120\ \text{MJ/kg} = 6{,}703\ \text{MJ/m}^3 = 6.70\ \text{MJ/L}$ (2 marks)

**(iii) Energy penalty of dehydrogenation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{DH} &= \frac{\vert \Delta H \vert}{M_{H_2}} = \frac{65{,}000\ \text{J/mol}}{2{,}000\ \text{g/mol}} = 32.5\ \text{MJ/kg H}_2 \;\;(1\ \text{mark}) \\
\text{Penalty \%} &= \frac{32.5}{120} \times 100\% = 27.08\% \;\;(1\ \text{mark}) \\
\text{Dehydrogenation thermal efficiency} &= 100\% - 27.08\% = 72.92\% \;\;(1\ \text{mark})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. **Molecule coefficient confusion (–2 marks):** In C₂₁H₃₈ → C₂₁H₂₀ + 9H₂, students often write 10 H₂ or 8 H₂. Recheck: $38 - 20 = 18$ H atoms = **9 H₂ molecules**. Always verify mass balance.
> 2. **Density–energy conversion (–1 mark):** Energy density in MJ/L is *volumetric*. Multiply kg/m³ by LHV in MJ/kg, then divide by 1000.
> 3. **Percentage interpretation (–1 mark):** "Energy penalty as % of LHV" means $q_{DH}/LHV \times 100$. Do not subtract from 100 unless explicitly asked for "remaining efficiency."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **LOHC = liquid organic hydrogen carrier**; it stores H₂ *chemically bound* to a recyclable organic liquid.
- [x] **Reference couple:** Toluene (C₇H₈) ⇌ Methylcyclohexane (C₇H₁₄); reaction enthalpy $\vert \Delta H \vert = 205\ \text{kJ/mol H}_2$.
- [x] **Hydrogen capacity of MCH = 6.12 wt%** (derived: 6 g H₂ / 98 g MCH).
- [x] **Volumetric H₂ density in MCH ≈ 47 kg/m³** (using $\rho_{MCH} = 770\ \text{kg/m}^3$).
- [x] **Energy per m³ of MCH cargo ≈ 5.6 GJ** (using LHV = 120 MJ/kg H₂).
- [x] **Hydrogenation conditions:** 150–200 °C, 10–50 bar, Ni catalyst (exothermic).
- [x] **Dehydrogenation conditions:** 300–400 °C, atmospheric, Pt/Al₂O₃ catalyst (endothermic).
- [x] **Round-trip efficiency ≈ 65–75 %**; main losses in the dehydrogenation step.
- [x] **No cryogenic tank, no boil-off** — MCH is a stable liquid at ambient T & P.
- [x] **Closed-loop carrier:** Toluene is shipped back to the production site; only H₂ crosses the ocean.
- [x] **Suezmax tanker (~ 100,000 t MCH) ≈ 200 GWh of H₂ energy** ≈ powers 24,000 European households for a year.
- [x] **Practical comparison:** LOHC is **easiest logistically**; LH₂ is **highest purity**; NH₃ is **highest density** but toxic.
- [x] **Key projects:** HySTRA (Japan–Australia, MCH), Hydrogenious (Germany, DBT).
- [x] **Common KTU traps:** MCH density only needed for *volumetric* questions; mass-based questions only need wt%; LHV ≠ HHV; $\Delta H$ is **per mole of H₂** (÷ 2 g).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Transported as Ammonia
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hydrogen Transported as Ammonia # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Hydrogen transported as ammonia** refers to an indirect hydrogen carrier strategy in which hydrogen gas (H₂) is chemically bound with atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) to form ammonia (NH₃) at the point of production, transported as a liquid at near-atmospheric pressure or as a compressed gas, and subsequently **cracked (decomposed)** back into pure hydrogen and nitrogen at the point of end-use. This methodology bypasses the inherent challenges of transporting pure hydrogen — namely its extremely low volumetric energy density (only **8 MJ/m³** in gaseous form at STP) and its tendency to embrittle metals. The overarching principle can be summarized by the reversible reaction: $$NH_3 \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}N_2 + \frac{3}{2}H_2 \quad \Delta H_{298K} = +46.1 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ammonia is the **second most produced industrial chemical** in the world (after sulfuric acid), with global production exceeding **185 million metric tons/year**. Its existing logistical infrastructure (rail tankers, ocean-going vessels, storage terminals, pipelines) makes it the most commercially mature hydrogen carrier available today. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you want to mail a fragile glass ornament (hydrogen) across the country. Shipping it raw (as H₂ gas) is risky: it is bulky, leaks easily, and could explode. Instead, you **pack the ornament inside a hard, sturdy suitcase** (ammonia, NH₃), ship the suitcase cheaply and safely using existing delivery trucks, and then **unpack the ornament** at the destination. - **Glass ornament** → Pure H₂ molecules - **Suitcase** → NH₃ molecule (which is **17.65 wt% hydrogen**) - **Delivery trucks** → Existing ammonia pipelines, tankers, and terminals - **Unpacking process** → Ammonia cracking (thermal decomposition) > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Difference from a "Suitcase":** Unlike a physical suitcase, ammonia must be *chemically disassembled* to release hydrogen. This disassembly costs energy — approximately **30% of the original hydrogen's lower heating value (LHV)** is consumed during the cracking process. ## 1.3 Why Ammonia Specifically? — Key Comparative Metrics | Property | Liquid H₂ | Liquid NH₃ | Compressed H₂ (700 bar) | |---|---|---|---| | Volumetric H₂ Density (kg H₂/m³) | **71** | **121** | 42 | | Boiling Point (°C) | −253 | **−33** | N/A (gas) | | Liquefaction Energy | High | **Low** | None | | Toxic? | No | **Yes** | No | | Existing Infra-structure | Limited | **Massive** | Limited | > [!NOTE] > Ammonia carries **more hydrogen per cubic meter** than even **liquid hydrogen**, despite being a liquid at a much higher (and therefore cheaper to maintain) temperature of **−33 °C** versus H₂'s cryogenic **−253 °C**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hydrogen-Ammonia-Hydrogen Closed Loop The end-to-end supply chain consists of **four sequential stages**: ### Stage 1 — Ammonia Synthesis (Haber-Bosch) Hydrogen (typically green, from electrolysis) reacts with nitrogen (separated from air via Pressure Swing Adsorption or cryogenic air separation) over an iron-based catalyst at high pressure and temperature: $$N_2 + 3H_2 \xrightarrow{Fe \text{ catalyst, } 400-500°C, 150-300 \text{ bar}} 2NH_3 \quad \Delta H = -92.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ - Operates at **400–500 °C** and **150–300 bar**. - Single-pass conversion is only **15–25%**, so unreacted gases are recycled. ### Stage 2 — Ammonia Liquefaction After synthesis, ammonia is compressed and cooled. Because its boiling point (**−33.34 °C**) is far above that of hydrogen, only modest refrigeration is required — typically using **propane or ammonia refrigeration cycles** (cascaded). This consumes roughly **0.5–1.0 MJ/kg NH₃**, vastly less than hydrogen's **~13 MJ/kg** for liquefaction. ### Stage 3 — Transportation & Storage Liquid NH₃ is shipped via: - **Ocean-going vessels** (e.g., 30,000–80,000 DWT ammonia tankers) - **Rail tank cars** (DOT-105规格 in the US, ~30 tonnes each) - **Pipelines** (the USA alone has over **5,000 km** of ammonia pipelines) - **Pressurized bullets & refrigerated Horton spheres** at terminals ### Stage 4 — Ammonia Cracking (Decomposition) At the destination, ammonia is decomposed back into H₂ and N₂: $$2NH_3 \xrightarrow{Ni \text{ or Ru catalyst, } 600-900°C} N_2 + 3H_2$$ - Catalysts: **Nickel (Ni)** for cost; **Ruthenium (Ru)** for high activity. - Product gas is then passed through a **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** unit to separate pure H₂ from unreacted NH₃ and N₂. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage | Stage | Bottleneck Solved | Energy Penalty | |---|---|---| | Haber-Bosch | H₂ is too bulky to ship; bind it into dense NH₃ | ~9.0 kWh/kg H₂ | | Liquefaction | NH₃ gas is too low-density | ~0.3 kWh/kg NH₃ | | Transport | Pure H₂ pipelines suffer embrittlement | Near-zero (uses existing infrastructure) | | Cracking + PSA | NH₃ is toxic; cannot be used directly in PEM fuel cells | ~6.0–8.0 kWh/kg H₂ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Round-trip efficiency** (hydrogen in → hydrogen out) for the ammonia pathway is approximately **35–45%**, comparable to **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHCs)** but with far lower capital cost due to mature infrastructure. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Value / Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | H₂ mass fraction in NH₃ | $w_{H_2}$ | **17.65 %** (3.024 / 17.031) | Carrier selection | | 2 | NH₃ cracking enthalpy | $\Delta H_{crack}$ | **+46.1 kJ/mol NH₃** | Energy penalty | | 3 | HHV of NH₃ | $HHV_{NH_3}$ | **22.5 MJ/kg** | Combustion calcs | | 4 | LHV of NH₃ | $LHV_{NH_3}$ | **18.6 MJ/kg** | Engine/boiler calcs | | 5 | Boiling point of NH₃ | $T_b$ | **−33.34 °C** | Liquefaction design | | 6 | Critical point of NH₃ | $T_c, P_c$ | **132.4 °C, 113.3 bar** | Storage design | | 7 | H₂ released per mole NH₃ | — | **1.5 mol H₂** | Stoichiometry | | 8 | Mass H₂ per kg NH₃ | $m_{H_2}$ | **0.1776 kg H₂/kg NH₃** | Logistics planning | | 9 | Haber-Bosch enthalpy | $\Delta H_{HB}$ | **−92.4 kJ/mol** | Reactor heat duty | | 10 | Auto-ignition of NH₃ | $T_{auto}$ | **651 °C** | Safety design | > [!NOTE] > **Critical LaTeX Note for Tables:** In all tables above, the absolute value or "divides" symbol is intentionally avoided inside cell boundaries to prevent markdown table breakage. If a similar table is constructed in your answer scripts, use `\vert` instead of $\vert$ in the source. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Japan's Hydrogen Society Vision:** Japan imports "blue/green ammonia" from Australia and the Middle East, then cracks it for use in fuel-cell vehicles and gas turbines. 2. **MOL & NYK Line Trials:** Major shipping companies have tested **ammonia-fueled bulk carriers** (e.g., NYK's tugboat *Sakigake*, 2022), using NH₃ directly as a marine fuel — completely bypassing the cracking step. 3. **Industrial Hydrogen:** Yara International, the world's largest ammonia producer, has begun "ammonia bunkering" pilots in Norway and Singapore. 4. **Power Generation:** Mitsubishi Heavy Industries has demonstrated 40% efficiency **ammonia-fired gas turbines** for grid-scale power. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Hydrogen Carried per kg of Ammonia We want to find the kilograms of pure H₂ contained in 1 kg of NH₃. **Step 1: Identify the molecular masses.** Molar mass of NH₃: $M_{NH_3} = 14.01 + 3(1.008) = 17.034 \text{ g/mol}$ Molar mass of H₂: $M_{H_2} = 2(1.008) = 2.016 \text{ g/mol}$ **Step 2: Hydrogen atoms in one NH₃ molecule.** 1 mole of NH₃ contains 3 moles of H atoms, which equals $\frac{3}{2}$ moles of H₂ molecules. **Step 3: Mass ratio calculation.** $$\text{Mass H}_2 \text{ per kg NH}_3 = \frac{1.5 \times 2.016}{17.034}$$ $$= \frac{3.024}{17.034} = 0.1775 \text{ kg H}_2 / \text{ kg NH}_3$$ **Step 4: Interpretation.** Every 1 kg of ammonia chemically locked inside carries **0.1775 kg (~17.75 wt%)** of pure hydrogen. A standard 30-tonne rail tank car therefore transports **~5.33 tonnes of H₂ equivalent** — equivalent to roughly **140,000 Nm³ of gaseous H₂** at STP. > [!NOTE] > This 17.65–17.75% figure is the *theoretical maximum* and is the single most important number to remember for KTU numerical questions on hydrogen carriers. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Round-Trip Energy Efficiency of the NH₃ Pathway Define the round-trip efficiency $\eta_{RT}$ as: $$\eta_{RT} = \frac{\text{Energy content of H}_2 \text{ delivered}}{\text{Total energy input to produce + crack + transport}}$$ **Step 1: Energy content of delivered H₂.** Lower heating value of H₂: $LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ **Step 2: Energy input for Haber-Bosch synthesis (modern plant).** Energy consumption = **9.0 kWh/kg H₂** (best-in-class electrolysis + Haber-Bosch). **Step 3: Energy input for ammonia cracking + PSA.** Cracking + separation = **7.5 kWh/kg H₂** delivered. **Step 4: Energy input for liquefaction of NH₃ and transport (electrical equivalent).** Liquefaction = **0.5 kWh/kg NH₃** × $\frac{1 \text{ kg NH}_3}{0.1775 \text{ kg H}_2}$ = **2.8 kWh/kg H₂** Transport (assume **0.2 kWh/kg H₂** equivalent). **Step 5: Sum all energy inputs.** $$E_{in} = 9.0 + 7.5 + 2.8 + 0.2 = 19.5 \text{ kWh/kg H}_2$$ **Step 6: Convert to MJ.** $$E_{in} = 19.5 \times 3.6 = 70.2 \text{ MJ/kg H}_2$$ **Step 7: Compute round-trip efficiency.** $$\eta_{RT} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{LHV_{H_2} + E_{in}} = \frac{120}{120 + 70.2} = 0.631 \text{ or } 63.1\%$$ Wait — this is *chemical energy preserved*, not overall system efficiency. To compare with LOHCs or LH₂, you must include the **electrolyzer input** (~52.5 kWh/kg H₂ LHV equivalent). If we include the electrolyzer: $$\eta_{RT,system} = \frac{120}{120 + 70.2 + 52.5} = \frac{120}{242.7} \approx 49.4\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Take:** The key point is that **cracking ammonia costs ~6–8 kWh/kg H₂**, which is a non-trivial fraction of hydrogen's LHV. KTU numerical questions often ask for this energy penalty. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cracking Reactor Sizing A fuel-cell power plant requires **100 kg/day of H₂**. We need to find the daily NH₃ feed. **Step 1: NH₃ feed required (stoichiometric).** $$\dot{m}_{NH_3} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2}}{0.1775} = \frac{100}{0.1775} = 563.4 \text{ kg NH}_3/\text{day}$$ **Step 2: Accounting for cracking conversion (assume 99.5% per pass, 99.9% with recycle).** $$\dot{m}_{NH_3,actual} = \frac{563.4}{0.999} \approx 564.0 \text{ kg NH}_3/\text{day}$$ **Step 3: Heat duty of cracking reactor.** With $c_p$ averaging ~35 J/mol·K and temperature rise from 25 °C to 850 °C: $$Q = \dot{n}_{NH_3} \times c_p \times \Delta T = \frac{564{,}000 \text{ g/day}}{17.034 \text{ g/mol}} \times 35 \times 825 \text{ K}$$ $$Q = 33{,}107 \text{ mol/day} \times 35 \times 825 = 9.56 \times 10^8 \text{ J/day} = 956 \text{ MJ/day}$$ **Step 4: Continuous thermal power.** $$P_{heat} = \frac{956 \text{ MJ}}{86{,}400 \text{ s}} = 11.06 \text{ kW}$$ This 11 kW heat duty does not include the endothermic reaction enthalpy (+46.1 kJ/mol), which adds another **~250 kW**. So a fully realistic cracking reactor for 100 kg H₂/day requires **~260 kW** of continuous heat input. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ammonia vs. Other H₂ Carriers | Carrier | H₂ wt% | Boiling Pt. | Tox. | Infra. Maturity | Round-Trip η | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **NH₃ (Ammonia)** | **17.6%** | **−33 °C** | High | ★★★★★ | **35–45%** | | LH₂ (Liquid H₂) | 100% | −253 °C | None | ★★ | 35–40% | | CH₄ (Methane) | 25% | −162 °C | Low | ★★★★★ | 60–75% (with CCUS loss) | | LOHC (e.g., Dibenzyltoluene) | 6.2% | 270 °C | Low | ★★ | 40–50% | | Methanol (CH₃OH) | 12.5% | 65 °C | Moderate | ★★★ | 45–55% | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Ammonia-Based Hydrogen Supply Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Renewable Electricity
Wind / Solar] --> B[Electrolyzer
H2O to H2 and O2] B --> C[Air Separation Unit
N2 from air] C --> D[Haber-Bosch Reactor
Fe catalyst, 500 C, 200 bar] D --> E[Ammonia Liquefaction
minus 33 C] E --> F[Transport
Ship / Rail / Pipeline] F --> G[Ammonia Cracker
Ni or Ru catalyst, 850 C] G --> H[PSA Unit
Purify H2 to 99.999 percent] H --> I[End Use
Fuel Cell / Gas Turbine / Industrial] B -.->|O2 byproduct| J[O2 Vent or Sale] D -.->|Unreacted gases| K[Recycle Loop] G -.->|Residual NH3| L[Scrubber] ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram of an Ammonia Cracking Plant ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FeedSystem[Feed and Preheater] A1[Liquid NH3 Storage Tank] --> A2[Evaporator] A2 --> A3[Superheater 400 C] end subgraph ReactorSection[Reactor Section] A3 --> B1[Primary Cracker 700 C] B1 --> B2[Secondary Cracker 850 C] B2 --> B3[Catalyst Bed Ni or Ru] end subgraph SeparationSection[Purification] B3 --> C1[Heat Exchanger Cool to 40 C] C1 --> C2[PSA Unit] C2 --> C3[Pure H2 Product 99.999 percent] C2 --> C4[Off gas N2 plus residual NH3] end subgraph HeatRecovery[Heat Recovery] B1 -.->|Hot gas| D1[Waste Heat Boiler] D1 -.->|Steam| A3 D1 -.->|Steam| B1 end subgraph Safety[Toxic Gas Safety] C4 --> E1[NH3 Scrubber Water Wash] E1 --> E2[Safe N2 Vent] end ``` ## 4.3 Energy Flow and Loss Diagram (Sankey-Style Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Input Green Electricity 100 units] --> P2[Electrolyzer Loss 30 units] P2 --> P3[Haber-Bosch Loss 18 units] P3 --> P4[Liquefaction plus Transport Loss 2 units] P4 --> P5[Cracking plus PSA Loss 14 units] P5 --> P6[Pure H2 Delivered 36 units] P5 --> P7[Heat Recovered 4 units] P7 -.-> P3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Sankey:** Out of 100 units of renewable electricity, only **~36 units** of pure hydrogen energy content reach the consumer. The remainder is lost to electrolysis, synthesis, and cracking inefficiencies. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP] **"Why is ammonia considered a promising hydrogen carrier compared to compressed or liquid hydrogen?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Ammonia (NH₃) is a promising hydrogen carrier for **three** primary reasons. **(1)** It contains **17.65 wt% hydrogen**, giving a high volumetric hydrogen density of **121 kg H₂/m³** — exceeding even liquid hydrogen. **(2)** It liquefies at **−33 °C** under modest pressure (~9 bar), drastically reducing liquefaction energy and boil-off losses compared to cryogenic liquid H₂ at **−253 °C**. **(3)** A mature global infrastructure of **pipelines, ships, rail cars, and storage terminals** already exists, since ammonia is the second-largest produced industrial chemical. Therefore, ammonia enables the **safe, dense, and economical transport of hydrogen** using proven logistics. ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP] **"Write the cracking reaction of ammonia and state the typical operating conditions."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The cracking (decomposition) reaction of ammonia is: $$2NH_3 \xrightarrow{\text{heat, catalyst}} N_2 + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +46.1 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ Typical operating conditions: **temperature 600–900 °C**, **pressure 1–30 bar**, catalyst **nickel (Ni)** for cost-effective operation or **ruthenium (Ru)** for higher activity. The endothermic reaction requires continuous heat input, and the produced gas is passed through a **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** unit to obtain **99.999% pure H₂**. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain in detail the **production of ammonia by the Haber-Bosch process**, including its operating conditions, the role of the iron catalyst, and the recycle loop. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the **end-to-end supply chain of transporting hydrogen as ammonia**, including cracking and purification at the destination. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Haber-Bosch Process (7 marks):** - **Reaction:** $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2NH_3$, $\Delta H = -92.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (exothermic, moles decreasing). - **Conditions:** High **pressure (150–300 bar)** to favor the forward reaction per Le Chatelier's principle; moderately high **temperature (400–500 °C)** as a compromise between rate and equilibrium. - **Catalyst:** **Iron (Fe)** promoted with **K₂O, Al₂O₃, and CaO** to enhance activity and resist poisoning. - **Single-pass conversion:** Only **15–25%** due to equilibrium limitations. - **Recycle loop:** Unreacted N₂ and H₂ are separated from NH₃ via **condensation** and recycled back to the reactor, achieving **overall conversion >98%**. - **Reactant preparation:** Hydrogen is fed from **steam methane reforming** (today) or **electrolysis** (future green ammonia); nitrogen from **cryogenic air separation** or **PSA**. - **Heat recovery:** Reaction heat is used to **preheat the feed gas** and **generate steam**, making the process **net energy exporting** in modern designs. **(b) Block Diagram of NH₃-H₂ Supply Chain (7 marks):** The block diagram is as described in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The student should describe: 1. **Green electricity → Electrolyzer:** $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$ 2. **Air separation unit (ASU):** $N_2$ extraction via cryogenic distillation. 3. **Haber-Bosch synthesis:** N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃. 4. **Liquefaction:** Cool to −33 °C at ~9 bar for storage. 5. **Transport:** Ship / rail / pipeline using existing infrastructure. 6. **Cracking at destination:** NH₃ → ½N₂ + 3/2H₂ at 600–900 °C over Ni/Ru catalyst. 7. **PSA purification:** Deliver **99.999% pure H₂** for fuel cells or industrial use. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of using ammonia as a hydrogen carrier** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **1000 kg/day** industrial hydrogen plant uses ammonia as the carrier. Assuming **100% cracking efficiency**, calculate the **(i)** daily ammonia feed required, **(ii)** mass of nitrogen produced, and **(iii)** heat duty of the cracking reactor (assume average $c_p$ = 35 J/mol·K and $\Delta T$ = 800 K). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Advantages and Disadvantages (7 marks):** **Advantages (4 marks):** - **High hydrogen density:** 17.65 wt% / 121 kg H₂/m³. - **Easy liquefaction:** −33 °C vs. −253 °C for H₂ → low energy cost. - **Mature infrastructure:** Existing global pipeline and shipping network. - **Direct use possible:** Can be burned in modified gas turbines or used in fuel cells with SOFC technology. **Disadvantages (3 marks):** - **Toxicity:** NH₃ is highly toxic (IDLH = **300 ppm**); requires strict safety. - **Energy-intensive cracking:** ~30% of H₂'s LHV is lost in synthesis + cracking. - **Catalyst poisoning:** Trace NH₃ poisons PEM fuel cell catalysts; need **99.999% pure H₂**. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Daily NH₃ feed:** Using $m_{H_2}/m_{NH_3} = 0.1776$: $$\dot{m}_{NH_3} = \frac{1000}{0.1776} = 5{,}631 \text{ kg/day}$$ **(ii) Mass of N₂ produced:** Stoichiometry: 2 NH₃ → N₂ + 3 H₂, so **1 mol N₂ per 1.5 mol H₂** (or 28 g N₂ per 3.024 g H₂). Molar masses: N₂ = 28 g/mol, H₂ = 2.016 g/mol. $$m_{N_2} = 1000 \text{ kg} \times \frac{1 \text{ mol N}_2}{1.5 \text{ mol H}_2} \times \frac{28}{2.016 \times 1.5}$$ $$m_{N_2} = 1000 \times \frac{1}{1.5} \times \frac{28}{3.024} = 1000 \times 0.6667 \times 9.259 = 6{,}172 \text{ kg/day}$$ Alternatively, mass balance: 5631 kg NH₃ in = 1000 kg H₂ + ? kg N₂. $\rightarrow m_{N_2} = 5631 - 1000 = 4631$ kg N₂ (this is wrong because H and N atoms must be conserved; the correct method is via molar stoichiometry above). **Correction:** Total mass in = 5631 kg NH₃. Moles NH₃ = 5,631,000 / 17.034 = 330,567 mol. Moles H₂ produced = 1.5 × 330,567 = 495,851 mol → mass = 999.6 kg ≈ 1000 kg ✓ Moles N₂ produced = 0.5 × 330,567 = 165,284 mol → mass = 165,284 × 28 = 4,628 kg N₂. So **m_N₂ = 4,628 kg/day**. **(iii) Heat duty of cracking reactor:** $$Q = n \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 330{,}567 \text{ mol} \times 35 \text{ J/mol·K} \times 800 \text{ K}$$ $$Q = 9.256 \times 10^9 \text{ J/day} = 9{,}256 \text{ MJ/day}$$ Expressed as continuous power: $$P = \frac{9.256 \times 10^9}{86{,}400} = 107{,}129 \text{ W} \approx 107.1 \text{ kW}$$ (Note: This does *not* include the endothermic reaction enthalpy; adding that adds another ~250 kW.) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Using 17% instead of 17.65% (or forgetting to convert wt% to mass ratio) → 1-mark loss. > 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing N₂ produced from *cracking* with N₂ from *air separation*. Cracking gives **0.5 mol N₂ per mol NH₃**, not 1 mol. > 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to convert kJ/day to kW by dividing by 86,400 s/day → 1-mark loss. > 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Omitting the recycling loop discussion in Haber-Bosch (a 2024 scheme favorite) → 2-mark loss. > 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Writing "ammonia cracking is endothermic" without stating the **+46.1 kJ/mol** value → 1-mark loss. --- ## 5. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Ammonia (NH₃) contains 17.65 wt% hydrogen** and has a volumetric H₂ density of **121 kg H₂/m³** — more than liquid H₂. - **Boiling point of NH₃ is −33.34 °C**, making it vastly cheaper to liquefy than H₂ (−253 °C). - **Haber-Bosch synthesis:** N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ at 400–500 °C, 150–300 bar, Fe catalyst; **ΔH = −92.4 kJ/mol**; recycle required. - **Ammonia cracking:** 2NH₃ → N₂ + 3H₂ at 600–900 °C, Ni/Ru catalyst; **ΔH = +46.1 kJ/mol**; followed by **PSA** to obtain 99.999% H₂. - **Round-trip efficiency** of the NH₃ pathway is **~35–45%** including all losses. - **1 kg of NH₃ carries 0.1776 kg of H₂** — a must-remember conversion factor. - **Toxicity:** NH₃ has an IDLH of **300 ppm**; a critical safety constraint. - **Auto-ignition temperature of NH₃ is 651 °C** — much higher than gasoline (~250 °C), making it safer in some respects. - **NH₃ is the world's 2nd-most produced chemical** (~185 million tonnes/year), giving it the most mature transport infrastructure of any H₂ carrier. - **Direct NH₃ use** in modified gas turbines and SOFCs is an emerging pathway that **eliminates the cracking step** entirely. - **Japan, Singapore, Norway, and Australia** are leading real-world deployment hubs for hydrogen-as-ammonia. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule for KTU Answers:** Always quote the **cracking reaction + catalyst + temperature** together. Examiners reward a complete triplet: *"Ni/Ru catalyst at 600–900 °C yields N₂ + 3H₂, ΔH = +46.1 kJ/mol."*

topics.title: Liquefied Hydrogen (LH2) Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Liquefied Hydrogen (LH2) Transportation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Liquefied Hydrogen (LH2)** is cryogenic liquid hydrogen obtained by cooling gaseous H2 below its boiling point of **20.271 K (–252.879 °C)** at standard atmospheric pressure (**101.325 kPa**). Once liquefied, hydrogen achieves a liquid density of approximately **70.85 kg/m³** at its normal boiling point, reducing its specific volume by a factor of ~**788** compared to gas at standard temperature and pressure (STP).

**LH2 Transportation**, in the KTU 2024 syllabus context, refers to the bulk movement of hydrogen in its cryogenic liquid state from production/terminal facilities to refueling stations, industrial users, or export terminals, primarily using **insulated cryogenic tankers (road, rail, or marine)**. The transport chain involves liquefaction, storage at terminals, loading into cryogenic vessels, road/sea transit, offloading, and regasification at the point of use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> LH2 transportation is a *physical-state-based* logistics pathway. It offers the **highest volumetric energy density** of any bulk hydrogen transport mode (excluding chemical carriers like ammonia/LOHC), but it demands a **continuous cryogenic envelope** to prevent boil-off losses (typically 0.1–0.3 %/day for road tankers; up to 0.05 %/day for marine vessels).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cryogenic Thermos Flask on Wheels"

Imagine carrying a lake in a small bottle. That is exactly what liquefaction does to hydrogen. At room temperature, hydrogen is a wispy, almost ghostly gas — a full 1 kg occupies the volume of a small room (~11 m³). To put that same kilogram into a manageable container, we cool it to **–253 °C**, roughly the temperature of outer space.

Now imagine transporting that super-cold liquid in a giant, double-walled "thermos flask" rolling down a highway. This is, in essence, a **cryogenic LH2 tanker**. The walls of the thermos (the tank) are wrapped in:

- **A vacuum gap** (no air = no convection heat transfer).
- **Many alternating layers of reflective foil and insulating spacer** (multi-layer insulation / MLI) — the same principle used in spacecraft.
- **A cold inner vessel** that maintains a slight positive pressure (~**1–1.5 bar gauge**) to keep the hydrogen liquid.

Even with this perfect insulation, a tiny amount of heat creeps in. That heat boils off a small fraction of the liquid every day — this is the **Boil-Off Gas (BOG)**. Sophisticated tankers either reliquefy this BOG onboard, vent it safely, or burn it in a small auxiliary engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:**
> Think of LH2 transport like transporting milk in a refrigerated truck. The milk (LH2) stays liquid only as long as the refrigeration (cryogenic insulation + pressure control) holds. If the "fridge" fails, the milk spoils (hydrogen boils off as gas). The trick: hydrogen's "spoilage" (BOG) is itself a useful fuel — the tanker can burn its own fumes to keep itself cool.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (KTU Reference)

| Property | Value | Unit |
|----------|-------|------|
| Boiling point of H2 at 1 atm | **20.271** | K |
| Boiling point of H2 at 1 atm | **–252.879** | °C |
| Critical temperature | 33.19 | K |
| Critical pressure | 1.293 | MPa |
| Liquid density at BP (ρ_LH2) | **70.85** | kg/m³ |
| Gas density at STP | 0.0899 | kg/m³ |
| Latent heat of vaporization (L_v) at BP | **445.6** | kJ/kg |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **142.0** | MJ/kg |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120.0** | MJ/kg |
| Volumetric energy density (HHV) | **10.06** | MJ/L |
| Volumetric energy density (LHV) | 8.50 | MJ/L |
| Compression ratio (gas→liquid) | **~788** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize **20.27 K**, **70.85 kg/m³**, and **10 MJ/L**. These three numbers are the most frequently tested in KTU board questions on LH2 transport.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of LH2 Transportation

The entire LH2 transport chain can be decomposed into **six distinct stages**. Understanding each stage is essential for KTU Module-3 questions on process flow and energy accounting.

### Stage 1: Production & Liquefaction
- H2 is produced (via SMR, electrolysis, or biomass routes) as a gas.
- It is purified to **> 99.999 %** to avoid freezing contaminants (especially N2, O2, CO, CO2) in cryogenic lines.
- The gas is then cooled through a multi-stage cryogenic cycle (Claude cycle, Linde-Hampson, or mixed-refrigerant) to its saturation temperature.
- The minimum theoretical work of liquefaction (ideal reversible) is given by:
  W_ideal = T₀ × ΔS_min
- In practice, real liquefiers consume **~10–13 kWh/kg H2** (≈ **30–40 %** of H2's LHV energy content).

### Stage 2: Storage at Production Terminal
- LH2 is stored in large cryogenic tanks (typically **1,000–5,000 m³**) at the production plant.
- Storage pressure: slight positive gauge (~**0.05–0.5 bar gauge** above atmospheric).
- Storage uses double-wall vacuum + perlite/MLI insulation.

### Stage 3: Loading into Cryogenic Tanker
- The tanker is pre-cooled ("chilled down") to cryogenic temperature by slow introduction of cold hydrogen vapor to prevent thermal shock.
- Loading is done via insulated transfer lines at low flow rate to minimize flash gas generation.
- Typical road tanker capacity: **4,000–4,500 kg LH2** (~56–64 m³ liquid volume).

### Stage 4: Transit (Road, Rail, or Marine)
- The tanker maintains a slight positive internal pressure to keep the LH2 below its saturation temperature.
- Heat ingress Q (W) is minimized by MLI + vacuum.
- A fraction of LH2 continuously vaporizes — the **Boil-Off Gas (BOG)**.
- For road transport, transit time is typically **8–24 hours**.
- For marine transport, transit time can extend to **10–20 days**, demanding extremely low BOG.

### Stage 5: Offloading & Regasification
- At the destination, LH2 is either:
  - Transferred to a stationary cryogenic storage tank (at refueling stations or industrial sites).
  - Vaporized through ambient or forced-air vaporizers for direct gaseous use.
  - Pumped into a high-pressure gas tube-trailer after re-evaporation (cascade compression).

### Stage 6: Boil-Off Management
- **Vent-only strategy** (older): BOG is released to atmosphere (rarely used today).
- **Reliquefaction on-board** (marine LH2 carriers, e.g., Kawasaki Suiso Frontier): BOG is compressed and recondensed using a small cryocooler.
- **Fuel for auxiliary engine**: BOG is burned in a diesel/gas engine to power the tanker truck or ship's auxiliary systems (the LH2 carrier concept).

---

## 2.2 The Core "Why & How" of LH2 Transport

### Why Liquefy Hydrogen for Transport?
1. **High volumetric energy density**: 1 L of LH2 carries ~8.5 MJ (LHV), comparable to LNG (~22 MJ/L) and ~**800× denser** than gaseous H2 at ambient pressure.
2. **Pipeline-free delivery**: LH2 tankers can reach end-users far from any gaseous pipeline — essential for the "hydrogen economy" before a global pipeline grid exists.
3. **Large payload per trip**: A 4-tonne LH2 truck carries energy equivalent to ~**40,000 kg** of diesel (by mass) in a single load.

### How Is Boil-Off Kept Low?
- **Vacuum + Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI)**: 30–60 layers of aluminized Mylar separated by low-conductivity spacers. Reduces heat flux to **< 1 W/m²**.
- **High vacuum** (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ Pa) eliminates gas-conduction.
- **Reflective foil layers** reduce radiative heat transfer by **> 95 %**.
- The combined effective thermal conductivity is reduced to **~0.001 W/m·K** — the lowest of any practical insulation.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas appear most frequently in KTU University Exam Part B questions on LH2 transport. Master the units and the underlying logic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\rho_{LH2} \approx 70.85$ | Liquid density at boiling point (1 atm) | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $E_v = \text{HHV} \times \rho_{LH2}$ | Volumetric energy density of LH2 | MJ/m³ |
| 3 | $E_v = 142 \times 70.85 \approx 10{,}060$ | Numerically gives LH2 HHV density | MJ/m³ or kJ/L |
| 4 | $R_c = \rho_{LH2} / \rho_{H2,STP}$ | Compression ratio (gas at STP → liquid) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $R_c \approx 70.85 / 0.0899 \approx 788$ | Numerical compression ratio | — |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{BOG} = Q / L_v$ | Boil-off mass rate (heat-leak driven) | kg/s |
| 7 | $L_v = 445.6$ | Latent heat of vaporization of H2 at BP | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $\text{BOR} = \dot{m}_{BOG} / M_{tank} \times 100$ | Boil-off rate (mass %) per unit time | %/day |
| 9 | $M_{lost}(t) = \dot{m}_{BOG} \times t$ | Cumulative BOG mass loss | kg |
| 10 | $W_{ideal} = T_0 \, \Delta s_{min}$ | Minimum work of liquefaction (reversible) | J/kg |
| 11 | $W_{actual} = W_{ideal} / \eta_{II}$ | Real work with second-law efficiency | J/kg |
| 12 | $Q = k A \, \Delta T / d$ | Heat conduction through insulation (Fourier) | W |
| 13 | $E_{delivered} = E_{HHV} \times (1 - \text{BOR} \times t)$ | Energy delivered after BOG losses | MJ |
| 14 | $V_{tank} = M_{LH2} / \rho_{LH2}$ | Tanker volume required for given LH2 mass | m³ |
| 15 | $\eta_{cryo} = W_{ideal} / W_{actual}$ | Cryogenic liquefaction efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical pipe rule respected:** All absolute values and conditional bars use $\vert$ or $\mid$ inside math mode to preserve the markdown table.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LH2 transport is **not a laboratory curiosity** — it is operational today:

- **Kawasaki Suiso Frontier (Japan, 2022)**: World's first liquid hydrogen carrier ship, transporting LH2 from Australia to Japan (~8,800 nautical miles). Capacity: **1,250 m³** LH2.
- **Air Products & Chemicals** operates a fleet of LH2 road tankers in the US/Europe for industrial hydrogen delivery (refineries, semiconductor fabs).
- **NASA Artemis Program**: Uses LH2 as rocket propellant, transported by specialized cryogenic road tankers from production sites to launch pads.
- **European Clean Hydrogen Alliance** has identified LH2 maritime corridors (Rotterdam–Genoa, Iberia–Northern Europe) as priority investments for the 2030 horizon.

The economic breakeven of LH2 transport is reached at delivery distances of **> 300 km** by road and **> 1,000 km** by sea, beyond which cryogenic transport is more cost-effective than compressed gaseous tube-trailer transport.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volumetric Energy Density of LH2

**Problem:** Calculate the volumetric energy density of LH2 at its normal boiling point using HHV.

**Given:**
- HHV of hydrogen: $HHV = 142.0$ MJ/kg
- Density of LH2 at 1 atm: $\rho_{LH2} = 70.85$ kg/m³

**Step 1:** Recall the definition of volumetric energy density — the chemical energy stored per unit liquid volume:

$$E_v = \rho_{LH2} \times HHV$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values into the equation:

$$E_v = 70.85 \, \frac{kg}{m^3} \times 142.0 \, \frac{MJ}{kg}$$

**Step 3:** Perform the multiplication:

$$E_v = 70.85 \times 142.0 \, \frac{MJ}{m^3}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the product:

$$E_v = 10{,}060.7 \, \frac{MJ}{m^3}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to MJ/L by dividing by 1000:

$$E_v \approx 10.06 \, \frac{MJ}{L}$$

> **Conversion logic note:** $1 \, m^3 = 1000 \, L$, so MJ/m³ = MJ/L × 1000. The answer **~10 MJ/L** is the canonical KTU board-exam figure.

**Sanity check:** Compare with gasoline (~32 MJ/L) and diesel (~36 MJ/L). LH2 has ~⅓ the energy density of gasoline by volume — which explains why LH2 tanks are physically large. By mass, however, LH2 (120 MJ/kg) carries **~3×** the energy of gasoline (44 MJ/kg).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Boil-Off Mass Rate from Heat Leak

**Problem:** A cryogenic LH2 tanker has a total heat leak of $Q = 50$ W from ambient into the liquid. Determine the boil-off mass rate and the percentage of the 4000 kg cargo lost per day.

**Given:**
- Heat leak: $Q = 50$ W = 50 J/s
- Latent heat of vaporization: $L_v = 445.6$ kJ/kg = $445.6 \times 10^3$ J/kg
- Cargo mass: $M_{LH2} = 4000$ kg
- Time period: $t = 1$ day = 86,400 s

**Step 1:** Apply the energy balance — all incoming heat drives vaporization:

$$Q = \dot{m}_{BOG} \times L_v$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the boil-off mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{Q}{L_v}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$\dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{50 \, J/s}{445.6 \times 10^3 \, J/kg}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the quotient:

$$\dot{m}_{BOG} = 1.122 \times 10^{-4} \, \frac{kg}{s}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to kg/day by multiplying by 86,400:

$$\dot{m}_{BOG,daily} = 1.122 \times 10^{-4} \times 86{,}400$$

$$\dot{m}_{BOG,daily} \approx 9.69 \, \frac{kg}{day}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the percentage boil-off rate (BOR) relative to cargo:

$$\text{BOR} = \frac{\dot{m}_{BOG,daily}}{M_{LH2}} \times 100$$

$$\text{BOR} = \frac{9.69}{4000} \times 100$$

$$\text{BOR} \approx 0.24 \, \frac{\%}{day}$$

**Sanity check:** This BOR (~0.24 %/day) is consistent with state-of-the-art cryogenic road tankers (industry range: 0.1–0.3 %/day). Over a 5-day trip, the cumulative loss is ~1.2 %, which is commercially acceptable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Compression Ratio of Liquefaction

**Problem:** Determine how many times denser liquid hydrogen is compared to gaseous hydrogen at STP.

**Given:**
- $\rho_{LH2} = 70.85$ kg/m³ (liquid at 20.27 K, 1 atm)
- $\rho_{H2,STP} = 0.0899$ kg/m³ (ideal gas at 273.15 K, 101.325 kPa)

**Step 1:** Define the compression ratio:

$$R_c = \frac{\rho_{LH2}}{\rho_{H2,STP}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$R_c = \frac{70.85}{0.0899}$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$R_c \approx 788$$

> **Conversion logic note:** This means 1 m³ of LH2 contains the same amount of H2 as 788 m³ of H2 gas at STP. Liquefaction essentially "squeezes" hydrogen into a 788× smaller volume by *cooling*, not by mechanical compression.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Penalty of Liquefaction

**Problem:** A hydrogen liquefier consumes $W_{actual} = 12$ kWh/kg. The minimum reversible work is $W_{ideal} = 3.92$ kWh/kg. Determine the second-law (cryogenic) efficiency.

**Given:**
- Actual work: $W_{actual} = 12$ kWh/kg
- Ideal (reversible) work: $W_{ideal} = 3.92$ kWh/kg

**Step 1:** Recall the definition of second-law efficiency:

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{W_{ideal}}{W_{actual}} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{3.92}{12} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$\eta_{II} \approx 32.7 \, \%$$

**Engineering implication:** Roughly **⅔ of the liquefaction energy is wasted** as irreversibility. State-of-the-art large-scale liquefiers (e.g., Linde StarLNG adaptation) achieve 40–45 % second-law efficiency, but consume **~10 kWh/kg** of electrical energy — equivalent to **~8–10 % of H2's LHV** energy content.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Effective Volumetric Capacity of a Cryogenic Tanker

**Problem:** A road tanker has a usable inner volume of 55 m³. What is the maximum LH2 mass it can carry, and what is the corresponding energy content?

**Given:**
- Tanker usable volume: $V = 55$ m³
- LH2 density: $\rho_{LH2} = 70.85$ kg/m³
- HHV: 142 MJ/kg

**Step 1:** Mass carried:

$$M = \rho_{LH2} \times V$$

$$M = 70.85 \times 55 = 3{,}896.75 \, kg$$

**Step 2:** Energy content (HHV):

$$E = M \times HHV = 3896.75 \times 142$$

$$E = 553{,}338.5 \, MJ$$

**Step 3:** Convert to GWh:

$$E = \frac{553{,}338.5}{3.6 \times 10^6} \approx 0.154 \, GWh$$

> **Conversion logic note:** 1 GWh = $3.6 \times 10^6$ MJ. This single LH2 truck load carries ~**154 MWh** of chemical energy — enough to power ~13,000 Indian households (avg 1 kW) for 12 hours, or refuel ~770 fuel-cell cars at 5 kg H2 each.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LH2 Transport Chain

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of LH2 from production to delivery, including the auxiliary cryogenic subsystems. Note: all node IDs are prefixed with letters (A1, B1, etc.) to comply with Mermaid compilation rules; node labels are kept clean (no markdown bold/italic tags inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["H2 PRODUCTION PLANT"] --> A2["PURIFICATION UNIT 99.999 percent H2"]
    A2 --> A3["LIQUEFIER PLANT Claude or Mixed Refrigerant Cycle"]
    A3 --> A4["TERMINAL CRYOGENIC STORAGE 1000 to 5000 m3"]
    A4 --> A5["PRE-COOL LOADING CHILLDOWN LINE"]
    A5 --> A6["CRYOGENIC ROAD OR RAIL TANKER"]
    A6 --> A7["TRANSIT WITH BOIL-OFF MANAGEMENT"]
    A7 --> A8["BOG RELIQUEFIER OR AUXILIARY ENGINE"]
    A7 --> A9["OFFLOADING AT DESTINATION"]
    A9 --> A10["DESTINATION CRYOTANK AT REFUELING STATION"]
    A10 --> A11["REGASIFICATION VAPORIZER"]
    A11 --> A12["END USER FUEL CELL INDUSTRY OR PIPELINE"]
    A8 -.->|"vented or reliquefied"| A7
    A6 -.->|"insulation MLI vacuum"| A7
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** denote the primary mass flow of hydrogen.
- The **dotted arrows** denote auxiliary thermal subsystems (insulation, BOG management).
- A single tanker round-trip cycle touches **12 functional blocks** — explaining why LH2 logistics is capex-intensive and reserved for high-volume, long-distance routes.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Cryogenic LH2 Tanker Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["INNER CRYOGENIC VESSEL LH2 at 20 K"] --> B2["VACUUM JACKET 10 to the minus 3 Pa"]
    B2 --> B3["MULTI LAYER INSULATION MLI 30 to 60 layers"]
    B3 --> B4["OUTER SHELL Carbon Steel or Aluminum"]
    B4 --> B5["AMBIENT SURROUNDING 298 K"]
    B1 --> B6["PRESSURE CONTROL VALVE 1.0 to 1.5 barg"]
    B1 --> B7["LEVEL GAUGE AND INSTRUMENTATION"]
    B1 --> B8["BOG RECOVERY LINE"]
    B8 --> B9["BOG VENT OR RELIQUEFIER OR AUX ENGINE"]
    B6 --> B9
```

**Key engineering takeaways from the subsystem diagram:**
- The **inner vessel** is typically **aluminum alloy 5083** (lightweight, ductile at cryogenic temperature, low thermal contraction).
- The **outer shell** is **carbon steel** for structural integrity against road vibrations and minor impacts.
- The **BOG recovery line** is the *most important* engineering feature for long-distance transit — without it, a 20-day marine voyage would lose **~5–6 %** of cargo purely to BOG.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Transport-Mode Decision Tree (KTU High-Yield Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1{"DELIVERY DISTANCE"} -->|"less than 50 km"| C2["GASEOUS H2 TUBE TRAILER 200 to 250 bar"]
    C1 -->|"50 to 300 km"| C3["COMPRESSED GASEOUS 350 to 700 bar"]
    C1 -->|"300 to 1500 km"| C4["LIQUEFIED H2 LH2 TANKER"]
    C1 -->|"more than 1500 km"| C5["MARINE LH2 CARRIER OR LOHC OR AMMONIA"]
    C1 -->|"fixed high volume"| C6["DEDICATED H2 PIPELINE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **LH2 transport mode** is preferred for delivery distances between ~300 km and ~1,500 km. Beyond 1,500 km, the cumulative BOG loss and energy penalty of reliquefaction make **LOHC (Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers)** or **NH3 (ammonia)** vectors more economic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OECHT616 Module 3]**

> State the normal boiling point of hydrogen, the density of liquid hydrogen at that temperature, and the volumetric energy density of LH2 on a Higher Heating Value (HHV) basis.

**Model Answer:**

The normal boiling point of hydrogen is **20.27 K (–252.88 °C)** at standard atmospheric pressure of 1 atm (101.325 kPa). At this temperature, the density of liquid hydrogen (LH2) is approximately **70.85 kg/m³**. Multiplying by the HHV of hydrogen (142 MJ/kg) gives the volumetric energy density:

$$E_v = 70.85 \times 142 \approx 10{,}060 \, MJ/m^3 \approx 10.06 \, MJ/L$$

(3 marks: 1 mark for BP, 1 mark for density, 1 mark for energy density calculation.)

---

### Question 2. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, OECHT616 Module 3]**

> Define "Boil-Off Gas (BOG)" in the context of LH2 transportation. What is the typical daily BOG loss range for a well-insulated cryogenic road tanker?

**Model Answer:**

**Boil-Off Gas (BOG)** is the small continuous fraction of liquid hydrogen that vaporizes inside a cryogenic vessel due to unavoidable heat ingress from the warmer surroundings, even with high-performance vacuum + multi-layer insulation (MLI).

For a well-insulated cryogenic LH2 road tanker, the typical daily BOG loss is in the range of **0.1 to 0.3 % per day** of the total cargo mass. For marine LH2 carriers (longer transit), the design target is **≤ 0.05 %/day** to limit cumulative losses over 10–20 day voyages.

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for road-tanker BOR range, 1 mark for marine target.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern, OECHT616]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete LH2 transportation chain from production to end-user, identifying each major functional unit and its purpose. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cryogenic LH2 road tanker carries 4,000 kg of LH2. The total heat leak into the tank is 60 W. Calculate: (i) the boil-off mass rate in kg/s and kg/day, (ii) the percentage daily boil-off rate, and (iii) the cumulative mass loss over a 3-day trip. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The LH2 transportation chain consists of the following six functional units (each carries 1 mark, with the connecting flow arrows carrying 1 mark for coherence):

1. **Hydrogen Production Plant** — Produces H2 via SMR, PEM/Alkaline electrolysis, or biomass gasification. Output is gaseous H2.
2. **Purification Unit** — Removes impurities (especially H2O, CO2, N2, O2, CO) to > 99.999 % purity to prevent solidification in cryogenic lines.
3. **Liquefier Plant** — Cools and condenses H2 to a cryogenic liquid using a multi-stage Claude or mixed-refrigerant cycle. Consumes ~10–13 kWh/kg of electrical energy.
4. **Terminal Cryogenic Storage** — Large insulated double-wall tank (1,000–5,000 m³) holding LH2 at ~20 K, slight positive pressure.
5. **Cryogenic Tanker (Road / Rail / Marine)** — Insulated vessel for transport; uses vacuum + MLI; equipped with BOG management system.
6. **Destination Storage & Regasification** — LH2 offloaded into stationary cryotank, then vaporized for gaseous delivery or pumped as liquid to refueling dispensers.

```
[H2 Plant] → [Purification] → [Liquefier] → [Terminal Tank]
    → [Loading] → [Cryo Tanker] → [Offloading] → [Destination Tank]
    → [Vaporizer] → [End User]
```

(7 marks distributed: 1 mark per block, 1 mark for arrow sequence and labeling of cryogenic temperatures.)

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Cargo mass $M = 4000$ kg
- Heat leak $Q = 60$ W
- Latent heat $L_v = 445.6$ kJ/kg = 445,600 J/kg
- Time $t = 3$ days = 259,200 s

**(i) Boil-off mass rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{Q}{L_v} = \frac{60}{445{,}600} = 1.347 \times 10^{-4} \, \frac{kg}{s}$$

Converting to kg/day (multiply by 86,400):

$$\dot{m}_{BOG,daily} = 1.347 \times 10^{-4} \times 86{,}400 \approx 11.64 \, \frac{kg}{day}$$

**(ii) Percentage daily boil-off rate:**

$$\text{BOR} = \frac{11.64}{4000} \times 100 \approx 0.291 \, \frac{\%}{day}$$

**(iii) Cumulative loss over 3 days:**

$$M_{lost} = \dot{m}_{BOG,daily} \times 3 = 11.64 \times 3 = 34.92 \, kg$$

**Verification by integration:** $M_{lost} = \dot{m}_{BOG} \times t = 1.347 \times 10^{-4} \times 259{,}200 = 34.92$ kg. ✓

(7 marks: 2 marks part (i), 2 marks part (ii), 3 marks part (iii) with all three units and a final boxed answer.)

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern, OECHT616]**

> **(a)** Explain the three main strategies for managing boil-off gas (BOG) in LH2 transport. Compare their relative merits. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hydrogen liquefier requires 11 kWh/kg of electrical energy to liquefy gaseous H2 at ambient conditions. The minimum reversible work of liquefaction is 3.92 kWh/kg. If 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ, calculate: (i) the energy content of 1 kg of liquid H2 on an LHV basis, (ii) the liquefaction energy as a percentage of LHV, and (iii) the second-law efficiency of the liquefier. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The three principal BOG management strategies are:

1. **Vent-Only (Open-Loop) Strategy**:
   - BOG is released to the atmosphere.
   - Simple, no extra equipment.
   - *Disadvantage*: Loses valuable H2 cargo; raises safety and environmental concerns; unsuitable for large/marine tanks.

2. **Reliquefaction (Closed-Loop) Strategy**:
   - BOG is recompressed and recondensed by a small onboard cryocooler.
   - Recovers the full cargo mass; ideal for long voyages.
   - *Disadvantage*: Adds significant capital cost, weight, and parasitic power demand (a small cryocooler can consume 1–3 kW continuously).

3. **Auxiliary Engine Fuel Strategy (Hybrid Use)**:
   - BOG is combusted in a gas engine that drives the truck or ship's pumps and refrigeration systems.
   - Self-sustaining: the BOG powers the very systems that need to keep the tank cold.
   - *Disadvantage*: Some H2 is permanently lost as exhaust; engine efficiency is 25–40 %.

(7 marks: 2 marks per strategy with merit, 1 mark for comparison table/summary.)

**Comparison summary table:**

| Strategy | Complexity | Cargo Recovery | Best Use Case |
|----------|------------|----------------|---------------|
| Vent-only | Low | 0 % (lost) | Short test/legacy operations |
| Reliquefaction | High | ~100 % | Marine LH2 carriers, long-distance |
| Aux engine | Medium | Partial (burned) | Road tankers, hybrid systems |

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Liquefier energy: $W_{actual} = 11$ kWh/kg
- Minimum reversible work: $W_{ideal} = 3.92$ kWh/kg
- LHV of hydrogen: 120 MJ/kg
- Conversion: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ

**(i) Energy content of 1 kg LH2 (LHV):**

$$E_{LHV} = 120 \, \frac{MJ}{kg} \times 1 \, kg = 120 \, MJ$$

**(ii) Liquefaction energy as % of LHV:**

First convert $W_{actual}$ to MJ/kg:

$$W_{actual} = 11 \times 3.6 = 39.6 \, \frac{MJ}{kg}$$

Now compute the percentage:

$$\% W_{LHV} = \frac{39.6}{120} \times 100 = 33 \, \%$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Roughly **one-third of the chemical energy** stored in the LH2 must be reinvested during liquefaction. This is a major energy penalty — and the central reason LH2 transport is only economical when the H2 is produced from cheap electricity (e.g., curtailed renewables).

**(iii) Second-law efficiency:**

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{W_{ideal}}{W_{actual}} \times 100 = \frac{3.92}{11} \times 100 \approx 35.6 \, \%$$

(7 marks: 2 marks each for parts (i) and (ii), 3 marks for part (iii) with the final boxed answer and one-sentence physical interpretation.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in LH2 transport questions (Module 3):**
>
> 1. **Wrong value of latent heat.** Some students write 449 kJ/kg (for H2O) or 510 kJ/kg (for LNG-methane). For hydrogen it is **445.6 kJ/kg** at 20.27 K. Use this value.
>
> 2. **Confusing LHV and HHV.** HHV (142 MJ/kg) is used when *combustion product water is condensed*; LHV (120 MJ/kg) is used when water leaves as vapor. KTU questions specify which. If unclear, default to HHV for tank-capacity calculations, LHV for engine/fuel-cell applications.
>
> 3. **Unit slip-ups.** $W = 12$ kWh/kg must be converted to MJ/kg (× 3.6) before comparing with LHV (MJ/kg). Forgetting this conversion is the **#1 unit error** in 14-mark numericals.
>
> 4. **Neglecting boil-off in long-distance questions.** A 15-day marine voyage at 0.1 %/day BOR = 1.5 % cumulative loss — significant enough to mention in design/economic questions.
>
> 5. **Confusing "compression ratio" with mechanical compression.** LH2 is *not* made by mechanical compression of room-temperature H2 (impossible — you cannot reach 788× by isothermal compression). It is made by *cryogenic cooling* until phase change to liquid occurs. Many students incorrectly describe liquefaction as "high-pressure compression."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Boiling point of H2:** 20.27 K (–252.88 °C) at 1 atm.
- [x] **Liquid density of LH2:** 70.85 kg/m³ (at BP, 1 atm).
- [x] **HHV / LHV of H2:** 142 / 120 MJ/kg.
- [x] **Volumetric energy density of LH2:** ~10.06 MJ/L (HHV), ~8.5 MJ/L (LHV).
- [x] **Compression ratio (gas STP → liquid):** ~788.
- [x] **Latent heat of vaporization of LH2:** 445.6 kJ/kg.
- [x] **Typical BOG rate:** 0.1–0.3 %/day (road), ≤ 0.05 %/day (marine).
- [x] **Liquefaction energy:** ~10–13 kWh/kg (~30–40 % of LHV).
- [x] **Second-law efficiency of liquefier:** typically 30–45 %.
- [x] **Insulation type:** Vacuum + Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI, 30–60 layers).
- [x] **Tanker inner vessel material:** Aluminum alloy 5083 (cryogenic-grade).
- [x] **BOG management strategies:** Vent, Reliquefy, Burn-as-fuel (aux engine).
- [x] **Economic delivery range for LH2 transport:** 300–1,500 km (road/rail); >1,500 km (marine).
- [x] **Real-world example:** *Suiso Frontier* (Japan) — 1,250 m³ LH2 carrier.
- [x] **Key KTU buzzwords to memorize:** cryogenic, BOG, MLI, reliquefaction, second-law efficiency, volumetric energy density, Claude cycle.
- [x] **Equation to memorize first:** $\dot{m}_{BOG} = Q / L_v$ and $E_v = \rho_{LH2} \times HHV$.

> **Final KTU Board Tip:** In 14-mark questions, *always draw a labeled block diagram first*, *then* derive the numerical answer in three clearly numbered steps with units. Examiners reward **clarity of method** as much as the final number. Good luck!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Refuelling Stations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT616 - HYDROGEN ENERGY: PRODUCTION, STORAGE, TRANSPORTATION AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Refuelling Stations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydrogen Refuelling Station (HRS)** is a specialized industrial infrastructure facility designed and engineered to dispense high-purity hydrogen (typically ISO 14687 Grade D quality, with a minimum purity of **99.97%**) into fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) or hydrogen internal combustion engine vehicles, at controlled pressure levels, flow rates, and temperatures, conforming to international safety and performance standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> A Hydrogen Refuelling Station is a *production–storage–compression–dispensing* integrated system that receives hydrogen in gaseous or liquid form, conditions it (compresses/cools), stores it in high-pressure vessels, and dispenses it to vehicles in a controlled manner following protocols such as **SAE J2601** (light-duty vehicles, 35 MPa and 70 MPa) and **SAE J2799** (heavy-duty / fast fill).

### 1.2 Common Nomenclature & Tiers of Refuelling Stations

| Class | Pressure Level | Use Case | Refuelling Time |
|-------|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| **35 MPa (350 bar)** HRS | Low pressure | Buses, light commercial, older FCEVs | 5–10 min |
| **70 MPa (700 bar)** HRS | High pressure | Modern passenger FCEVs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) | 3–5 min |
| **Cryo-compressed (≥ 70 MPa, cryogenic)** HRS | Cryo + HP | Heavy-duty trucks, long range | 8–12 min |
| **Liquid H₂ (LH₂) station** | Near-ambient, cryogenic | Aviation, long-haul, large fleets | 15–30 min |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hydrogen Petrol Pump"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Hydrogen Station is to FCEVs what a Petrol Pump is to Cars, but with far more engineering hidden inside.**
>
> Think of a hydrogen refuelling station as a **"petrol pump on steroids"**. When you fill petrol at a pump, the fuel is simply sucked out of an underground tank and pushed into your car's tank. The pressure differential is small, and the fuel is at ambient temperature.
>
> With hydrogen, you are instead **squeezing an extremely light, leaky, explosive gas into a tiny tank at 700 times atmospheric pressure** — and doing so in **under 5 minutes** — without the tank or fittings getting dangerously hot or embrittled. To do this safely, the station must (a) pressurize the gas through multiple stages of compressors, (b) store it in banks of massive pressure vessels, (c) cool the gas to as low as **−40 °C** before dispensing, and (d) communicate with your car's onboard computer to monitor temperature and pressure in real time.
>
> So while a petrol pump is a simple hose, an HRS is a **miniature chemical plant with its own compressors, control valves, sensors, refrigeration unit, and pressure vessels — all sitting under a canopy that looks just like a regular fuel station**.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Industry Benchmark)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Metrics Every KTU Student Must Know:**
> - **Dispensing Pressure:** 70 MPa (Type IV tanks) / 35 MPa (older buses)
> - **Hydrogen Purity Required:** **99.97%** (ISO 14687 Grade D)
> - **Standard Refuelling Duration:** **3 to 5 minutes** (SAE J2601 fast fill)
> - **End-of-Fill Temperature Limit:** **−40 °C to +55 °C** ambient tolerance, gas pre-cooled to **−40 °C** at dispenser
> - **Energy Cost to Compress 1 kg H₂ from 20 to 70 MPa:** **≈ 3.0–3.5 kWh/kg**
> - **Compressor Throughput Capacity:** Typically **200–600 kg/day** for a public station
> - **Station Footprint:** **~ 400 to 800 m²** (above ground); **~ 200 m²** (urban canopy-only)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Architecture and Functional Components of an HRS

A hydrogen refuelling station is not a single machine but a **system of integrated subsystems**. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists the following core functional blocks:

#### A. Hydrogen Supply / Source Module
This is the **input** side of the HRS. Hydrogen can arrive at the station in three primary ways:

1. **Gaseous Hydrogen Delivery (GH₂)** — by tube trailers at **20–30 MPa (200–300 bar)**. Most common for current stations.
2. **Liquid Hydrogen Delivery (LH₂)** — by cryogenic tankers at **1 atm, −253 °C**. Used for high-throughput stations.
3. **On-Site Production (Electrolyser or SMR)** — produced at the station itself. Common for renewable-powered stations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why on-site electrolysis is becoming popular in Kerala/Karnataka/Tamil Nadu:**
> With the rapid expansion of solar and wind capacity in South India, producing hydrogen on-site using grid-connected or off-grid renewables is a *distributed production* strategy that eliminates costly and risky road transportation of compressed gas.

#### B. Compression Module
- The compression system is the **largest single energy consumer** in the station.
- **Pressure ratio per stage** is limited to roughly **1:4** for positive-displacement compressors to limit discharge temperature.
- Multi-stage compression with **inter-stage cooling** is mandatory.
- Most common compressor types: **Reciprocating piston**, **Ionic liquid piston**, **Metal hydride compressor**, and **Diaphragm compressor**.

#### C. High-Pressure Storage (Cascade)
- The compressed gas is stored in **banks of carbon-fiber-wound, polymer-lined Type IV pressure vessels**.
- The cascade is divided into **3 pressure levels (low, medium, high)** to enable fast filling while keeping the vehicle's tank within safe temperature limits.

#### D. Pre-cooling / Chiller Module
- A **cryogenic heat exchanger** cools the dispensed H₂ down to **−40 °C** (for 70 MPa SAE J2601 fast fill).
- Without pre-cooling, the gas would heat up adiabatically inside the vehicle's tank to **+85 °C or higher**, which is unsafe and exceeds tank design limits.
- Common refrigerants: **R-744 (CO₂)**, R-23, R-14, or **Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂)**.

#### E. Dispensing Module
- A **breakaway hose**, **hydrogen-specific nozzle** (coded by pressure — different for 35 MPa vs. 70 MPa), and a **flow meter** (Coriolis type, accuracy ±1%).
- Includes a **fume detector**, **flame detector**, **leak detector**, and **emergency shutdown (ESD) system**.

#### F. Control & Communication Module
- A **PLC / RTU-based SCADA** system implementing the **SAE J2601 protocol** which negotiates the filling process with the vehicle's onboard **Hydrogen Refuelling Controller (HRC)**.
- Monitors pressure, temperature, mass dispensed, and computes **Pressure Equalization (PE)**, **Average Pressure (AP)**, and **End-of-Fill Termination**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Form | Variables & Units |
|---|------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Ideal Gas Law** | $PV = nRT$ | $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³, $n$ in mol, $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K |
| 2 | **Compressor Work (Isothermal, ideal)** | $W_{iso} = nRT \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $W$ in J/mol |
| 3 | **Compressor Work (Polytropic)** | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{nRT_1 \,\eta}{\eta - 1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\eta-1}{\eta}} - 1\right]$ | $\eta$ = polytropic index (≈ 1.35 for H₂) |
| 4 | **Energy to compress 1 kg H₂ from $P_1$ to $P_2$** | $E = \dfrac{R_s T}{\eta - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\eta-1}{\eta}} - 1\right]$ | $R_s$ = 4124 J/kg·K (specific gas constant for H₂) |
| 5 | **Density of H₂ at STP** | $\rho = 0.0899 \text{ kg/m³}$ | — |
| 6 | **Mass of H₂ in a tank** | $m = \dfrac{P \cdot V \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ | $M$ = 2.016 g/mol (molar mass of H₂) |
| 7 | **Adiabatic Temperature Rise on Filling** | $T_2 = T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ | $\gamma$ = 1.405 for H₂ |
| 8 | **Hydrogen Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** | **4.0% by volume in air** | — |
| 9 | **Hydrogen Detonation Limits** | **18.3% to 59%** by volume in air | — |
| 10 | **Energy Content of H₂ (LHV)** | **120 MJ/kg** = **33.33 kWh/kg** | — |
| 11 | **Energy Content of H₂ (HHV)** | **142 MJ/kg** = **39.4 kWh/kg** | — |
| 12 | **Joule-Thomson Coefficient for H₂** | $\mu_{JT} \approx -0.362 \text{ K/bar}$ at 300 K (cooling on expansion) | — |
| 13 | **Storage Cascade Pressure Levels** | Low: 25–35 MPa, Med: 50 MPa, High: 87.5 MPa | (for 70 MPa service) |
| 14 | **Station Capacity (typical)** | **200 – 600 kg/day** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pitfall (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> When computing compressor work, students often forget that the **isothermal** equation assumes perfect cooling, but the **polytropic** equation is the realistic engineering case. Marks are awarded for *stating the assumption clearly* before substituting values.

### 2.3 The SAE J2601 Refuelling Protocol — Operational Logic

The **SAE J2601** standard governs how an HRS communicates with a vehicle to perform a fast fill. The logic, in layman's terms, is:

- **Step 1 — Vehicle Arrival:** Driver connects the nozzle. The HRS queries the vehicle's onboard **Hydrogen Refuelling Controller (HRC)** via a hardwired communication line.
- **Step 2 — Pressure & Temperature Reporting:** The vehicle transmits its current pressure ($P_0$), temperature ($T_0$), and the **Limit Pressure (PL)** — the maximum pressure the tank is rated for (typically 87.5 MPa nominal for a 70 MPa Type IV tank).
- **Step 3 — Lookup Table Selection:** Based on $P_0$, $T_0$, and ambient temperature, the HRS looks up a pre-validated filling table from SAE J2601. The standard defines multiple tables for combinations of 35 MPa / 70 MPa and ambient temperatures from **−40 °C to +50 °C**.
- **Step 4 — Ramp Termination Pressure Calculation:** The system computes a **target end-of-fill pressure ($P_{end}$)** such that the gas temperature inside the vehicle's tank remains **below 85 °C** at end-of-fill.
- **Step 5 — Pre-cooling & Dispensing:** The HRS pre-cools the gas to **−40 °C**, then begins dispensing while monitoring **mass flow rate, vehicle pressure, and gas temperature in real time**.
- **Step 6 — Termination:** When either (a) the calculated $P_{end}$ is reached, (b) the mass meter reads the target mass, or (c) the temperature exceeds the limit, the dispenser **shuts off automatically** within milliseconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why −40 °C?** Hydrogen has a **negative Joule-Thomson coefficient** at near-ambient temperatures (≈ −0.36 K/bar). When it expands rapidly into a vehicle tank, it tends to *cool* initially — but the heat of compression from the dispenser line and the heat of the gas itself dominate. Pre-cooling the gas to **−40 °C** ensures that after Joule-Thomson expansion, the in-tank gas temperature is still safely below 85 °C.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Logistics:** A modern 70 MPa HRS can refill a Toyota Mirai with **5.6 kg of hydrogen** in **3–5 minutes**, giving **650 km of driving range**. This is critical to make FCEVs consumer-acceptable.
- **Grid Load:** A 600 kg/day station consumes **2.0–2.5 MW** of electricity for compression, refrigeration, and dispensing. Stations are typically paired with on-site renewables to decarbonize the *entire* hydrogen pathway.
- **Urban Planning:** The smaller the station's *footprint*, the higher the *real estate cost*. KTU-relevant projects in Kerala examine how stack-integrated stations (with on-site electrolysers) can fit inside **200 m² of canopy area**, comparable to a standard diesel station.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Compressor Work Required to Compress 1 kg of Hydrogen from 20 MPa to 87.5 MPa

This is a **classic KTU numerical problem** on hydrogen refuelling stations. We solve it using the **polytropic compression equation**, since the isothermal case is unrealistic (it requires infinite cooling), and adiabatic compression would overheat the gas.

**Given:**
- Mass of hydrogen: $m = 1$ kg
- Initial pressure: $P_1 = 20$ MPa = $20 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Final pressure: $P_2 = 87.5$ MPa = $87.5 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Initial temperature: $T_1 = 298.15$ K (25 °C ambient)
- Polytropic index: $\eta = 1.35$ (typical for multi-stage H₂ compression)
- Specific gas constant for H₂: $R_s = 4124$ J/(kg·K)
- Number of moles: $n = m / M = 1 / 0.002016$ = 496.03 mol

**Step 1: Recall the Polytropic Work Formula**

For 1 kg of an ideal gas undergoing polytropic compression:

$$W = \frac{R_s \, T_1}{\eta - 1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\eta-1}{\eta}} - 1\right]$$

**Step 2: Compute the Pressure Ratio**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{87.5}{20} = 4.375$$

**Step 3: Compute the Polytropic Exponent Term**

$$\frac{\eta - 1}{\eta} = \frac{1.35 - 1}{1.35} = \frac{0.35}{1.35} = 0.2593$$

**Step 4: Raise the Pressure Ratio to the Polytropic Exponent**

$$\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.2593} = 4.375^{0.2593}$$

We solve this logarithmically:

$$\ln(4.375) = 1.4759$$

$$0.2593 \times 1.4759 = 0.3827$$

$$e^{0.3827} = 1.4663$$

So: $\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{0.2593} \approx 1.4663$

**Step 5: Substitute into the Work Formula**

$$W = \frac{4124 \times 298.15}{1.35 - 1}\left[1.4663 - 1\right]$$

Compute numerator:

$$4124 \times 298.15 = 1{,}229{,}732.6 \text{ J/kg}$$

Compute denominator:

$$1.35 - 1 = 0.35$$

Compute bracket:

$$1.4663 - 1 = 0.4663$$

Therefore:

$$W = \frac{1{,}229{,}732.6 \times 0.4663}{0.35}$$

$$W = \frac{573{,}329.4}{0.35}$$

$$W \approx 1{,}638{,}084 \text{ J/kg} \approx 1.638 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 6: Convert to kWh**

Since 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J:

$$W = \frac{1{,}638{,}084}{3.6 \times 10^6} \approx 0.4551 \text{ kWh/kg}$$

**Step 7: Add Real-World Compressor Inefficiency**

A real compressor has an **isentropic efficiency** of typically 70–80%. Accounting for an efficiency $\eta_c = 0.75$:

$$W_{real} = \frac{W_{ideal}}{\eta_c} = \frac{0.4551}{0.75} \approx 0.607 \text{ kWh/kg}$$

**Step 8: Account for Inter-stage Cooling and Pressure Losses**

A practical engineering factor of **1.15 to 1.20** is applied for inter-stage pressure drops and auxiliary parasitic loads:

$$W_{practical} = 0.607 \times 1.15 \approx 0.698 \text{ kWh/kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The actual electrical energy required at the motor shaft of the compressor to compress 1 kg of hydrogen from 20 MPa to 87.5 MPa in a real, multi-stage H₂ compressor is approximately **0.7 kWh/kg**.
>
> Industry ranges from 3.0 to 3.5 kWh/kg *include* the **whole station** (compression + refrigeration for −40 °C pre-cooling + dispenser control + losses), not just the compressor.

**Step 9: Derive the Adiabatic (Worst-Case) Temperature Rise**

If the gas were compressed in a *single* adiabatic stage, the discharge temperature would be:

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

For H₂, $\gamma = 1.405$:

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.405}{1.405} = 0.2883$$

$$T_2 = 298.15 \times (4.375)^{0.2883} = 298.15 \times 1.5572 \approx 464.2 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 \approx 464.2 - 273.15 = 191.0 \text{ °C}$$

This extreme temperature justifies why **multi-stage compression with inter-stage cooling** is mandatory — single-stage compression would push the gas to **191 °C**, beyond safe lubricant and seal limits.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Refuelling Station Architectures

| Parameter | Gaseous H₂ Delivery (GH₂) | Liquid H₂ Delivery (LH₂) | On-Site Electrolysis |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Hydrogen arrives as** | Compressed gas at 20–30 MPa | Cryogenic liquid at −253 °C | Produced on demand |
| **Footprint** | Large (cascade storage) | Medium (cryo-tank + vaporizer) | Small (electrolyser only) |
| **Boil-off losses** | None | 0.1–0.3% per day | None |
| **CAPEX** | Medium (₹ 4–6 Cr for 200 kg/day) | High (₹ 6–9 Cr) | Very high (₹ 8–15 Cr) |
| **OPEX** | Medium (delivery cost dominant) | Medium (re-liquefaction losses) | Low (electricity dominant) |
| **Suitable for** | Urban / highway stations | High-throughput stations | Remote / renewable-rich sites |
| **Carbon Footprint** | Depends on upstream source | Depends on upstream source | Zero (if renewable powered) |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | **Very High** | Medium | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a 70 MPa Hydrogen Refuelling Station

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[H2 SUPPLY INLET\nGH2 Trailer 20 MPa\nor LH2 Cryotanker\nor Onsite Electrolyser] --> B[COMPRESSOR UNIT\nMulti-stage Reciprocating\nwith Inter-stage Cooling]
    B --> C[HIGH PRESSURE STORAGE CASCADE\nType IV Composite Cylinders\nLow Bank 35 MPa\nMedium Bank 55 MPa\nHigh Bank 87.5 MPa]
    C --> D[PRE-COOLING CHILLER\nR-744 Refrigeration\nOutlet Temperature minus 40 C]
```
